item.title: Will ICE Create a Human Rights Nightmare at the FIFA World Cup?
item.description: The world’s leading human rights groups are generally associated with tireless crusades against state-sanctioned violence and political persecution. But some of them are sounding the alarm about an entirely different danger: the risk that attendees at the FIFA World Cup this summer could face…
item.author: Zeteo
item.content:encoded:
The world’s leading human rights groups are generally associated with tireless crusades against state-sanctioned violence and political persecution. But some of them are sounding the alarm about an entirely different danger: the risk that attendees at the FIFA World Cup this summer could face detention or expulsion by U.S. authorities.

Ever since the Trump administration announced that the Immigration and Customs Enforcement agency (ICE) would play a “key” role in the tournament’s security operations at matches in the U.S., human rights organizations have been expressing safety concerns – and not just for attending fans. Several groups told me that they’re worried for everyone at the games, from the vendors outside the stadiums to local business owners and patrons in host cities, and even for the players.

“The risk of the World Cup being an enforcement action – of being so attractive to ICE that they scoop up people at this event – is very high,” Minky Worden, director of global initiatives for Human Rights Watch (HRW), told me. “ICE at the World Cup is not a security plan. It’s a threat. And it shouldn’t make people feel secure. It’s very much the opposite.”

By all projections, the FIFA World Cup 26, which gets underway on June 11, will be the most-watched sporting event ever. The quadrennial championship of the world’s most popular sport is expected to draw a global television viewership of some 6 billion people around the world. The first World Cup where human rights criteria were considered during the host selection process, as many as 5 million fans are expected to converge on 16 cities in the US, Mexico, and Canada to attend the global sports event.

But it is largely the US-hosted games that have human rights advocates worried.

ICE and other federal police have been carrying out brutal crackdowns throughout U.S. cities that have seen the detentions and expulsions of tens of thousands of people – even very young children. These operations have even led to the deaths of several people at the hands of federal agents.

According to deportation data reviewed by HRW, ICE arrested more than 90,000 people in and around the 11 US-hosted Club World Cup venues between January and October of last year. Rights groups now fear that soccer fans could find themselves detained in the kinds of immigration sweeps that have been carried out in cities across the US. That’s what compelled human rights advocates in Miami, where several World Cup games are scheduled, to issue a travel alert.

“We wanted to warn visitors and tourists of the risks of coming to Florida for the World Cup. We’ve seen videos of ICE murdering U.S.citizens, heard stories of tourists with valid visas who still end up in detention,” said Yareliz Mendez-Zamora, a policy coordinator for American Friends Service Committee.

“We’ve seen how ICE and local law enforcement will racially profile people. For fans who don’t speak English or speak English with an accent, this makes them more vulnerable to being detained after a routine stop,” she told me.

ICE’s outgoing acting director, Todd Lyons, told a congressional hearing earlier this year that the agency would be “a key part of the overall security apparatus for the World Cup.”

Pressed by CBS News in an interview that aired this month, Department of Homeland Security (DHS) Secretary Markwayne Mullin said that while immigration enforcement is under the purview of ICE, “We’re not there to go round up mass individuals.”

This week, DHS posted a video of Mullin saying immigration authorities would be “out there every day” of the World Cup “fighting against the counterfeit tickets, human trafficking, drug smuggling, counterfeit products.” He did not, however, say whether the same authorities would also be targeting undocumented immigrants.

When I asked the Department of Homeland Security, a spokesperson said in an emailed statement: “Our mission is simple: ensure every fan – Americans and visitors alike – has a safe and unforgettable experience. What makes someone a target for immigration enforcement is whether or not they are illegally in the U.S.— full stop. Speculation to the contrary is ill-informed.”

After Lyon’s comments earlier this year, Democratic Representative Nellie Pou, a ranking member on the House Homeland Security Task Force dedicated to overseeing security for the games, wrote on X that, “If visitors and players are afraid to attend, the effects will be devastating. We cannot celebrate unity while DHS agents are conducting violent raids, detaining people outside the stadium and shoving them into unmarked vans.”

“This is not security. This is chaos,” said Pou, whose home state of New Jersey will host the championship finals on July 19.

The scale and the scope of potential U.S. federal police operations at the World Cup are a big part of what rights organizations find so troubling.

“During the World Cup, there is real risk that ICE’S involvement could extend to airports, stadiums, and surrounding neighborhoods, creating fear and threatening the rights to peaceful assembly, non-discrimination, and due process,” Amy Fischer, Director of Refugee and Migrant Rights with Amnesty International USA, said in an email.

“Particularly concerning is the administration’s abusive and unchecked immigration enforcement tactics and harm they are causing. ICE is inciting fear in our communities, tearing families apart, arresting people without warrants, and inflicting death, cruelty, and suffering in our communities and on those in its custody. This is not public safety; it is state-sponsored violence.”

The Human Rights Campaign (HRC) said a stepped-up federal police presence at the World Cup poses a particular risk to LGBTQ attendees.

“The World Cup is meant to be a moment of celebration, an opportunity to showcase some of this country’s most beautiful communities on a global stage. Unfortunately, the yearslong onslaught of anti-LGBTQ+ rhetoric and policies across the country and the Trump administration’s unleashing of masked terror on our streets rightly has people worried,” said HRC National Press Secretary Brandon Wolf.

Even for those not facing restrictions from entering the country, for soccer fans of color or those who speak with an accent, HRW’s Worden said “the risk that if you don’t bring your passport, you could end up in immigration detention, is extremely high.”

Following pressure from human rights groups, the EU, and some individual member federations, FIFA senior management reportedly discussed with Gianni Infantino, the organization’s president and Trump friend, about making a direct appeal to the U.S. leader for a moratorium on ICE raids during the World Cup. But those discussions have apparently gone nowhere.

HRW has also called on FIFA to persuade the administration to implement an “ICE Truce,” halting immigration enforcement operations for the duration of the World Cup.

FIFA, however, has yet to respond, and the Trump administration has shown no signs of acquiescing.

“If there are risks that you will be separated from your children and never see them again at a FIFA World Cup event, FIFA has a responsibility under its own human rights policy to stop that,” Worden said. “It’s not acceptable that you’re put in jeopardy and at risk of detention, violent abuse, beatings because you’re going to a supposedly fun World Cup event.”

Worden said among those who could be targeted by federal authorities in the U.S. are protesters, or even people who have merely expressed opposition in the past to the policies of the current administration.

Given the risk to fans attending the games, Worden said that at the very least, “FIFA needs to put in place a system of pro bono legal support so that if you’re arrested and separated from your children, you can file a habeas [corpus] complaint on the spot. There needs to be a massive architecture of human rights reporting so that these abuses can be accurately reported and dealt with.”

Requests for comment from FIFA regarding human rights monitoring at the tournament went unanswered.

Worden called on fans to put pressure on football authorities in their respective countries to make sure that their rights are respected. They have to speak out about their right to attend football matches without the risk of being separated from their children and family members.”

Meanwhile, street vendors in Los Angeles, a host city, are bracing for potential enforcement sweeps at the games, while workers at the city’s host stadium are preparing to strike.

With as many eyes on what’s happening outside the stadiums as within them, the Trump administration is set to deliver a spectacle unlike any seen before.

Frances Nguyen is an American freelance journalist who covers social movements and how identity intersects with politics and culture.

item.title: 'Muslims Are Treated as Second-Class Citizens': Mehdi Tells Piers Morgan about Rising Islamophobia in the Wake of …
item.description: Zeteo’s editor-in-chief is back on ‘Piers Morgan Uncensored’ — this time, to talk about Islamophobia in the wake of the horrific shooting at San Diego’s largest Islamic center. “Muslims are treated as second-class citizens in this country,” Mehdi tells Piers, arguing that the rise in political and…
item.author: Zeteo
item.content:encoded:
Zeteo’s editor-in-chief is back on ‘Piers Morgan Uncensored’ — this time, to talk about Islamophobia in the wake of the horrific shooting at San Diego’s largest Islamic center.

“Muslims are treated as second-class citizens in this country,” Mehdi tells Piers, arguing that the rise in political and extremist violence has gotten worse since Donald Trump was first elected to office.

The San Diego incident is being investigated as a hate crime after a 75-page manifesto, allegedly written by the shooters, surfaced online, containing Islamophobic, antisemitic, and homophobic statements, among other extremist views.

While Muslims and Jews are often pitted against each other, Mehdi notes the anti-Jewish bigotry in the shooters’ alleged manifesto and tells Morgan that Islamophobia and antisemitism are “two sides of the same coin.”

He explains, “Minority communities need to stick together right now because the far right hate all of us. The people who claim to be white Christian nationalists who want this country to be a white, Christian nation, they don’t want any of us here — Jews or Muslims.”

Check out their full exchange, above, to also hear Mehdi discuss how the right has created a “moral panic” around Islam and immigration, and debunk right-wing arguments around political violence, as well as ‘rape gangs’ and ‘sharia courts’ in the UK.

Plus, Mehdi returned to Busboys & Poets on Friday evening as part of an expert panel, “How to Prevent Another Forever War in the Middle East.”

He joined Joe Kent, Trita Parsi, Narges Barjoghli, Olivia Troye, Wajahat Ali, and others to break down Trump’s illegal war in Iran and where the conflict stands as the US and Iran struggle to reach a peace deal.

Watch the full conversation above – Mehdi comes on at around 2 hours and 11 minutes in.

item.title: Israel Kills Gaza Community Kitchen Workers and Tortures Global Sumud Flotilla Activists
item.description: This week, Israel’s ultranationalist National Security Minister Itamar Ben-Gvir posted a video that drew the kind of condemnation from Western governments that Palestinians and their supporters have been demanding for years. In the video, Ben-Gvir is seen smiling as he taunts the activists,…
item.author: This Week in Palestine | Zeteo
item.content:encoded:
This week, Israel’s ultranationalist National Security Minister Itamar Ben-Gvir posted a video that drew the kind of condemnation from Western governments that Palestinians and their supporters have been demanding for years.
In the video, Ben-Gvir is seen smiling as he taunts the activists, humanitarians, and journalists whom Israel abducted earlier in the week from the Global Sumud Flotilla. In the background, those detained, including Zeteo contributor Alex Colston, are kneeling face down, their hands handcuffed behind their back.

Italy, the UK, Canada, and several other countries rightly protested and condemned the far-right minister and his tactics. But the questions remain: If this is what an Israeli minister does to foreigners, imagine what’s being done to the thousands of Palestinians held in Israeli prisons, and where is the same level of condemnation for the abuses Palestinians have repeatedly told the world they’ve been forced to endure for decades?

Here’s a round-up of just some of those abuses this week, and more on what happened to the flotilla:

Four Israeli settlers reportedly attacked 50-year-old Jaber Shabaneh as he collected herbs in Wadi Abuwein in the occupied West Bank.

Shabaneh told local media that one of the settlers attempted to stab him in the stomach, but cut his leg instead after Shabaneh pushed him away. He was later able to escape.

Israeli strikes targeted a community kitchen in Gaza’s Deir el-Balah, killing three of its workers, according to Al Jazeera.

At least five others were killed in Israeli strikes across the Strip, showing once again that the ceasefire is no ceasefire at all.

Israel began illegally intercepting dozens of boats that were part of the Global Sumud Flotilla, trying to break Israel’s illegal siege of the enclave.

While held on Israeli prison boats and later at an Israeli prison facility, Zeteo contributor Alex Colston, who was on the flotilla, says Israeli forces beat, tortured, and inflicted other kinds of abuse, including sexual violence.

Flotilla organizers reported that at least 12 sexual assaults, including rape, were documented on an Israeli vessel that activists described as a “torture boat.”

Hear more from Alex:

Israel’s ultranationalist Finance Minister Bezalel Smotrich ordered the eviction of the Bedouin village of Khan Al-Ahmar in the occupied West Bank after he said he was notified that the International Criminal Court was seeking a warrant for his arrest.

It’s not clear whether such a warrant exists or whether he was responding to an official notification or to reports that the court was considering one.

“From today, any economic or other target that I have the power to harm within the framework of my powers as Minister of Finance and as a minister in the Defense Ministry will be attacked,” he said. (A settler himself, Smotrich has long been using these “powers” to go after Palestinians and their land.)

Several countries, including Canada, Italy, France, and the Netherlands, summoned the Israeli ambassadors or chargés d’affaires in their respective countries to protest Ben-Gvir’s video and the treatment of flotilla activists

The UN, European Council, and others condemned Ben-Gvir’s behavior.

The video also generated backlash inside Israel, though it’s hard to take such condemnation seriously by a government that continues its genocide in Gaza and routinely violates international law across Palestine. Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu, who is wanted by the ICC for alleged war crimes, proposterisly said his own minister’s actions are “not in line with Israel’s values and norms,” while defending the illegal interception of the flotilla.

A day later, Israel announced it was releasing the activists.

Riyad Mansour, the Palestinian ambassador to the UN, dropped his bid for the vice presidency of the UN General Assembly after the US threatened to potentially revoke his delegation’s visas if he did not withdraw his application, NPR reported.

The leaders of the UK, France, Germany, Italy, Canada, Australia, New Zealand, Norway, and the Netherlands demanded that Israel halt its expansion of settlements in the occupied West Bank and take more action against escalating Israeli settler violence.

“We strongly oppose those, including members of the Israeli government, who argue for annexation and forcible displacement of the Palestinian population,” the leaders said in a joint statement. The statement also urged construction companies not to bid on settlement projects.

The leaders, however, did not say there would be consequences for Israel if it didn’t take action on their demands.

Miss last week’s edition? Catch up here. Plus, check out more from Zeteo:

If you enjoy Zeteo, share it with your friends and earn rewards when they subscribe.

item.title: Yes, Trump Was ‘Very Involved’ in His $1.8 Billion Slush Fund Deal
item.description: NOTE: If you subscribe to Zeteo’s U.S. Politics’ morning newsletter, After news broke this week that Donald Trump’s administration had agreed to create a $1.776 billion slush fund for his allies, in order to settle the president’s completely bogus lawsuit, Trump got busy telling a blatant lie. “I…
item.author: "Zeteo | U.S. Politics"
item.content:encoded:
NOTE: If you subscribe to Zeteo’s U.S. Politics’ morning newsletter,

After news broke this week that Donald Trump’s administration had agreed to create a $1.776 billion slush fund for his allies, in order to settle the president’s completely bogus lawsuit, Trump got busy telling a blatant lie.

“I wasn’t involved in the settlement,” Trump told reporters on Wednesday. “I could have been involved, but I didn’t choose to be.”

Of course, just months ago, the president said on camera: “I have to work out some kind of a settlement. I’m supposed to work out a settlement with myself.” Further, three sources with knowledge of the matter tell Zeteo that Trump and his advisers signed off on the fund, received updates in recent months as so-called “negotiations” continued, and that the president could have stopped the deal at any time.

“He was very involved,” one of the sources tells Zeteo. “He was kept in the loop, White House officials were kept updated on progress. Donald Trump isn’t doing the busy work himself, but he was involved in it as much as Tim Cook is involved with Apple…or Elon [Musk] with SpaceX.”

Trump’s second administration has been completely lawless and corrupt from the start, with the administration spending absurd amounts of taxpayer dollars to stroke the president’s ego and make him feel good about himself.

Even still, the president’s settlement with himself – and his corresponding plan to put $1.776 billion in U.S. taxpayer money into an “Anti-Weaponization Fund” for his friends and allies – is shocking: The deal more closely represents a literal bank heist than it does any legitimate government action.

The Justice Department announced the deal in exchange for Trump dropping his lawsuit against his own government, over an IRS leak of his tax returns that occurred during his first presidency.

The judge overseeing Trump’s case against the IRS had expressed concern that the parties – Trump and his administration – might not be “sufficiently adverse” for there to be any actual case or controversy, since Trump “is the sitting president and his named adversaries are entities whose decisions are subject to his direction.” Before she could rule on that question, the Trump DOJ announced a settlement, ending the matter.

In January, Trump claimed he wanted any settlement to benefit “established and respected” charities. “I think what we’ll do is do something for charity,” he said. “We could make it a substantial amount, nobody would care because it’s gonna go to numerous very good charities.” He suggested money could go to the American Cancer Society.

Now that the settlement has been inked, the fund could instead be used to reward Trump allies and even Jan. 6 rioters who claim they were mistreated by Democrats.

And while Trump’s administration has claimed the president won’t personally benefit from the settlement, the deal came with a special late addendum, signed by Acting Attorney General Todd Blanche, to wipe out any audits and investigations Trump and his business empire could be facing in relation to his tax returns. That’s convenient, since the Trump Organization reportedly could have owed $100 million, depending on the outcome of a long-running dispute with the IRS, which believed Trump had tried to write off the same losses twice.

“Per usual the liberal hacks at Zeteo have no idea what they’re talking about, nor do their so-called sources,” an unnamed White House spokesperson said in response to our emailed request for comment.

Almost immediately, the Trump administration’s IRS settlement and slush fund drew a furious response, and not just from elected Democrats and an outraged public. A number of elected Republicans – who want to keep their jobs and maintain their party’s majority on Capitol Hill in this year’s midterm elections – complained about how, as one GOP senator put it to Zeteo, “fucking ridiculous and unhelpful” the settlement deal is, as the party seeks to hold on to voters already livid at Trump.

Two Trump administration officials, for their part, claimed to Zeteo that they and several of their senior colleagues were surprised by the intensity and level of sustained backlash they’ve received throughout the week.

Early in the week, Trump tried to claim his IRS settlement had been “very well received,” as he asserted: “I know very little about it. I wasn’t involved in the whole creation of it and the negotiation.”

“I wasn’t involved in the settlement,” he claimed two days later, even though his personal lawyer’s signature is on the document withdrawing his lawsuit.

It was a lie on Wednesday, just as it was on Monday. By Friday, the president could barely keep up the pretense.

“I gave up a lot of money in allowing the just announced Anti-Weaponization Fund to go forward,” Trump posted to social media. “I could have settled my case, including the illegal release of my Tax Returns and the equally illegal BREAK IN of Mar-a-Lago, for an absolute fortune. Instead, I am helping others, who were so badly abused by an evil, corrupt, and weaponized Biden Administration, receive, at long last, JUSTICE!”

item.title: Inside Israel's Abusive Treatment of Gaza Flotilla Activists: Rape, Tasers, and Beatings
item.description: “Shooting fish in a barrel.” Tasers. Rape . This is what Israel allegedly just did to many of the hundreds of activists, humanitarians, and journalists it abducted after illegally intercepting the Global Sumud Flotilla that was seeking to break Israel’s illegal siege of Gaza and bring aid to the…
item.author: Zeteo
item.content:encoded:
“Shooting fish in a barrel.” Tasers. Rape. This is what Israel allegedly just did to many of the hundreds of activists, humanitarians, and journalists it abducted after illegally intercepting the Global Sumud Flotilla that was seeking to break Israel’s illegal siege of Gaza and bring aid to the Palestinian people.

Zeteo contributor Alex Colston was among those detained by Israeli forces. He gives Prem his first-hand account of what he saw and endured inside Israeli imprisonment. And the details are shocking.

“You could hear people screaming” at every checkpoint in the prison facilities, Alex says. He was in the room when Israel’s far-right National Security Minister Itamar Ben-Gvir gleefully harassed detainees – a moment that went viral and sparked international condemnation. Alex says viral videos may not have captured other abuses, including that soldiers blared the national anthem over and over as they escorted (and tortured) the detainees through different checkpoints.

Colston himself lost feeling in his hands due to Israeli soldiers cuffing them tightly and then using the handcuffs to torture him, again and again. At one point, he said he “just blacked out” because of the extreme pain. When he came to, a guard asked if he was from the US. “I said yes, and he was like, ‘Well, this is my country’. And then he hit me in the ribs a couple times.’”

Watch the video above to hear much more of what Alex saw inside Israel’s abduction and detention of the Gaza aid flotilla volunteers.

Donate

We are removing the paywall from this conversation – but on-the-ground reporting like Alex’s is only possible because of your support. Alex’s work helps us see the violence Israel does in the shadows, and the volunteers who have persisted in the face of it. Please subscribe to help us do more of this work, that mainstream media refuses to. And if you’re already a paid subscriber, you can always support our work further with a donation.

NOTE: Alex participated in this interview in a public setting to have access to Wi-Fi. We apologize for the increased background noise you hear throughout the conversation.

Share


Read Alex's previous reporting from the latest flotilla:

item.title: BREAKING: Tulsi Gabbard, Trump Warmonger and Aspiring Election-Rigger, Resigns
item.description: Tulsi Gabbard, President Donald Trump’s director of national intelligence, plans to leave her cabinet-level position by the end of June, according to her resignation letter. Gabbard, once a Democratic congresswoman, staked her reputation on being vigorously opposed to America’s “regime change…
item.author: "Zeteo | U.S. Politics"
item.content:encoded:
Tulsi Gabbard, President Donald Trump’s director of national intelligence, plans to leave her cabinet-level position by the end of June, according to her resignation letter.

Gabbard, once a Democratic congresswoman, staked her reputation on being vigorously opposed to America’s “regime change wars.” She endorsed Joe Biden in 2020. Then, she spent nearly a year and a half propping up a Trump administration dedicated to authoritarian violence and power grabs, a world-historically corrupt personality cult, and – who could forget? – launching regime-change wars.

In her formal resignation letter, Gabbard cited her husband’s struggle with “an extremely rare form of bone cancer” as her reason for quitting, and wrote to Trump about how “deeply grateful” she is for “the trust you placed in me and for the opportunity to lead the Office of the Director of National Intelligence for the last year and a half.” She is the fourth cabinet-level official to resign or be pushed out of Trump’s second administration; all four have been women.

By her own words, Gabbard is not resigning out of principle or in protest. She is resigning because of an intensely personal, serious family matter. Taking her at her word, Gabbard is not quitting because she is incensed by what the Trump-Vance administration is doing on Iran, Venezuela, Cuba, and elsewhere across the globe.

As President Trump bombed Iran, as he invaded Venezuela and kidnapped its leader, as he started his illegal war of aggression on the Iranian people, as he’s ramping up preparations for a possible invasion of Cuba, as he refuses to take invading Mexico off the table, as he sought to conquer Greenland, as he waged deadly warfare on his own people, as he bombed, and bombed, and bombed – Tulsi Gabbard stayed put. She did not leave in protest, including when it could have – maybe? – mattered or meant something. She did not meaningfully fight any of the war policies she once claimed to abhor.

She was proud to serve as a willing cog in Trump’s vast war machine, even as she was reportedly regularly excluded from sensitive, high-level meetings – like those over kidnapping Venezuela’s leader – to the point that White House aides would joke that her title, DNI, stood for “Do Not Invite.”

But for Trump, enough is never enough.

The president, according to sources in and out of government who’ve talked to Trump about this over the past several months, had indeed flirted with the idea of getting rid of Gabbard altogether. As Zeteo reported in March, Trump has made snide, bitter comments behind the scenes about Gabbard not being enthusiastic enough about his disastrous war in Iran. And on Friday afternoon, Reuters reported, citing one “source familiar with the matter,” that ​Gabbard had been “forced out by the White House,” as the Trump administration continued to insist her departure was due to her husband’s health.

But one professional saving grace for Gabbard was that Trump – an administration official and another source briefed on the matter tell Zeteo – had gushed to confidants how thrilled he was by some of the work Gabbard had been doing on what he dubs “election integrity.”

There is, however, no reason that the U.S. director of national intelligence, one of the most influential positions for the most sensitive of national security-related matters and vital intelligence analysis, should be anywhere near Donald Trump’s thoroughly debunked conspiracy theories about the 2020 election – an election he lost and then tried to steal, leading to death and destruction.

But that’s what Gabbard’s been doing in recent months, serving as the face and one of the ringleaders of Trump’s intelligence community “investigation” into U.S. voting systems. Her job, officially, is to keep the American people safe. Instead, she used her soon-to-be former office to aid Trump’s efforts to rig future elections and punish his political foes who committed the crime of refusing to help Trump rig a past election.

To say that Gabbard disgraced herself and her office is both a dramatic understatement and also perhaps the only true qualification she had for working in the second Trump presidency.

Gabbard wrote in Friday’s letter that “we have made significant progress at the ODNI – advancing unprecedented transparency and restoring integrity to the intelligence community,” while Trump thanked her and said, “Tulsi has done an incredible job.”

For most of her political career, Gabbard presented herself as an unshakably anti-war, anti-imperialist political figure of the progressive left – she literally sold campaign T-shirts that declared, “No War with Iran.” As Gabbard joined the MAGA movement, she maintained her professed commitment to preventing wars. “A vote for Donald Trump is a vote for a man who wants to end wars, not start them,” she proclaimed shortly before the 2024 election.

As Zeteo has noted, Gabbard’s anti-war story was always a lie, and that was the case even before she proudly, shamelessly served the agenda of one of the most fiendishly warmongering and violently imperialistic presidents that the United States has produced in a very long time.

And by the end of her time spent at the highest levels of Trump’s government, Gabbard revealed herself to have nothing but sneering contempt for American democratic values: someone who would gladly allow the U.S. intelligence apparatus to be perverted and disgraced, all to make Donald Trump feel better about a presidential election he lost and failed to steal.

At best, she allowed President Trump to turn her into one of his warmongers-by-proxy. By her own admission, being stridently anti-war was one of the most important things she could have done with her life. She wanted to stop regional conflicts before they started, she claimed. She said she wanted to save untold numbers of lives.

She threw that all away, like a used Swiffer wipe, in a heartbeat, so that she could serve at the pleasure of a former host of ‘Celebrity Apprentice’ who keeps threatening Iran with full-blown genocide.

Let that be her legacy.

item.title: No, China Isn’t Winning the Iran War. The U.S. Is Just Losing
item.description: Donald Trump and Benjamin Netanyahu’s war on Iran has killed thousands, violated U.S. and international law, and sparked deadly worldwide energy and food shortages, but the D.C. commentariat has been unable to resist innumerable takes that the real problem with the war is that it’s a “ win for…
item.author: Zeteo
item.content:encoded:
Donald Trump and Benjamin Netanyahu’s war on Iran has killed thousands, violated U.S. and international law, and sparked deadly worldwide energy and food shortages, but the D.C. commentariat has been unable to resist innumerable takes that the real problem with the war is that it’s a “win for China.”

It’s true that Xi Jinping has not confirmed Trump’s claim that Beijing offered to help with a deal during their summit last week, Chinese companies continue to equip the Iranian military, and the Trump administration has just claimed the Iran war forced them to conserve munitions promised to Taiwan.

But while Xi might stand to benefit from Trump’s insistence on undermining U.S. economic, diplomatic, and military power, he does not see the Iran war as a “win.” Washington’s fixation on viewing the degradation of American institutions from the perspective of the Chinese president is a cop-out that misunderstands Beijing and obscures – and empowers – the actual threats to our democracy.

Here are five things you need to know:

Beijing views Trump’s war in Iran as a threat to the stability China’s growth depends on, Jessica Chen Weiss, professor of China studies at the Johns Hopkins School of Advanced International Studies and author of the upcoming book What China Wants, tells me. She says Beijing’s paramount goal in the region is market access, in support of “defending their own security, prosperity, and development.”

But the Iranian Navy’s closure of the Strait of Hormuz has put these goals at risk, literally constricting access to Middle Eastern markets and depriving China of up to half of its energy imports. A global recession triggered by energy shortages would further threaten China’s export-driven growth.

Beijing is better prepared than most to weather the closure of the Strait of Hormuz thanks to healthy oil reserves, coal, green energy investments, and access to Russian and Iranian oil, and could pick up market share from smaller manufacturing economies, but it’s unlikely Beijing would see possibly emerging in a relatively stronger position from a growth-killing global economic crisis it didn’t want in the first place “winning.”

To the extent that U.S. partners and dependencies are hedging their bets by looking to China for leadership, that’s more of a function of Trump’s chaos than any plot by Xi.

One of Congress’s chief concerns has been that the prodigious number of munitions expended in the Iran war, including some likely scavenged from U.S. forces in Asia, could “hurt readiness” in the event the U.S. went to war with China over Taiwan. The Trump administration fed those fears yesterday, claiming it paused a weapons sale to Taiwan because it needed those munitions to fight Iran.

To even have this conversation, we have to wave away the serious risk of global nuclear holocaust from any U.S.-China conflict, as D.C. China hawks tend to, but here it goes: If the U.S. military expended 20% to 50% of its strategic munitions without coming close to defeating a regional power with no air force to speak of, then even prewar stockpiles never offered substantial “readiness” against one of the largest militaries and the largest industrial power in the world. Which is to say, the war on Iran made the Pentagon marginally less prepared for a war it was already wholly unprepared for.

That’s not an argument to prepare more judiciously for war with China; it’s a warning that proponents of civilizational conflict might not have been totally upfront about the costs, which we’ve now gotten a taste of before being talked into war with a nuclear power.

As for the held-up arms shipment to Taiwan: that had nothing to do with munitions shortages, at least according to Trump, who framed the sale as a bargaining chip with Xi; he did the same thing last year – long before this war – to try to gain leverage over Beijing in tariff negotiations.

But if Beijing isn’t winning the Iran war, why is it helping Iran’s government? The accusations are accurate: Chinese companies are buying Iranian oil, selling “dual use” components like the engines used in Iranian attack drones, selling satellite imagery, and might have sold anti-air missiles to Iran. It certainly looks like Beijing has stepped up to come to Tehran’s rescue – but this is pretty much exactly what Chinese companies were already doing long before the war, and they do similar business with countries Iran has been fighting. Chinese firms sell finished weapons to Gulf states like Saudi Arabia and the UAE, and Beijing imported far more oil from them than from Iran before the blockade. And on the topic of dual-use components, Chinese companies also continue to sell the rare earth minerals the U.S. military needs for the missiles it uses to attack Iran.

Of course, China’s deepening ties with Arab Gulf states also raised alarms in D.C., where policymakers maintain the challenging expectation that Beijing be neither friends with America’s allies nor its enemies.

As for Beijing negotiating limited safe transit through the Strait of Hormuz – it turns out governments can do that if they’re not actively bombing Iran, as even U.S. allies like France have demonstrated, though they may be paying a higher toll.

Despite Trump’s pleas, Xi sees little to gain from trying to push Tehran to open the Strait of Hormuz. Beijing “doesn’t think of Trump as a particularly reliable interlocutor,” Weiss says, and so “won’t twist arms to bring the conflicting parties to the table.” Plus, even if he wanted to risk burning his relationship with Tehran, Xi doesn’t expect his leverage to be effective given the existential stakes for Iran’s leadership.

Whether in Tehran or Beijing, Trump’s untrustworthiness is the main obstacle to progress, and is probably why the Trump-Xi summit produced almost zero solid commitments from Xi on any issue, including Iran.

On closer inspection, Beijing’s big win from the Iran war looks more like the Chinese government trying to maintain the same relationships and development goals it had before being subjected to a crisis it sees as largely outside its control.

The danger with diagnosing every American setback as a win for the CCP is that the prescription will inevitably be renewed belligerence, at the expense of treating the actual sources of American dysfunction, which are entirely homegrown. Anti-China policy offers policymakers a tempting escape, which is why it’s the closest thing left to a bipartisan consensus in D.C.

For fading elder statesmen, China fits neatly into a zero-sum Cold War paradigm that expired 30 years ago. For younger counterparts who believed “The End of History” was real, China is the obstacle to an equally zero-sum, ahistoric vision of U.S. security that depends on every country in the world being functionally subservient to the United States, forever. For the insecure and aimless, China is an opportunity to talk tough. And for politicians of both parties generously funded by the military-industrial complex, which now includes ascendant AI giants and technofascist outfits like Palantir, the menacing CCP is an evergreen pretense to funnel taxpayer dollars to billionaires. That’s why the founders of Palantir and OpenAI have gone in on an anti-China influence campaign – they’re the ultimate beneficiaries of D.C.’s tough talk.

The Chinese government does have policies Washington should strongly object to, especially crimes against humanity against ethnic minorities and aspirations to absorb democratic Taiwan under Beijing’s autocratic system. These and other concerns demand diplomatic engagement, but they don’t amount to the existential threat to the United States that lawmakers and Palantir CEO Alex Karp describe, and do not justify the trillions of precious taxpayer dollars spent for a “strategic competition” that Trump, giving away the game as always, couldn’t care less about.

“I don’t think [Beijing’s] goal is to replace the United States,” says Weiss, “they are much more nationalist and parochial in their objectives.”

Even if you don’t believe her, it hardly matters. Beijing posing a genuine threat to the security of its fellow nuclear power and top trade partner remains far off, if not entirely hypothetical. But right now, American democracy – that thing we’re supposed to be afraid of the CCP taking – is being collapsed in front of our eyes by the same oligarchs who demand we pay them to protect us from China.

Harrison Mann is a former US Army major and executive officer of the Defense Intelligence Agency’s Middle East/Africa Regional Center who resigned in protest of his office’s support for Israel’s war in Gaza under the Biden administration. He is currently with the group Win Without War and recently became a Zeteo contributor.

Check out more from Zeteo:

If you enjoy Zeteo, share it with your friends and earn rewards when they subscribe.

item.title: Has the Democratic Party Learned Nothing From 2024?
item.description: On this day in 1856, pro-slavery South Carolina congressman Preston Brooks used his cane to savagely beat abolitionist Massachusetts Senator Charles Sumner at his desk on Capitol Hill – a key point on the road to civil war. Good morning! Andrew here. Democrats have lost to Donald Trump twice, and…
item.author: First Draft | Zeteo
item.content:encoded:
On this day in 1856, pro-slavery South Carolina congressman Preston Brooks used his cane to savagely beat abolitionist Massachusetts Senator Charles Sumner at his desk on Capitol Hill – a key point on the road to civil war.

Good morning! Andrew here. Democrats have lost to Donald Trump twice, and we all continue to pay for it – the vulnerable more than others. You might hope, at some point, the Democratic Party would carry out a fact-finding mission to tell us all why that happened. Well, then I have some good news, as well as some bad news.

In today’s ‘First Draft,’ we look under the hood of the Democrats’ failed 2024 autopsy report, Trump’s $1.776 billion slush fund threatens his $1 billion ballroom, and the Trump administration drops charges against six ICE protesters in Chicago after a judge alleged prosecutorial misconduct.

The Democratic National Committee on Thursday finally released its long-awaited autopsy, supposed to explain why the party lost to Donald Trump a second time.

The primary reasons why Democrats lost in 2024 are well-documented and widely understood: There was broad dissatisfaction with the economy and brutal post-pandemic inflation; Joe Biden chose to run again even though his physical decline was obvious, to the point that he was forced to quit the race with just months to go; and Biden’s vice president, Kamala Harris, adamantly refused to break with her boss over his support for Israel’s genocide in Gaza.

As I previously reported with Swin in our own version of a 2024 autopsy, Democrats also ran a bad campaign from a technical perspective. They failed to adapt to a rapidly changing media environment, didn’t present a cohesive message, and relied obsessively on data to drive decision-making in ways that didn’t make much sense.

Swin and I had to complete our story on “How the Democrats Blew It” by January 2025. The DNC never finished its actual autopsy report.

The document released on Tuesday, titled “BUILD TO WIN. BUILD TO LAST,” remains shockingly incomplete a year and a half after Trump won, even though these reports are a perfunctory campaign tradition: The losing side conducts countless interviews and extensive research, disclosing it for the sake of transparency, with the point of doing better next time.

In this case, the DNC appears to have studiously avoided public discussion of any topics that would require introspection on the part of decision-makers. Within its 192 pages, the autopsy fails to mention Gaza or Biden’s health, or his decision to run again, even once.

The fact that the document ignored Biden’s choice to run again isn’t entirely a surprise: The New York Times reported last summer that the report would avoid this topic.

The lack of interest in Gaza was more surprising, given the report’s author, Democratic consultant Paul Rivera, met with a pro-Palestinian group, IMEU Policy Project, and “told them the war in Gaza hurt Democrats in the 2024 election,” according to Axios.

The DNC autopsy is embarrassing for other reasons.

It begins with this disclaimer: “This document reflects the views of the author, not the DNC. The DNC was not provided with the underlying sourcing, interviews, or supporting data for many of the assertions contained herein and therefore cannot independently verify the claims presented.”

The document contains many notes in which unnamed persons reject the author’s assertions. “No evidence or sourcing provided for many claims in this section,” reads one. “Data appears to be inaccurate and contradicts public reporting,” says another.

The report has no conclusion, meaning it does not even attempt to detail a path forward.

When DNC chair Ken Martin was elected last year, he pledged to release the 2024 autopsy, noting the party buried its report after Trump’s first win in 2016. “It will be released to our members,” he said. “And we all have to learn from that.”

Martin backed off that promise in December, suggesting the report would be a “distraction.”

After weeks of pressure, the DNC chair chose to release the document – though that might have been to preempt CNN, which had obtained it.

“I am not proud of this product; it does not meet my standards, and it won’t meet your standards,” Martin wrote on Substack. “I don’t endorse what’s in this report, or what’s left out of it. I could not in good faith put the DNC’s stamp of approval on it.”

Indeed, the document did not meet many people’s standards. As Democratic pollster John Hagner joked, “The DNC autopsy is maybe the first thing I’ve read in the AI era that would have been improved by an LLM.”

Harris’s 2024 deputy campaign manager Rob Flaherty told Swin, “It’s not that they didn’t complete it, though – it’s that they completed badly. I think there’s a lot of people who were involved in the campaign on all sides who would have interesting insights. It’s a shame that we didn’t get to have that discussion. They didn’t reach out to basically anyone directly.”

Flaherty published his own version of a 2024 autopsy in the Bulwark last week. His article is a more interesting and thoughtful read than what the DNC put together.

Martin is now facing calls to resign over the botched, buried, now-released autopsy.

Some Democratic insiders believe the point of demanding Martin release the report was about the party establishment and centrist Democrats wanting to force him out for other reasons.

One strategist tells me Martin has been pushing for a revised primary calendar that wouldn’t give priority status to redder states like Iowa and South Carolina, where Democrats aren’t competitive in presidential contests.

“I think this has a lot of people in the Democratic establishment shook,” the strategist says.

“Who gives a shit about the autopsy? What really matters is the presidential primary calendar. Everyone knows inflation and Gaza are why we lost.”

  • Vote pulled:House Republicanspulleda vote on a resolution to invoke congressional authority and end the Iran war, when it became clear they would lose.
  • Talks report:Secretary of State Marco Rubiosaid“some good signs” were emerging from negotiations mediated by Pakistan, but also said Iran’s stock of enriched uranium and control of the Strait of Hormuz remained sticking points.
  • Loggerheads:Trumptoldreporters the U.S. would seize the uranium, saying: “We will get it. We don’t need it, we don’t want it. We’ll probably destroy it after we get it, but we’re not going to let them have it.” ReuterssaidAyatollah Mojtaba Khamenei had directed that the uranium must not be surrendered.
  • Trump at Bay:Michael Bay, that is. The director of bombastic Hollywood flicks, including Transformers and Pearl Harbor, isreportedlydeveloping a film based on the recent rescue of two U.S. airmen downed over Iran. Presumably, the film will be shot after he’s finished work on “Untitled Skibidi Toilet Movie.”Really.
  • Lebanon news:As Israeli strikes continued in defiance of the supposed ceasefire, killing at least one person, the U.S.announcedsanctions on nine people it said enabled Hezbollah to “undermine Lebanon’s sovereignty.” Lebanese officialsputthe death toll from Israeli strikes at close to 3,100.

Acting Attorney General Todd Blanche went to Capitol Hill on Thursday to sell Republican senators on Donald Trump’s slush fund for allies, including January 6 rioters, aka the $1.776 billion “Anti-Weaponization Fund” announced this week.

Created in the supposed “settlement” of Trump’s outrageous $10 billion lawsuit against the Internal Revenue Service, the fund came with a bonus announcement that Trump, his sons, and his business are now immune from IRS scrutiny.

Oddly enough, Blanche’s big date didn’t go well.

At lunch, GOP senators got mad. Strong statements spewed forth. To pluck one, Mitch McConnell of Kentucky said: “So the nation’s top law enforcement official is asking for a slush fund to pay people who assault cops? Utterly stupid, morally wrong – take your pick.”

John Thune of South Dakota sent his troops home for the Memorial Day recess. It meant an ICE funding bill will not be ready by the White House’s June 1 deadline.

That bill won’t include $1 billion for Trump’s ballroom, that politically toxic quest having been dropped. But there are plenty more uncivil wars to be had. Consider Trump’s actions to rid himself of critics, Louisiana Senator Bill Cassidy and Kentucky Rep. Thomas Massie beaten by MAGA challengers. Consider Trump’s endorsement of scandal-ridden Texas Attorney General Ken Paxton over John Cornyn, part of Senate leadership.

There is also increasing pain in the House: Brian Fitzpatrick of Pennsylvania, a swing-seat Republican whose Fox reporter fiancée Trump has attacked, released a bill with New York Democrat Tom Suozzi. Its aim: to deny Trump’s slush fund taxpayer dollars.

Late on Thursday, House GOP leaders pulled a vote on a resolution to assert congressional authority and end Trump’s disastrous war with Iran. Such votes have failed repeatedly. Now, the GOP knew it would lose if it tried.

  • ICE case dropped:Federal prosecutorsdroppedall charges against the “Broadview Six,” including formerZeteocontributor Kat Abughazaleh, who were indicted in Chicago for allegedly blocking vehicles outside an ICE facility last September. Detailing prosecutorial misconduct, Judge April Perrysaidtrust in federal authorities had been “broken.”
  • Archvillain:OKedby a key federal commission filled with Trump’s cronies, the president’s ludicrous 250-foot triumphal arch cleared another key hurdle on its way to besmirching the capital and ruining the view at Arlington National Cemetery.
  • Unhappy returns:Trump sent birthdaywishesto Laura Loomer, his extremist ally whopeddled hate speechin the immediate aftermath of the deadly shooting at the Islamic Center of San Diego on Monday.
  • Order disorder:Trump abruptly postponed an executive order on AI policy.Politicosaidtop AI execs had indicated they would not attend a signing ceremony.
  • Dem shift:Nearly three-quarters of Democratic voters oppose military aid to Israel, up from 45% three years ago, newNew York Times-Siena pollingfound.Nearly half such voters say the party is too supportive of Israel.
  • Stand firm, Maine:A judge rejected Trump’s attempt to obtain voter information in Maine. Secretary of State Shanna Bellowssaid: “Trump and the DOJ may continue to try to interfere with free and fair elections run by the states. We will not let them.”
  • Bad signs:Also in Maine, a referendum on banning transgender girls from girls’ sports willlikelybe removed from the November ballot, after hundreds of petition signatures weredisqualified.

In 2024, as part of a sponsorship deal for her Rumble channel, what did devoted (and deranged) Trump stan Laura Loomer film herself eating?

Keep your eyes out for the answer below!

  • 🇵🇸 Don’t forget Gaza:Israelkilledat least six people in Gaza yesterday, despite the supposed ceasefire. Meanwhile, Israeldeportedhundreds of members of the international aid flotilla it repeatedly – and illegally – stopped from reaching Gaza. Arriving in Istanbul, some deportees appeared to be limping. Others, includingZeteocontributor Alex Colston,describedviolent mistreatment.

  • 🇨🇺

    Cuba crosshairs:U.S. military against Cuba action looms, as Trumpsaidhe would be “happy” to do “something.” Marco Rubio said he didn’t think diplomacy could achieve U.S. ends in relation to the communist government in Havana.

  • 🇹🇼

    Sale pause:Acting Navy Secretary Hung Caotoldsenators the U.S. was pausing a $14 billion arms sale to Taiwan because of the war in Iran, but insisted U.S. stockpiles were not a problem. Trump had said he might hold off on the deal, after discussing Taiwan with Xi Jinping of China in Beijing last week.
  • 🇵🇱 Pole position:After causing alarm by saying the U.S. would reduce its NATO commitment, Trump reversed course andsaid5,000 troops would go to Poland.

  • 🇬🇱

    Nuuk off:Several hundred Greenlandersprotestedthe opening of a U.S. facility in the capital, amid fierce opposition to Trump’s wish to acquire the island.

  • 🇨🇩

    Ebola news:People angry at not being able to retrieve a body burned an Ebola treatment center in eastern Congo, the heart of the outbreak, officialssaid.


Fresh from being censured by his own Democratic Party for commuting the sentence of Tina Peters, a county clerk who backed Donald Trump’s attempt to overturn the 2020 election, Colorado Governor Jared Polis held a press conference to promote healthy foods. Biting into a carrot, he asked for questions. It wasn’t his best move.

On his ‘Unpopular Front’ Substack, Nation columnist John Ganz looks at how Donald Trump can be so utterly screwed in the polls and yet so clearly in control of his party that critics – Senator Bill Cassidy, Rep. Thomas Massie – are easily defeated in high-profile primaries. The answer is… not a cheerful one.

From Liz Skalka at the Columbia Journalism Review, how the White House Correspondents’ Association has courted Trump amid his assault on press freedom and the assassination attempt at its dinner last month. “Depending on your view,” Skalka writes, “the association has been either shrewdly calculating in its restrained approach or shamefully feckless, projecting normalcy during an autocratic time.”

Inside Graham Platner’s Controversial Rise

The rough-hewn Maine progressive surged past Governor Janet Mills to seize the Democratic Senate nomination and is polling ahead of Susan Collins, the Republican incumbent. He tells Julia Terruso of Time, “If the Democratic Party is going to have a future with working people, it needs to pick the side of working people.”

The Man Who Explains Israel to John Fetterman

Finally, a great look inside John Fetterman’s office from New York magazine that explores his friendship with a little-known but apparently wealthy “business professional” … who reportedly listens in on the Pennsylvania Democrat’s phone calls with Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu.

Zeteo’s Martin Pengelly contributed to this newsletter.

Still want to get Zeteo content, but not this newsletter? You can unsubscribe directly from ‘First Draft’ by going to ‘

If you enjoy Zeteo, share it with your friends and earn rewards when they subscribe.

He tells Julia Terruso of Time, “If the Democratic Party is going to have a future with working people, it needs to pick the side of working people.”

The Man Who Explains Israel to John Fetterman

Finally, a great look inside John Fetterman’s office from New York magazine that explores his friendship with a little-known but apparently wealthy “business professional” … who reportedly listens in on the Pennsylvania Democrat’s phone calls with Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu.

Zeteo’s Martin Pengelly contributed to this newsletter.

Still want to get Zeteo content, but not this newsletter? You can unsubscribe directly from ‘First Draft’ by going to ‘

item.title: When a MAGA Manufactured Outrage Campaign Came After Me
item.description: A social media post from libertarian journalist Nick Gillespie caught my eye recently. He was crowd-sourcing ideas. “I’m interviewing Neil Gorsuch later today,” Gillespie wrote. “What should I ask him?” The Reason magazine editor-at-large was doing what diligent journalists do, soliciting ideas for…
item.author: Zeteo
item.content:encoded:
A social media post from libertarian journalist Nick Gillespie caught my eye recently. He was crowd-sourcing ideas. “I’m interviewing Neil Gorsuch later today,” Gillespie wrote. “What should I ask him?”

The Reason magazine editor-at-large was doing what diligent journalists do, soliciting ideas for topics he might have overlooked in earlier preparation. His post also alerted his 118,500 followers on Twitter of special content heading their way.

That reminded me of a lesson I learned 10 years ago: Even such basic research can be made to look sinister in the hands of bad-faith political actors. And by bad-faith political actors, I mean Donald Trump’s presidential campaign, the Russian intelligence agents helping him, and the right-wing media flunkies on their side.

Here’s what happened.

In 2015, as another presidential campaign heated up, I set out to conduct in-depth CNBC interviews with major candidates in both parties. That September, former Florida Gov. Jeb Bush agreed to sit down with me in Iowa.

It seemed an important interview. Foolish though it sounds today, I expected Bush to become the Republican nominee.

I did my normal preparation, checking out his speeches and campaign materials, reviewing economic data, reading assessments by other journalists. I also, as usual, reached out to Bush’s rivals.

Smart opponents know pressure points an interviewer might explore. Before sitting down with President Barack Obama, I’d reach out to Senate Republican leader Mitch McConnell; before questioning McConnell, I’d reach out to House Speaker Nancy Pelosi. And so on.

For the Bush interview, I consulted campaigns with varying perspectives like Marco Rubio, John Kasich, and Mike Huckabee. Since I expected Hillary Clinton to face off against Bush the following November, I also tried her team in an effort to “skate to where the puck is going,” as hockey legend Wayne Gretzky liked to say.

My terse email to Clinton campaign chair John Podesta bore this subject line: “What should I ask Jeb”?

Like Gillespie with Gorsuch, I had more than one purpose. I hoped letting Team Clinton know Bush had granted an interview might prod them to finally grant the one I had sought for months.

Podesta never responded. The Bush interview went well. The Clinton campaign didn’t consent to one for another six months.

I forgot about that digital outreach until October 2016. That’s when Podesta’s emails, hacked by Russian intelligence in an attempt to help Trump win, were publicly released by Wikileaks to try to harm Clinton’s campaign.

It worked. The strategically timed release occurred immediately after the Washington Post published a now-infamous video of Trump bragging backstage to a television host about committing sexual assault.

There were no genuinely scandalous Podesta emails. But gossipy accounts of internal Team Clinton communications helped offset damaging coverage of Trump’s raw misogyny.

And what do you know? Because I periodically emailed Podesta, some of the stories involved me.

“This is breaking this morning – and it’s big,” declared Fox Business anchor Stuart Varney, reporting on my “What should I ask Jeb” note, calling it “an outrage.”

“Campaign collusion: Is CNBC’s John Harwood too close to the Clinton operation?” read The Hill’s headline. The Washington Free Beacon chimed in: “WikiLeaks Emails Reveal Cozy Relationship Between John Harwood and Clinton Campaign.”

That was absurd. Other notes in the Podesta treasure trove showed how angry he was over my spring 2016 story mocking Clinton’s hyper-caution in dealing with reporters – a political weakness I’d pointed out for years.

Honest Republican political operatives, including Stuart Stevens, spoke up to call my email to Podesta routine. “A standard reporter query,” tweeted longtime GOP consultant Gordon Hensley.

But the Russians only hacked and released Podesta’s emails, not those of the Republican campaigns to which I sent the same question. So I gave pro-Trump hacks a fat target for their efforts to rouse support by discrediting journalistic “bias” against Republicans.

I’ve long observed myself that the mainstream media leans left of center; that’s because of the kind of people drawn to the profession. But this episode was not an example.

Nor, in fact, was press coverage of the 2016 campaign overall. The mainstream media’s greatest failure that year was the wildly disproportionate attention lavished on Clinton’s email practices as Obama’s secretary of state.

The false impression of wrongdoing that created badly damaged her campaign and helped make Trump president. Today, the outlandish corruption and malfeasance of his second presidency offers daily reminders of the triviality of “but her emails.”

My part was just another twig tossed onto the gigantic bonfire of anger whipped up by cynics exploiting white working-class grievance for their benefit. If only it hadn’t burned down so much of the American experiment for everyone else.

To this day, I’m frequently disparaged on Twitter by hostile commenters invoking that 11-year-old email. As in a children’s game of telephone, where a whispered message gets altered from one player to the next, sometimes they insist my note rigged a campaign debate.

That won’t be a problem for Nick Gillespie. His Gorsuch interview came off well, though he ended up not using any of the crowd-sourced questions.

He actually liked proffered suggestions that he explore Gorsuch’s periodic alignment with liberal justices on cases involving Native Americans. Alas, Gillespie told me this week: He forgot to ask the question.

If you enjoy Zeteo, share it with your friends and earn rewards when they subscribe.
