item.title: Inside Trump’s War on Late-Night Comedy – and Why It Matters
item.description: @celebpop_ Donald Trump lets Jimmy Fallon mess up his hair. #trump #jimmyfallon #interview #funny Donald Trump’s lawless and increasingly authoritarian second term has been marked by a vicious and corrupt campaign against the media – with the president extracting major payoffs from the parent…
item.author: "Zeteo | U.S. Politics"
item.content:encoded:
@celebpop_

Donald Trump lets Jimmy Fallon mess up his hair. #trump #jimmyfallon #interview #funny

Donald Trump’s lawless and increasingly authoritarian second term has been marked by a vicious and corrupt campaign against the media – with the president extracting major payoffs from the parent companies of ABC and CBS, threatening to pull broadcast licenses or derail corporate mergers after seeing unflattering TV segments, and incessantly demanding the firing of a TV host he hates, after celebrating the cancellation of a different host’s long-running show.

This has been a long time coming, and the seeds of this cartoonishly mad-king presidential conduct were planted during his first term. Trump has been trying to start several of his wars for nearly a decade – not just on Iran, but on two former Comedy Central hosts, Stephen Colbert and Jimmy Kimmel. The difference now is he’s surrounded himself with aggressive personality-cultists who don’t think laws or democratic norms are real, and who want to help Trump cement his cultural resentments and catty fury into federal policy.

Tonight, Colbert’s show will end. The president’s crusade against comics and talk show hosts will continue.

This story is based on interviews with various former and current Trump advisers, TV network sources, professional comedians, and others with intimate knowledge of the American president’s crackdowns on free speech and popular culture. In the course of our reporting, it became even clearer to us than it was before that Trump’s war on late-night TV isn’t a joke or some distraction. A cornerstone of the international autocrats’ playbook is smothering dissent in the media and on the airwaves. And that, perhaps above all, includes satire.

“Comedians are sort of always in the line of fire, because for the strongman to retain his image, he can’t conscience ridicule,” progressive political strategist Anat Shenker-Osorio tells Zeteo.

Trump’s effort to destroy late-night comedians underscores the depths to which he and the modern-day GOP will sink to vindictively pull the levers of state power, and they’ve had some success so far.

They haven’t won the war yet.

In early 2018, a then-senior White House official was stepping into the Oval Office to meet with Trump and other close advisers to the U.S. president. This official – who relayed this anecdote to Zeteo on the condition of anonymity – had a prescheduled meeting to discuss, among other things, the state of Special Counsel Robert Mueller’s federal investigation, which had (to put it mildly) become a target of Trump’s ire. But as soon as this official sat down at the Resolute Desk, the president of the United States clearly did not want to talk about the Mueller probe, or official government business, or domestic or foreign affairs.

Trump was brandishing a piece of paper that included copy-and-pasted headlines and social media posts about how Stormy Daniels – the former pornographic film star whose alleged sexual encounter with Trump would later become central to his criminal conviction in a New York hush money case – had recently sat for a Jan. 30, 2018 interview on ‘Jimmy Kimmel Live,’ an ABC late-night talk show hosted by a comedian Trump used to “love.”

The interview aired the night of President Trump’s State of the Union address in Washington, DC.

The then-senior Trump aide tells Zeteo that Trump “definitely was not happy,” and that the president took up “almost half” of the unrelated scheduled meeting railing against Kimmel and the former adult-film actress.

At one point, this source recalls, the president ranted about how what Kimmel was doing was illegal (it wasn’t) and said he wanted to sic his outside legal team on the comedian for platforming “lies” about him and Stormy Daniels.

“I told him why it was a bad idea to do that, and everyone just kept moving forward on other things,” this ex-official says. “Stuff like this would happen all the time.”

Trump himself, though, did not move on. In fact, the president has spent almost a decade nursing this grudge and contemplating how to use the legal system – and the full force of the U.S. federal government and law enforcement – to punish Kimmel and other famous hosts on late-night TV who make fun of him. It likely has not helped quell his rage that Trump knows that he used to be a welcome celebrity guest on the late-night talk show circuit, even once getting his hair playfully tussled by NBC’s ‘Tonight Show’ host Jimmy Fallon during his 2016 campaign.

But for whatever reasons, according to sources close to Trump who talked to him about these hosts during Term 1, Kimmel seemed to consistently rank the highest on the president’s late-night enemies list. “I don’t know why, but it really sounded to me, just with how angry he’d get, that he hated Kimmel the most; and this was back in 2018 and 2019, is what I’m talking about,” one former senior administration official recalls.

But that Oval Office outburst wasn’t the end of his obsession with threatening late-night comedy during his first term in office. In early 2018, as Rolling Stone reported, Trump was so mad about Kimmel that he ordered White House staff to call Disney’s government relations office in DC, including longtime Disney executive Richard Bates, to pressure the mega-corporation (which owns ABC) to get Kimmel to knock it off. (These efforts didn’t work.) By March 2019, the American president kept telling senior officials in his administration that he wanted the Justice Department to criminally investigate the NBC sketch comedy series ‘Saturday Night Live,’ ‘The Late Show with Stephen Colbert’ on CBS, Kimmel’s, and other hosts who teased Trump and got under his skin.

Trump’s legal reasoning, if you can call it that, was that these TV shows somehow constituted “an illegal campaign contribution from the Democrat Party,” and therefore warranted federal probes and charges.

During this first term, Trump’s then-Federal Communications Commission chair Ajit Pai announced that the FCC would investigate a crude joke made by ‘Late Show’ star Colbert about Trump’s apparent bromance with Russian leader Vladimir Putin.

The agency ultimately declined to take further action, even as Trump continued to privately pester his aides about the progress of that investigation, and about whether Colbert would be penalized.

All of that, though, took place during Trump’s last administration. As scandalous and horrific as Trump’s first term was, the president, his inner sanctum, and the MAGAfied Republican Party had yet to reach the violent, authoritarian heights that would soon be touched by the Trump-Vance administration.

During the Trump-Pence era, the president had appointed a number of senior advisers who routinely rolled their eyes at his demands to use the government to ransack late-night comedy.

Officials at the FCC and White House just shrugged. So did executives and lobbyists for the media companies and mega-corporations that ruled over the predominantly liberal and Trump-hating late-night television. The president’s wishes to tame or crush late-night hosts were, for the most part, ignored by those in positions of influence, as if they were listening to a toddler throwing a temper tantrum over nothing.

That was then.

Today, no one in corporate America or his administration is laughing it off anymore.

Indeed, Trump is now surrounded by senior government appointees who want to turn his bleak, wildly censorious fantasies into a cultural reality for the United States of America.

Since the dawn of the second Trump era, the federal government and its allies have waged nonstop warfare on free speech, a free press, and dissent in this country. And, as maddeningly simplistic as it sounds, Team Trump has used the president’s disdain for left-leaning late-night talk show hosts as a testing ground for some of their most brazen efforts to suppress speech that the Trump White House doesn’t like.

Ever since his reelection in 2024, President Trump has prioritized wielding the threat of FCC regulatory, licensing, and merger-approval powers in his attempts to bully massive corporations into silencing and undermining the media properties they own – the news programs and comedy shows that commit the high crime of pissing off Donald John Trump. Trump’s new FCC hatchetman, Brendan Carr, has been willing to partake in these intimidation and shakedown campaigns, in ways that Ajit Pai simply was not.

“I’m sure the New York Times has already written my obituary,” Carr told the Financial Times recently, as he laid out what he sees as his legacy. “We tried to do a large number of things. We tried to be bold in the decisions that we made … I got my time, I got my shot, I took my shot.”

Still, in recent months, according to two sources who have discussed the matter directly with Trump, the president has expressed billowing frustration that his administration is spending too much time threatening to pull federal TV licenses, and not enough time actually “doing it,” as one of the people specifically recalls Trump saying in a meeting. (The president and his administration appointees have floated stripping television networks of their broadcasting licenses because of things like late-night comedy monologues that this White House condemns, and also over critical news coverage of Trump’s disastrous war in Iran.)

Already, there are signs that sustained, grassroots pressure and resistance to Trump’s agenda and censorship ambitions do, in fact, work – and can force major institutions to not immediately cave to the White House’s clampdown bluster.

Even still, on Thursday night, CBS is scheduled to run Colbert’s final episode of ‘The Late Show.’ Colbert is a staunch critic of the president and has told Trump to “go fuck yourself.” His series was an early casualty of the Trump-Vance era – and President Trump barely had to lift a finger to achieve such a victory.

Starting late last year, the nominal leader of the free world began bragging again, to longtime confidants and aides, about the ‘Late Show’ cancellation.

“Did you see I got rid of Colbert?” the president once remarked at his Mar-a-Lago luxury club in Florida, according to a Trump adviser, who referred to Trump’s aside as a “joke that isn’t really a joke.”

With Colbert’s CBS curtain call fast approaching, Trump has seemed elated by the development. He wanted Colbert criminally investigated since the days of his first presidency; playing a leading role, in Trump’s estimation, in the accelerated downfall of liberal late-night star’s program is the next best thing for MAGAworld.

Not long after his 2024 electoral victory over Democratic candidate Kamala Harris, Trump and his advisers quickly started making it clear to corporate America that those companies needed to fall in line with The Donald, pay up or offer up other “pounds of flesh,” and beg for mercy – otherwise, Team Trump is coming after you. The new Trump era had barely begun, and already CEOs and industry titans were lining up around the block for the chance of pre-surrendering to a notoriously spiteful wannabe “king.”

During the Biden-Trump presidential transition period, ABC rushed to settle one of Trump’s highly dubious lawsuits against ABC News; well-respected legal observers were extremely skeptical that Trump would prevail in court, but ABC – eager to defuse tensions with the incoming president – suddenly agreed to shove $15 million into the coffers of Trump’s supposed presidential library fund. This legal surrender infuriated a variety of ABC staffers and journalists, who viewed the move as plain cowardice and “capitulation.”

Months later, Paramount, which owns CBS, pledged to cough up $16 million to make another frivolous Trump lawsuit – this one about the editing of a CBS News ‘60 Minutes’ segment with then-Vice President Kamala Harris – go away. At the time, Paramount was counting on the Trump administration to approve its blockbuster, $8 billion merger with Skydance Media, with a conspicuously Trump-aligned mogul, David Ellison, taking control of the entertainment giant.

Right around the time of the Paramount settlement with Trump in the summer of 2025 is when CBS decided to announce the cancellation of Colbert’s late-night show, which constantly skewered the president and his policies and actions. The network press release insisted this was “purely a financial decision” not a political one, alluding to the declining fortunes of late-night comedy that has to compete with the duelling democratization and Balkanization of internet media.

Not many people were fooled by the spin, least of all comedians and late-night writers rooms.

Comedian Anthony Atamanuik, who used to play President Trump on Comedy Central, tells Zeteo he was asked to play Robert F. Kennedy Jr., Trump’s health and human services secretary, on Colbert just a few weeks after Trump’s inauguration.

“They had written a sketch and we had done a makeup test and all that stuff… then I found out CBS, not Colbert’s show, was pulling the segment,” he says. “It was corporate and network pressure, apparently. What was conveyed to me was that CBS had said: Look, his voice is considered a disability, and we are concerned about the blowback of doing RFK Jr.’s voice on the show. So, CBS bailed on the bit, and it didn’t seem that unusual to me at the time because networks and shows make decisions like that every day. But when I heard the announcement months later that Colbert was getting canceled, I thought to myself: ‘Ah… they shoulda done the bit!’”

By September of last year, the Trump-Vance administration claimed its biggest celebrity scalp yet – or so they thought. Following the assassination of Trump ally and right-wing media star Charlie Kirk, the administration and its conservative allies embarked on a widespread, multipronged attempt to curtail the free speech of their liberal opponents, big and small, and to harass left-wing organizations. Team Trump, led by the president’s White House policy architect Stephen Miller, got so serious about this that they were pulling all-nighters drafting confidential memos and legal justifications exploring novel ways of unleashing the tools left behind by President George W. Bush’s Global War on Terror – solely on the American left.

Kimmel – who Trump predicted would be “next” shortly after the announcement of the Colbert cancellation – would soon find himself caught up in this hysterical dragnet.

In a mid-September monologue on ‘Jimmy Kimmel Live,’ the host made a brief remark about the hyper-politicization of Kirk’s killing, a moment that was stripped entirely of its context by conservative luminaries, senior Trump officials, and right-wing influencers who wanted an excuse to muzzle Kimmel.

Soon enough, Trump’s FCC chair went on a MAGA podcast to threaten Disney, ABC, and local affiliates with government retaliation, if they refused to silence one of the president’s leading critics on network television. “We can do this the easy way or the hard way,” Carr said. “These companies can find ways to change conduct to take action on Kimmel or, you know, there’s going to be additional work for the FCC ahead.”

For a moment, it all seemed to work exactly as President Trump had so desired and envisioned.

Sinclair Broadcast Group and Nexstar – which own dozens of ABC affiliate stations and (like Disney) have important regulatory business before the Trump-controlled regulators – freaked out and yanked Kimmel’s show. ABC and Disney held rapid-fire emergency meetings, similarly flipped out, and hastily suspended production of Kimmel’s show.

Before the month was over, an intense public backlash in support of Kimmel and free expression (which reportedly included 1.7 million users canceling paid subscriptions to Disney+, ESPN, and Hulu in the span of one week) led to those decisions being reversed and Kimmel restored to the airwaves.

‘Jimmy Kimmel Live!’ was back, but in many respects the damage was done.

By then, it had been nearly a year of Trump racking up millions and millions of dollars worth of wins in his protracted crusade against late-night comedy and the media. It was a level of U.S. government corruption ready made for the history books, and all because some comedians and their writers had too frequently hurt the feelings of the most powerful person on the face of the planet.

Sources in and close to the administration who’ve talked to the president about this topic tell Zeteo that all of those developments taken together – the ABC payment, the Paramount cash, Colbert getting pushed out, Kimmel’s initial suspension – had convinced the president that he was on an unstoppable roll and that he could get his way with these sorts of demands, again and again.

“The payments were a huge deal for him,” a person close to the Trump White House says. “He’d say it means [the major companies] know we mean business.”

Sources in writers rooms and on the production teams of different late-night shows say that at the beginning of Trump’s second term, there was a distinct atmosphere of fear gripping much of late-night comedy, with staff unsure about whether their corporate overlords would have their backs, if push came to Trumpian-fascist shove. Fears were only exacerbated by last year’s announcement of the Colbert cancellation.

“There was a strong climate of fear in late-night because of that,” one senior member of a network late-night talk show says. “If Stephen Colbert isn’t safe in this political environment, then who or what is, you know?”

But by the middle of 2026, things appear to have shifted in both our politics and our culture, as President Trump’s poll numbers continue to crash through the floor. With that loss of whatever momentum he thought he had in 2024 and 2025, fewer people seem so openly afraid of him.

Last month, the president, First Lady Melania Trump, and their senior lieutenants restarted their campaign to get Kimmel thrown off the air. In a segment designed as a fake White House Correspondents’ Dinner “roast” of Trump, Kimmel made a crack that the first lady was beaming like an “expectant widow,” an obvious reference to Melania having married a celebrity billionaire who’s now old and in increasingly poor health. Just days later, an alleged anti-Trump gunman stormed the Washington hotel where the president was about to deliver a speech at the correspondents’ event. So, Team Trump started screeching, ludicrously, that Kimmel was calling for political violence with his “widow” jab.

Suddenly, the FCC’s Carr was concurrently announcing an early review of Disney’s broadcast licenses, in a move that the administration wants you to believe was just a coincidence in timing.

But at least for the time being, it appears that Disney and ABC have learned their lesson from the last time the Trump administration waged a ravenously corrupt blitz on ‘Jimmy Kimmel Live!’

“Disney’s not bending,” one ABC insider tells Zeteo. “The president might have to just take the ‘L’ on this one.”

Three sources with knowledge of the situation say that in recent weeks, Disney and ABC executives have privately assessed that – at least for now – it would be significantly worse for them from a business standpoint if they were seen as caving to Trump, than it would be if Donald and Melania Trump and Brendan Carr just kept being huffy toward them. They all remember the fierce backlash they got when they suspended Kimmel last year, and are incredibly eager to not go through that again.

Administration officials may be bidding a vengeful farewell to Colbert today – but that hasn’t stopped the Trump White House from continuing to keep a close eye on its nemeses in late-night TV – especially Kimmel. One White House official tells Zeteo that some staffers are still maintaining an “oppo file” on Kimmel – meaning, a running dossier and monitoring of the things he does and says publicly, just waiting for another moment to exploit or strike.

You can find some of this “file” online, in fact. The Trump White House now maintains an official government, taxpayer-funded webpage called the “Jimmy Kimmel Archives.” On it, the Trump-Vance administration has dubbed Kimmel possibly the worst thing it could ever think to call another human being: the White House refers to him as a “REPORTER.”

“Billionaires, and the right-wing political operatives they own as servants, hate comedy and despise comedy because it’s a great threat to their power,” says comedian James Adomian, who has played Trump loyalist and MyPillow CEO Mike Lindell on ‘Jimmy Kimmel Live.’ “They have tried over the past decade to crack down on it, to suppress it, to replace it with their artificial bot-net publicity version of it – but they’re still not funny and they still lose in comedy.”

Adomian adds: “The lucky, magical thing that everyone should remember is that the First Amendment is still the law, and it gives even greater, more heightened leeway and freedom to artists. And people need to remember that the government has no authority over what is funny… Any government that pretends to exercise authority over art and comedy is doing so illegitimately.”

In the coming years, that absolutely will not stop this iteration of the federal government from trying.

Zeteo’s Kennedy Garmong and Andrew Perez contributed reporting.

item.title: Jeff Bezos, Trump Toady
item.description: On this day in 1956, over Bikini Atoll in the Pacific Ocean, the U.S. conducted the first airborne test of an improved hydrogen bomb. Four miles across, the fireball was brighter than 500 suns. By 1963, open-air and undersea nuclear tests were banned. Good morning, friends. Mehdi here. I went on ‘…
item.author: First Draft | Zeteo
item.content:encoded:
On this day in 1956, over Bikini Atoll in the Pacific Ocean, the U.S. conducted the first airborne test of an improved hydrogen bomb. Four miles across, the fireball was brighter than 500 suns. By 1963, open-air and undersea nuclear tests were banned.

Good morning, friends. Mehdi here. I went on ‘Piers Morgan Uncensored’ yesterday and, painfully, Piers ended our interview insisting I - a Liverpool fan - congratulate him - an Arsenal fan - for his team winning the Premier League after 22 years. I reluctantly did so. Sorry.

Anyways, in today’s ‘First Draft,’ Jeff Bezos reminds us that he has no principles and no shame; Democrats mount a fightback against Donald Trump’s most crazily corrupt act ever; and Benjamin Netanyahu wants us to believe that Itamar Ben-Gvir doesn’t represent Israel or its values (LOL!).

Here we go…

Do you remember what Donald Trump used to call Amazon founder and Washington Post owner Jeff Bezos during his first term as President? I do.

“Jeff Bozo.”

Second-term Trump and the fourth-richest man in the world now get along much better than they did before. Because Bezos, like so many other billionaire tech moguls, has gone from loudly criticizing the president to shamelessly capitulating to him.

On Wednesday, in an interview with CNBC’s Andrew Ross Sorkin, Bezos fawned over the president, saying “Trump has lots of good ideas” and “you have to give him credit where credit is due.”

That is, of course, billionaire-speak for “He cut my taxes!” - which Trump definitely did. According to one study, since the 2017 Trump tax cuts, “Amazon founder Jeff Bezos has pocketed $36.6 billion in capital gains from selling shares of his company stock” and “owed $6.2 billion less in federal taxes on these gains.” According to another study, Amazon stands to gain $15 billion from Trump’s One Big Beautiful Bill Act (OBBBA) last year.

Billionaires can never have enough billions, so Bezos is happy to humiliate himself by heaping praise on a man-child president who obviously deserves none. Consider this line about Trump from Bezos that jumped out to me from his interview with Ross Sorkin.

“I think he is a more mature, more disciplined version of himself than he was in his first term.”

Sorry, what? WHAAAAAAAATTTTT?

The president who spent three hours in the middle of the night the other week posting 50 times on social media, a mix of insane conspiracy theories and unhinged threats, including references to Barack Obama as a “demonic force” and a “traitor”?

The president who posted an image depicting himself as… Jesus Christ?

The president who posted an AI-generated video of himself in a fighter jet dropping excrement on American citizens protesting against his presidency?

The president who took to social media to warn that “a whole civilization will die tonight, never to be brought back again”? Who has repeatedly threatened Iran with a nuclear holocaust?

That’s the guy who we’re being told to believe is the “more mature, more disciplined” commander-in-chief this time around? Really, Jeff? REALLY?

Do these billionaires have no shame? No self-respect? No spines? And do they not understand that the history books will judge them for having been enablers of a deranged fascist?

  • Netanyahu angry:Benjamin Netanyahu’s “hair was on fire” after a tense call with Donald Trump over a peace proposal drafted by Pakistan and Qatar, Axios (surprise!)quoteda source as saying. The site added that the Israeli prime minister wants more strikes on Iran.
  • Trump unsure:The president said Netanyahu would do as he was told, butequivocatedon talks, saying: “The only question is, do we go and finish it up, or are they gonna be signing a document. Let’s see what happens.”
  • Boarding party:In the Gulf of Oman, U.S. forcesboarded, redirected, and released a tanker flying the Iranian flag.
  • Lebanon latest:Eight people werekilledin strikes on southern Lebanon as Israel continued to ignore the supposed ceasefire. Israel haskilledmore than 3,073 people in Lebanon since the latest escalation began, according to Lebanese officials.

On Wednesday, Democrats and others attempted to fight back against Donald Trump’s latest corrupt outrage: a $1.776 billion slush fund to reward allies he claims were the victims of weaponized government under Joe Biden, and the attendant DOJ declaration that Trump family taxes are off limits for government probes.

Writing to acting Attorney General Todd Blanche, Treasury Secretary Scott Bessant, and Internal Revenue Service CEO Frank Bisignano, House ranking members Rep. Jamie Raskin (Oversight) and Rep. Richard Neal (Ways & Means) lamented “one of the most brazen acts of public corruption and self-dealing in American history,” and demanded documents and answers before Bessant testifies at Ways & Means in June.

Alas, Republicans control those committees. But Raskin also introduced a bill to stop the use of federal cash for payouts. Rep. Mark Pocan’s gambit was a bill to tax payouts at 100%, “so all taxpayer funding goes back to the Treasury rather than into the hands of Trump allies.”

Republicans were not entirely silent. Amid rumblings in the Senate, Rep. Brian Fitzpatrick, a vulnerable Pennsylvanian, wrote to Blanche to express “urgent concern” about “dangerous backsliding in the transparency of our institutions and our commitment to the American taxpayer.” To reporters, he was more direct. “We’re gonna try to kill” the fund, he said. Other Republicans are unhappy, too.

January 6 offenders are likely to be prominent beneficiaries should Trump’s slush fund pay out. In federal court on Wednesday, Harry Dunn and Matthew Hodges, cops on duty at the Capitol that day, filed suit, seeking to have the fund declared illegal.

  • Albanese win:The U.S.removedUN special rapporteur Francesca Albanese from a sanctions list a week after a judge temporarily blocked enforcement of the sanctions, saying they likely violated her free speech.
  • Ballroom bust:Senator John KennedysaidRepublicans had given up on finding a way to pass $1 billion in taxpayer funds for Trump’s White House ballroom.
  • Cop chiller:Askedif he would be okay with people convicted of hurting police – Jan. 6 rioters, say – getting payouts from Trump’s “Anti-Weaponization Fund,” acting AG Todd Blanche said: “Just to be clear, people that hurt police get money all the time.” So that’s a yes, then.
  • Rest in peace:Former Massachusetts Democratic Rep. Barney Frankdiedat 86. In office for 32 years and one of the first openly gay members of Congress, Frank put his name to historic financial reforms and often clashed with the left of his party.
  • Maine chance:A Pan-Atlantic Research pollputprogressive Graham Platner seven points up on Republican incumbent Susan Collins in the race for U.S. Senate, 48%-41%. Platner also had a 21-point lead on favorability, +3 to -18.
  • Freed speech:A Tennessee man who spent 37 days in jail for posting a meme critical of Trump after the death of Charlie Kirk willcollect$835,000, after agreeing to drop a civil rights suit against the county sheriff and other officials.

Which Republican led calls for Barney Frank to be expelled from Congress over a 1980s relationship with an escort, only to end up retiring in 2009, after pleading guilty to disorderly conduct in an airport men’s room?

Keep your eyes out for the answer below!

  • 🇵🇸 Don’t forget Gaza:At least four Palestinians werekilledin Israeli attacks on Gaza today, bringing the death toll from Israel’s violations of the so-called ceasefire to more than 880 people.
  • 🇵🇸 Deranged minister:Yesterday, ultranationalist Israeli National Security Minister Itamar Ben-Gvirpostedavideoof himself mocking members of the international aid flotilla abducted in international waters earlier this week, as they knelt with hands tied and heads pressed into the ground. Numerous countriesprotested. Preposterously, Benjamin Netanyahu – who appointed Ben-Gvir and has refused to fire him – said Ben-Gvir’s behavior was “not in line with Israel’s values and norms.” Today, Israel began deporting the flotilla participants it had illegally detained.
  • 🇹🇼 Arms plea:Speaking a week after Chinese President Xi Jinping warned Donald Trump that the U.S. and China could collide over Taiwan, Taiwanese President Lai Ching-tesaidhe wanted to buy weapons from the U.S.

  • 🇨🇺

    Pressure builds:The U.S.indictedRaúl Castro, 94, over the 1996 downing of two planes flown by Cuban exiles. The former president and five other men are charged with conspiracy to kill U.S. nationals, destruction of aircraft, and murder, amid messaging apparently meant topave the way for military action.

  • 🇨🇩

    Ebola update:ISIS-linked militantskilled at least 17in Ituri province, a hotspot of the rare form of Ebola that has killedmore than 130in DR Congo.


Democratic ad makers are certainly spoiled for choice these days. What to pick from this inane word salad about the Iran war? Perhaps this breathtaking claim, in reference to US military casualties: “I get a kick when I look at somebody on television and [they say], 'He's lost 13 people.'”

“A kick.” He said that. Ahead of Memorial Day. FFS.

Zeteo’s Martin Pengelly contributed to this newsletter.

item.title: Jeff Bezos, Trump Toady
item.description: On this day in 1956, over Bikini Atoll in the Pacific Ocean, the U.S. conducted the first airborne test of an improved hydrogen bomb. Four miles across, the fireball was brighter than 500 suns. By 1963, open-air and undersea nuclear tests were banned. Good morning, friends. Mehdi here. I went on ‘…
item.author: First Draft | Zeteo
item.content:encoded:
On this day in 1956, over Bikini Atoll in the Pacific Ocean, the U.S. conducted the first airborne test of an improved hydrogen bomb. Four miles across, the fireball was brighter than 500 suns. By 1963, open-air and undersea nuclear tests were banned.

Good morning, friends. Mehdi here. I went on ‘Piers Morgan Uncensored’ yesterday and, painfully, Piers ended our interview insisting I - a Liverpool fan - congratulate him - an Arsenal fan - for his team winning the Premier League after 22 years. I reluctantly did so. Sorry.

Anyways, in today’s ‘First Draft,’ Jeff Bezos reminds us that he has no principles and no shame; Democrats mount a fightback against Donald Trump’s most crazily corrupt act ever; and Benjamin Netanyahu wants us to believe that Itamar Ben-Gvir doesn’t represent Israel or its values (LOL!).

Here we go…

Do you remember what Donald Trump used to call Amazon founder and Washington Post owner Jeff Bezos during his first term as President? I do.

“Jeff Bozo.”

Second-term Trump and the fourth-richest man in the world now get along much better than they did before. Because Bezos, like so many other billionaire tech moguls, has gone from loudly criticizing the president to shamelessly capitulating to him.

On Wednesday, in an interview with CNBC’s Andrew Ross Sorkin, Bezos fawned over the president, saying “Trump has lots of good ideas” and “you have to give him credit where credit is due.”

That is, of course, billionaire-speak for “He cut my taxes!” - which Trump definitely did. According to one study, since the 2017 Trump tax cuts, “Amazon founder Jeff Bezos has pocketed $36.6 billion in capital gains from selling shares of his company stock” and “owed $6.2 billion less in federal taxes on these gains.” According to another study, Amazon stands to gain $15 billion from Trump’s One Big Beautiful Bill Act (OBBBA) last year.

Billionaires can never have enough billions, so Bezos is happy to humiliate himself by heaping praise on a man-child president who obviously deserves none. Consider this line about Trump from Bezos that jumped out to me from his interview with Ross Sorkin.

“I think he is a more mature, more disciplined version of himself than he was in his first term.”

Sorry, what? WHAAAAAAAATTTTT?

The president who spent three hours in the middle of the night the other week posting 50 times on social media, a mix of insane conspiracy theories and unhinged threats, including references to Barack Obama as a “demonic force” and a “traitor”?

The president who posted an image depicting himself as… Jesus Christ?

The president who posted an AI-generated video of himself in a fighter jet dropping excrement on American citizens protesting against his presidency?

The president who took to social media to warn that “a whole civilization will die tonight, never to be brought back again”? Who has repeatedly threatened Iran with a nuclear holocaust?

That’s the guy who we’re being told to believe is the “more mature, more disciplined” commander-in-chief this time around? Really, Jeff? REALLY?

Do these billionaires have no shame? No self-respect? No spines? And do they not understand that the history books will judge them for having been enablers of a deranged fascist?

  • Netanyahu angry:Benjamin Netanyahu’s “hair was on fire” after a tense call with Donald Trump over a peace proposal drafted by Pakistan and Qatar, Axios (surprise!)quoteda source as saying. The site added that the Israeli prime minister wants more strikes on Iran.
  • Trump unsure:The president said Netanyahu would do as he was told, butequivocatedon talks, saying: “The only question is, do we go and finish it up, or are they gonna be signing a document. Let’s see what happens.”
  • Boarding party:In the Gulf of Oman, U.S. forcesboarded, redirected, and released a tanker flying the Iranian flag.
  • Lebanon latest:Eight people werekilledin strikes on southern Lebanon as Israel continued to ignore the supposed ceasefire. Israel haskilledmore than 3,073 people in Lebanon since the latest escalation began, according to Lebanese officials.

On Wednesday, Democrats and others attempted to fight back against Donald Trump’s latest corrupt outrage: a $1.776 billion slush fund to reward allies he claims were the victims of weaponized government under Joe Biden, and the attendant DOJ declaration that Trump family taxes are off limits for government probes.

Writing to acting Attorney General Todd Blanche, Treasury Secretary Scott Bessant, and Internal Revenue Service CEO Frank Bisignano, House ranking members Rep. Jamie Raskin (Oversight) and Rep. Richard Neal (Ways & Means) lamented “one of the most brazen acts of public corruption and self-dealing in American history,” and demanded documents and answers before Bessant testifies at Ways & Means in June.

Alas, Republicans control those committees. But Raskin also introduced a bill to stop the use of federal cash for payouts. Rep. Mark Pocan’s gambit was a bill to tax payouts at 100%, “so all taxpayer funding goes back to the Treasury rather than into the hands of Trump allies.”

Republicans were not entirely silent. Amid rumblings in the Senate, Rep. Brian Fitzpatrick, a vulnerable Pennsylvanian, wrote to Blanche to express “urgent concern” about “dangerous backsliding in the transparency of our institutions and our commitment to the American taxpayer.” To reporters, he was more direct. “We’re gonna try to kill” the fund, he said. Other Republicans are unhappy, too.

January 6 offenders are likely to be prominent beneficiaries should Trump’s slush fund pay out. In federal court on Wednesday, Harry Dunn and Matthew Hodges, cops on duty at the Capitol that day, filed suit, seeking to have the fund declared illegal.

  • Albanese win:The U.S.removedUN special rapporteur Francesca Albanese from a sanctions list a week after a judge temporarily blocked enforcement of the sanctions, saying they likely violated her free speech.
  • Ballroom bust:Senator John KennedysaidRepublicans had given up on finding a way to pass $1 billion in taxpayer funds for Trump’s White House ballroom.
  • Cop chiller:Askedif he would be okay with people convicted of hurting police – Jan. 6 rioters, say – getting payouts from Trump’s “Anti-Weaponization Fund,” acting AG Todd Blanche said: “Just to be clear, people that hurt police get money all the time.” So that’s a yes, then.
  • Rest in peace:Former Massachusetts Democratic Rep. Barney Frankdiedat 86. In office for 32 years and one of the first openly gay members of Congress, Frank put his name to historic financial reforms and often clashed with the left of his party.
  • Maine chance:A Pan-Atlantic Research pollputprogressive Graham Platner seven points up on Republican incumbent Susan Collins in the race for U.S. Senate, 48%-41%. Platner also had a 21-point lead on favorability, +3 to -18.
  • Freed speech:A Tennessee man who spent 37 days in jail for posting a meme critical of Trump after the death of Charlie Kirk willcollect$835,000, after agreeing to drop a civil rights suit against the county sheriff and other officials.

Which Republican led calls for Barney Frank to be expelled from Congress over a 1980s relationship with an escort, only to end up retiring in 2009, after pleading guilty to disorderly conduct in an airport men’s room?

Keep your eyes out for the answer below!

  • 🇵🇸 Don’t forget Gaza:At least four Palestinians werekilledin Israeli attacks on Gaza today, bringing the death toll from Israel’s violations of the so-called ceasefire to more than 880 people.
  • 🇵🇸 Deranged minister:Yesterday, ultranationalist Israeli National Security Minister Itamar Ben-Gvirpostedavideoof himself mocking members of the international aid flotilla abducted in international waters earlier this week, as they knelt with hands tied and heads pressed into the ground. Numerous countriesprotested. Preposterously, Benjamin Netanyahu – who appointed Ben-Gvir and has refused to fire him – said Ben-Gvir’s behavior was “not in line with Israel’s values and norms.” Today, Israel began deporting the flotilla participants it had illegally detained.
  • 🇹🇼 Arms plea:Speaking a week after Chinese President Xi Jinping warned Donald Trump that the U.S. and China could collide over Taiwan, Taiwanese President Lai Ching-tesaidhe wanted to buy weapons from the U.S.

  • 🇨🇺

    Pressure builds:The U.S.indictedRaúl Castro, 94, over the 1996 downing of two planes flown by Cuban exiles. The former president and five other men are charged with conspiracy to kill U.S. nationals, destruction of aircraft, and murder, amid messaging apparently meant topave the way for military action.

  • 🇨🇩

    Ebola update:ISIS-linked militantskilled at least 17in Ituri province, a hotspot of the rare form of Ebola that has killedmore than 130in DR Congo.


Democratic ad makers are certainly spoiled for choice these days. What to pick from this inane word salad about the Iran war? Perhaps this breathtaking claim, in reference to US military casualties: “I get a kick when I look at somebody on television and [they say], 'He's lost 13 people.'”

“A kick.” He said that. Ahead of Memorial Day. FFS.

item.title: Is Bari Weiss Getting Demoted?
item.description: Good evening, and welcome to this week’s ‘Ragebait.’ Justin Baragona here, bringing you the latest media dispatches from the right-wing industrial outrage complex and Bari World. In tonight’s edition, speculation that Bari Weiss’s role will be scaled back has reached a fever pitch. So, who could…
item.author: Zeteo
item.content:encoded:
Good evening, and welcome to this week’s ‘Ragebait.’

Justin Baragona here, bringing you the latest media dispatches from the right-wing industrial outrage complex and Bari World. In tonight’s edition, speculation that Bari Weiss’s role will be scaled back has reached a fever pitch. So, who could lead a combined CNN-CBS if not Bari? Also, Fox’s coverage of the San Diego mosque shooting sparks internal criticism, and Jeff Bezos has thoughts on ‘Melania.’

In just seven short months at the helm of CBS News, Bari Weiss is already hearing the rumors rumbling in the background that she could see her role significantly scaled back just as Paramount is readying itself to add CNN to the mix.

“I’m guessing she has one or two fuck-ups left before something like this happens,” a CBS News senior staffer told me, referencing Weiss’s recent spate of embarrassing blunders. “It would not be shocking considering the bad press and freefall she’s responsible for,” another network reporter added.

Earlier this week, Puck’s Dylan Byers reported that there have been informal conversations among Paramount’s leadership team about shifting Weiss’s mandate and bringing in an experienced broadcast news executive who would oversee the linear TV operations of the combined CBS and CNN.

At the same time, Byers was careful to note that Weiss retains the support of Paramount chief David Ellison, and it isn’t clear how involved he’s been in these discussions to reshape Weiss’s job duties as the merger with Warner Bros. Discovery grows ever closer. And a Paramount spokesperson insisted that the 42-year-old heterodox opinion writer remained in Ellison’s good graces.

“Bari has the full support of Paramount and David Ellison as the editorial leader overseeing CBS News and ‘60 Minutes.’ Reports suggesting otherwise are inaccurate,” the spokesman stated.

We reached out to CBS News and Paramount for additional comment, and did not get a response.

Still, even if Weiss is carrying out the conservative-friendly editorial direction that Ellison has envisioned for CBS, which also allows the neophyte media mogul to appease Donald Trump as he seeks regulatory approval for his growing studio empire, the constant flood of negative headlines under her leadership – not to mention the plummeting ratings and logistical screwups – may finally be reaching a boiling point.

According to Byers, the broad strokes of Paramount’s plan would be that Weiss would give up day-to-day supervision of CBS News programming – namely ‘60 Minutes,’ ‘CBS Evening News’ and ‘CBS Mornings’ – and shift her focus to growing the news division’s digital footprint while maintaining editorial influence over CBS and CNN.

“I’ve heard from more than a few TV exec sources that Paramount will not put her in charge of CNN,” a media industry expert told me. “Only David has her back, the rest of Paramount thinks she’s a fuck-up. And if they want a combined CBS and CNN operation, the execs want to find a bona fide TV visionary to run it all.”

As for who would step into the linear TV role, it appears unlikely that CBS News president Tom Cibrowski would be given the mandate of overseeing both CNN and CBS, especially considering that Weiss’s struggles have occurred amid their powershare arrangement. It is also unclear if current CNN chief Mark Thompson would see his role expanded, though Ellison has reportedly met with him recently.

Instead, the names that continue to be floated around inside both Paramount and Warner are former CBS News president David Rhodes, who was in talks to return to CBS following Ellison’s acquisition of Paramount last year, and ex-CNN head honcho Jeff Zucker.

Zucker, who resigned from CNN in 2022 for not disclosing a consensual relationship with a colleague, is still extremely well-liked by CNN veterans but is seen as a long shot entirely because it would incense Trump. “I don’t think Jeff Zucker is going to do it because Trump hates him,” a Paramount insider told me.

Rhodes, however, appears to be the one high-profile executive whom many industry sources agree fits the bill. Prior to his eight-year run atop CBS News – where he became the youngest network news president in U.S. history – Rhodes spent more than a decade at Fox, eventually rising to vice president of news. He is currently the executive chairman of Sky News Group after briefly returning to the Murdoch media fold.

“Rhodes could work. He’s also from Sky, and his name was floated before Bari as well. That’s what everyone’s kind of thinking about and talking about,” the Paramount insider said. “Rhodes could make sense,” the media industry expert noted.

Adding that “I don’t think Bari is going away,” the insider speculated that Weiss – who had no broadcast news experience before Ellison hired her – is likely “still going to push editorial direction and push guests, and she’s basically going to be a glorified booker.”

“It’s just clear from the top down that she doesn’t know what she’s doing on the management side and on the TV side, but they want to keep her to make sure that stories fall in the right lane,” they continued. “I don’t think she’s fully going away.”

At the same time, especially with Anderson Cooper saying goodbye to ‘60 Minutes’ by not-so-subtly urging Weiss not to meddle with the venerable newsmagazine, the insider predicted that The Free Press founder would be less involved with CBS News’ flagship programming.

“But I do think that she won’t have a hand in the day-to-day of ‘60’ and the day-to-day ‘Evening News,’” they suggested, adding that this “isn’t good” for anchor Tony Dokoupil and executive producer Kim Harvey. During Dokoupil’s brief tenure as host, the nightly news program has seen its viewership sink to all-time lows and has been assailed by critics.

“I think all that is going to get merged with CNN somehow,” the insider concluded. “David Rhodes is going to figure out how to map this all out.”

It would be shocking if it weren’t all so predictable.

In the immediate aftermath of the horrific shooting at the Islamic Center of San Diego, which saw three men killed and the two suspected teenage shooters die of self-inflicted gunshots, analysts, hosts, and guests on Fox speculated that the hate-filled attack could have been carried out by Iran while ludicrously suggesting the “radical” mosque brought it on itself.

While also not turning to any Muslim voices to speak about the tragedy, the conservative cable giant did discuss the matter with a panel that consisted of a conservative Jewish plastic surgeon and an Orthodox rabbi – both of whom have been extremely outspoken in their criticism of Islam.

Meanwhile, after it became apparent that the suspected shooters ascribed to neo-Nazi and racist ideology, Fox seemingly became increasingly uninterested in covering the story on air – with the network’s primetime programming completely ignoring it by Tuesday night.

To be fair, Tuesday also saw primary elections in several states, which took up much of cable news’s focus that evening. Meanwhile, Fox’s digital side has continued to extensively cover developments surrounding the shooting.

But the on-air coverage there has been is troubling to say the least.

With police on the scene in San Diego, Paul Mauro – one of Fox’s go-to legal analysts – wondered if this was a sectarian Muslim terror attack.

“It is a Sunni mosque location. Why do I bring that up? Because Iran is a Shia nation,” Mauro declared on Monday afternoon. “And some of this stuff we’ve seen lately, relative to Iran – like, for instance, Hezbollah-linked attacks that were planned to go off here in America – that has a Shia footprint.”

The following hour, Fox host Will Cain ran with Mauro’s assertion during a conversation with two other law enforcement experts, adding that the “details are interesting” while asking if “we have that kind of sectarian conflict here in the United States.”

“I thought it was almost laughable and disgusting that they’re trying to attach Shia and Sunni conflict in other countries to where Shia and Sunni Muslims live side by side without conflict,” a Fox News on-air personality told me.

Not to leave the speculation at just Muslim-on-Muslim terror, Cain also questioned whether the attackers were immigrants.

“The first question that popped into my mind was, ‘What is he doing in the United States?’ Is he a permanent resident? Illegal alien?’ It’s one of the first things I want to know,” Cain asked his guests. “Is this an American, a radicalized American?”

While the network’s “hard news” shows – such as ‘Special Report’ – aired straight matter-of-fact dispatches from on-the-scene reporters on Monday and Tuesday, other Fox programs either sidestepped the story or began looking for ways to lay the blame on the mosque itself.

Sean Hannity, the president’s shadow chief of staff, actually had both FBI Director Kash Patel and Secretary of Homeland Security Markwayne Mullin on his show Monday evening – and only asked Mullin one brief question about the shooting. That’s right, he didn’t even bother to broach the subject with the head of federal law enforcement.

It was the Monday night broadcast of ‘Fox News @ Night’ – the show also covered the shooting on Tuesday – that really rose to the level of self-parody. At the end of a panel discussion on the Iran war, anchor Trace Gallagher turned to his two guests – Rabbi Chaim Mentz and Dr. Sheila Nazarian, a reality TV star and plastic surgeon who describes herself as a conservative Iranian Jew – to weigh in on the shooting.

Mentz, a frequent Fox guest who has called Islam a “conquering religion” and accused Muslim Americans of having an “agenda,” appeared to look for other motivations on the shooters’ part outside of Islamophobia and bigotry.

“I’ll put money on it that there is some mental illness going on over there,” Mentz stated. “I don’t think that these two kids, regular American kids, are going to say, ‘Let’s go shoot up a mosque.’”

Nazarian, another regular presence on Fox airwaves, said that while she wanted to “say prayers for everyone” at the Islamic center, she made a point of describing it as “very controversial” because “two of the 9/11 attackers attended this mosque.” (Earlier in the evening, Laura Ingraham also brought up “that 9/11 connection” to the center.)

“It’s horrific to point out that something that happened 25 years ago, how that would be relevant today, especially when we now know that it was a white guy and a Hispanic kid who had anti-Islam and racial pride, according to authorities, written on things that were found in the car with regards to their weaponry,” the on-air personality told me.

Fox News did not respond to a request for comment.

“Yes, the ‘Melania’ thing is a falsehood that will not die,” megabillionaire Jeff Bezos proclaimed during a rare televised interview on Wednesday, insisting the film wasn’t meant to “placate the president,” before adding: “By the way, it appears it was a good business decision. It did very well in theaters.”
Yes, Jeff. A film that cost your company $75 million to purchase and market, yet made only $16.3 million at the box office, was a “good business decision” that “did very well in theaters.”

Watch out for my newsletter, ‘Ragebait,’ every week. I focus on the right-wing outrage-industrial complex and the intersection of our political and media landscapes. Send any tips on Signal (justinbaragona.85).

Check out more from Zeteo:

If you enjoy Zeteo, share it with your friends and earn rewards when they subscribe.

item.title: The 9 Most Insane Things About Trump's $1.8 Billion Slush Fund
item.description: It is difficult to put into words the staggering illegality of the so-called “settlement” of Donald Trump’s $10 billion lawsuit against the Internal Revenue Service and Treasury Department. In a normal world, it would be the stuff of a B-list Hollywood movie – except that it’s so clownishly…
item.author: Zeteo
item.content:encoded:
It is difficult to put into words the staggering illegality of the so-called “settlement” of Donald Trump’s $10 billion lawsuit against the Internal Revenue Service and Treasury Department. In a normal world, it would be the stuff of a B-list Hollywood movie – except that it’s so clownishly unbelievable that nobody would be dumb enough to try and pitch it.

Except that it’s real life. Happening now.

On Monday, acting Attorney General Todd Blanche made public the purported “Settlement Agreement” in the case filed on Jan. 29 in the U.S. District Court of the Southern District of Florida by Donald J. Trump, his sons Don Jr. and Eric, and the Trump Organization against the IRS and Treasury Department, both of which constitutionally fall within Trump’s chain of command.

The “agreement” purports to transfer $1,776,000,000 of taxpayer money “to an account for the sole use by the Anti-Weaponization Fund,” which Blanche is “directed” to establish, to hear claims by individuals and “entities” who say they were unfairly targeted by the government – a pretext that doesn’t even bother to tether itself to the lawsuit it pretends to “settle.”

As if the corruption wasn’t already obvious, things got worse on Tuesday when the DOJ announced that the IRS is “FOREVER BARRED” and “PRECLUDED from prosecuting or pursuing” pending tax claims against Trump, his sons, or the Trump Organization.

Here are nine things to understand about this insanity:

Trump was both the named plaintiff in the case and effectively the defendant, because as president of the United States, he is the head of the executive branch of which the IRS and Treasury Department are a part. The lawsuit claimed that Trump, his sons, and his business were entitled to $10 billion from the federal government because a former federal contractor for the IRS allegedly disclosed the plaintiffs’ tax returns to the press from 2019 to 2020.

The judge immediately noticed that the parties did not seem “sufficiently adverse to each other,” which is a requirement for there to be a judicial controversy under Article III of the U.S. Constitution, even noting that Trump had told reporters that “it’s very interesting” to be on both sides of the case. The case, in other words, looked a lot like legal garbage, but that doesn’t seem to give pause to lawyers in Trump’s orbit anymore.

Trump, his sons, and his organization moved to dismiss the case before the grift was announced, essentially cutting the judicial branch out of any oversight role. Judge Kathleen Sullivan condemned the maneuver in her dismissal order:

“Defendants—federal agencies represented by the Department of Justice, which has an independent obligation to uphold the ‘public’s strong interest in knowing about the conduct of its Government and expenditure of its resources’ and the ‘fair administration of justice,’ — neither submitted any settlement documents nor filed any documents ensuring that settlement was appropriate where there was an outstanding question as to whether an actual case or controversy existed.”

In what looked like a panic response to a Trumpian fit, Blanche added a one-pager to the “agreement” on Tuesday to ban the federal government from enforcing federal laws against Trump, his sons, his companies, and “affiliated entities” regarding “any matters” that “could be pending” (specifically including their tax returns) before any federal agency. Not only does this suggest that the family has things to hide (cue Trump’s over 3,700 2026 stock trades involving high-profile companies that have dealings with his administration), but it’s an attempt to extend immunity beyond the Oval Office – as well as to cover the egregiously finite spectrum of his own “unofficial” activity that the Supreme Court left open for possible prosecution in its disastrous ruling in Trump v. U.S.

The addendum is probably unenforceable against a future administration that might properly seek to hold this president and his affiliates accountable for violations of federal law. Still, the brazenness of Trump’s ability to literally do whatever he wants with zero legal consequences is staggering.

As Judge Sullivan explained, the attorney general “has a statutory obligation to defend the IRS when it is hailed into court.” With this so-called “settlement,” he has violated his oath of office and should one day be held legally accountable. Given the powers he yields over the approximately 116,000 people within DOJ, which is tasked with enforcing the federal criminal laws against everyone other than Trump and, now, his family, Blanche’s shocking behavior cannot go unnoticed.

Where is the money coming from? Congress appropriates spending under Article I of the Constitution, and has not authorized this “Fund.” Under the Congressional Budget and Impoundment Control Act of 1974, as well, presidents cannot usurp spending laws enacted by Congress or grab money for their own pet projects.

Blanche apparently plans to raid the federal “Judgment Fund” reserved for legitimate resolution of legitimate cases against the government. That would be legally unfounded. The “Anti-Weaponization” cash is vaguely earmarked for future, unnamed “claimants,” and unlike serious lawsuits resolved against the government with money judgments adjudicated through courts of law, there is no valid law backing any of this. This is simply taking the money – not executing valid judgments.

The laws, however, are only so good as they are enforced. Nobody is doing that with Trump, especially Congress. Donald Trump is now the law. The Trump-friendly Supreme Court majority effectively endorsed suspension of the Constitution for a man indicted for participating in a violent riot to illegally take power on January 6, 2021. After Monday’s announcement of this “settlement,” the world cannot legitimately continue to pretend to call the U.S. a functioning democracy.

There are many different federal and state laws that ban stealing. At a very basic level, the crime of theft is the taking of another person’s property with the intent to deprive them of the use of it. Trump has no legal authority to take the nearly $1.8 billion. The “agreement” makes clear that he plans to do whatever he wants with it, with zero transparency or accountability (more on that below), though Trump claims to “know very little about” this scheme.

Federal and state law also bans criminal conspiracy, which is an agreement between two or more people to commit an unlawful or fraudulent act – arguably the intentional taking of $1.8 billion in hard-earned taxpayer dollars from the federal Treasury with no legal authority. The intention is evidently to use the money for something other than the good of the American public.

Although the Supreme Court majority granted Trump immunity from prosecution for his commission of crimes while president, Blanche and the other acolytes ensnared in this scam are not covered by presidential immunity. That includes Associate Attorney General Stanley E. Woodward, Jr., and Chief Executive Officer of the IRS Frank J. Bisignano, who signed on behalf of the United States agencies as the “defendants,” as well as the two attorneys who signed for the Trumps as the “plaintiffs.” Trump could try to pardon them, of course, but that just underscores the obscene lawlessness of the overall maneuver.

Also on Monday, Blanche signed what the “agreement” called an “accompanying order” stating that “[o]nce the funds are deposited into the Designate Account, the United States has no liability whatsoever for the protection or safeguarding of those funds.” The “Fund” consists of five members appointed by Blanche (only three are required for a quorum), but they can be “removed by the President . . . without cause.”

The fund can dole out money for any “factors” it “deems just and appropriate” and “may make” the procedures it employs for deciding who gets money transparent if it wants to – or not. The fund can “receive and request evidence or other support for claims,” whatever that means.

The agreement is “enforceable” and “challengeable” only by Trump, his sons, the Trump Organization, or the government defendants that Trump also controls. There “shall be no appeal, arbitration, or judicial review” of what the fund decides, although Trump’s DOJ “or a third-party contractor” can audit it. Only the Trump plaintiffs and the Trump defendants are authorized to modify the “agreement.” The fund is supposed to provide Blanche with quarterly reports (he can direct “otherwise” if he wants to), but those are to remain “confidential,” along with the names and addresses of any claimants that receive “any relief.”

The “agreement” is being widely billed as a nod to the more than 1,500 Jan. 6 defendants, many violent, whom Trump pardoned when he took office. But “Claimants can include entities,” meaning corporations. All that is required to theoretically qualify is a gripe about (a) “the unlawful raid on Mar-a-Lago, in Palm Beach, Florida, in August of 2022;” (b) “the Russia-collusion hoax”; or (c) “the sustained use of the levers of government power by Democrat elected officials . . . to target individuals, groups and entities for improper and unlawful political, personal, and/or ideological reasons (‘Lawfare’ and ‘Weaponization’)” – such as the Biden Administration’s enforcement of the FACE Act prohibiting the interference with abortion clinics, its “wrongful labeling of certain parents as domestic terrorists, and the IRS’s targeting of groups based on improper ideological criteria.”

In other words, Trump can use the fund to vindicate whatever is bothering him in any given moment on any given day.

With this news, the Jan6ers might feel especially emboldened to continue to commit crimes so long as they do it for Trump. The terms of the “agreement” leave open the possibility that they will ultimately get nothing, however. “Just” compensation is obviously not the point.

There’s a big election coming in November – one that could determine the fate of the Republican Party. It could also lay the groundwork for a Democratic president – someone with a spine of steel and an attorney general willing to prosecute crimes being committed in and around the Oval Office right now – to be elected one day. Given that theoretical possibility, November could also wind up deciding the personal fates of Trump and his enablers.

After all, the criminal laws remain on the books in America. Even if nobody – at least right now – seems willing to enforce them.

Kimberly Wehle, Zeteo’s legal contributor and author of ‘ Constitution in Crisis,’ is a constitutional law professor at the University of Baltimore School of Law.

Check out more from Zeteo:

If you enjoy Zeteo, share it with your friends and earn rewards when they subscribe.

item.title: Trump and AIPAC Take Out Thomas Massie
item.description: On this day in 1996, the U.S. Supreme Court ruled in Romer v. Evans that states could not deny LGBTQ+ citizens protection against discrimination – a milestone in the fight for equal rights some now see at risk from Donald Trump’s extremist Court. Good morning, friends. Prem here. Wishing you a…
item.author: First Draft | Zeteo
item.content:encoded:
On this day in 1996, the U.S. Supreme Court ruled in Romer v. Evans that states could not deny LGBTQ+ citizens protection against discrimination – a milestone in the fight for equal rights some now see at risk from Donald Trump’s extremist Court.

Good morning, friends. Prem here. Wishing you a wonderful Wednesday wherever you are. Some nice DC news is that one of the most beloved fountains in Malcolm X Park is back on after years out of order, thanks in part to Donald Trump’s insistence on “beautifying” the capital for the nation’s 250th birthday. A source tells me, however, of an odd “odor” wafting around the fountains. Yet another thing that smells fishy in Trump’s America…

In today’s ‘First Draft,’ Trump and pro-Israel interests spend big to stamp out a Republican maverick; an extraordinary report details the U.S. and Israel’s original plan for regime change in Tehran; and Trump’s financial corruption reaches unbelievable heights. Let’s dig in.

Nearly $33 million. That’s how much was thrown into one Republican House primary in Kentucky. The most expensive such primary ever. And it was enough to beat Thomas Massie.

Millions were dropped by outside groups and Trump allies seeking to boost Massie’s challenger, Ed Gallrein. It paid off, Gallrein winning 55% to 45%.

Massie told supporters he had called his opponent to concede. Though he couldn’t resist a crack at Gallrein, he said he was committed to “basic decency” in politics, adding: “We’ve been honorable the whole time and we’re going to stay that way.”

The spending in Kentucky’s 4th Congressional District surpassed even that seen in two vastly expensive House primaries in 2024, successful challenges against then Democratic Reps. Jamaal Bowman in New York and Cori Bush in Missouri. Bowman and Bush had something in common beyond progressive politics: they were leading critics of the Israeli government and advocates for Palestinian rights. Yes, your guess is correct: many of the same groups and individuals who spent against Bowman and Bush spent to get rid of Massie, AIPAC prominent among them.

It should be said that Massie is no one’s idea of a progressive, hidden or otherwise. Hardline positions include abortion (anti), guns (pro), and birthright citizenship for the children of undocumented migrants (you guessed it, anti).

Nonetheless, his breaks from Trump created headlines, and not just thanks to colorful quotes like this one, when he told a minion of pro-Trump troll Laura Loomer: “I thought you knew. I vote with Republicans 91% of the time, and the 9% I don’t, they’re taking up for pedophiles, starting another war, or bankrupting our country.”

That line indicated how, for a MAGA movement under pressure created by its own unpopular positions, Massie went from slight annoyance to significant inconvenience.

Massie joined Democratic Rep. Ro Khanna in leading the charge to release the Jeffrey Epstein files to hold accountable those implicated in the crimes of the late financier and sex offender. Such efforts deeply angered close Epstein associate Donald Trump, whose administration sought to stop the files from being released.

On Tuesday, Khanna mourned Massie’s defeat, noting his success with younger voters. Massie told supporters to consider the effects of his Epstein work: “We’ve taken out two dozen CEOs, an ambassador, a prince, a prime minister, a minister of culture – and that was just six months. I’ve got seven months left in Congress.”

An anti-interventionalist, Massie has long opposed most foreign aid. In recent years, he has become a leading GOP voice against unconditional support for Israel and its violence. He’s also been enthusiastic in his opposition to Trump and Israel’s illegal and out-of-control war on Iran. He’s voted against military aid for Israel, opposed a resolution he said equated anti-Zionism with antisemitism, and has been a rare Republican criticizing the American Israel Public Affairs Committee, going so far as to tell Tucker Carlson many lawmakers have a designated “AIPAC person” guiding them on votes and policy positions.

Last week, Massie introduced a bill to require AIPAC to register under the Foreign Agents Registration Act.

And so pro-Israel interests dumped a monumental amount of cash into the effort to unseat him. Some such groups were linked to billionaires Miriam Adelson and Paul Singer. Even Pete Hegseth, Trump’s so-called “Secretary of War,” took time away from his many disastrous adventures to visit Kentucky and slam Massie while boosting his opponent.

Trump was directly involved too, actively endorsing Gallrein, a former Navy SEAL.

Gallrein was reliant on such support. His own background is murky, his public appearances sparse. The MAGA effort was less in support of Gallrein than against Massie. In a video on Monday, Trump spent most of his time attacking the incumbent. When he mentioned Gallrein, he mispronounced his name.

Ultimately, Gallrein pledged himself a loyal Trump serf. The MAGA media machine pitched him as a reliable soldier. He benefited from a gargantuan pile of cash. And it worked – another Republican who crossed Trump has been kicked to the curb.

Still, what does it say that it took a full court press – and then some – for the entire ruling party’s apparatus to unseat one man?

What does it also say that someone who voted with Trump 91% of the time proved so unacceptable to the GOP establishment? The slightest divergence is unacceptable: to the power brokers, to Trump, and to the pro-Israel interests. Voters critical of US foreign policy, or Epstein-linked corruption, will not find much for them in the Republican Party.

On Tuesday, some barbs turned ugly. CNN reported a “senior White House adviser” as saying Trump, who last week turfed out Louisiana Senator Bill Cassidy, had not mounted “a retribution campaign” but “a send a message campaign” instead.

“It’s about reminding people of the political power,” the adviser reportedly said. “It’s not about retribution. This is basic political management of a party. You have to keep everybody on the reservation. Occasionally you have to shoot a hostage. The next one is Thomas Massie.”

In turn, Massie was withering, saying “it took a while to find Ed Gallrein in Tel Aviv” before conceding, and adding that the race was “not just the most expensive. This thing went on longer than Vietnam. Why did this race get so expensive? Because they decided to buy the seat.”

Ultimately, the price was right. The question now, for Trump and his acolytes, is whether getting rid of Massie will be enough to stamp out other sources of such dissent.

  • Bill due:The Senateadvanceda measure to force Trump to end the Iran war, after Louisiana Republican Bill Cassidy, beaten in a primary by a Trump-endorsed challenger last week, switched his vote to yes. A vote on final passage will follow.
  • Regime change:In a stunningreport, theNew York Timessaid the U.S. and Israel began the war planning to free former hardline Iranian President Mahmoud Ahmadinejad from house arrest and put him back in power. But an Israeli strike meant to free him wounded him instead, and Ahmadinejad, reportedly in on the plan, has not been seen since.
  • Another threat:Trump talked up new strikes,tellingreporters: “I hope that we don’t have to do the war. But we may have to give them another big hit.” He also said he’d been “an hour away” from ordering a strike on Tuesday, andsaidIran wanted to make a deal to end the war in “two or three days, maybe Friday, Saturday, Sunday, something, maybe early next week, a limited period of time.”
  • Ad-Vance man:The VPsaidtalks were in “a pretty good spot”, though it was “not sometimes totally clear” what Iran’s “negotiating position” was. Oil pricesdropped, though one analyst told Reuters: “We are just holding our breath here.”
  • Then again:Thesaid “regional mediators and U.S. officials” saw little progress in talks, with Iran inflexible. The U.S.Wall Street Journalimposednew sanctions on an Iranian currency exchange house and what it called “front” groups for Iran.
  • Lebanon news:Four bodies were recovered after an Israeli strike on Deir Qanoun al-Nahr, the Lebanese National News Agencysaid, with women and children wounded. The death toll from Israeli attacks on Lebanon hashit at least3,042.

Back in the innocent spring of 2024, when a different way-too-old man was in the White House and that year’s presidential campaign horror show hadn’t really gotten going, ProPublica and the New York Times released startling reports.

If Donald Trump lost “a yearslong audit battle” with the Internal Revenue Service over “a dubious accounting maneuver to claim improper tax breaks from his troubled Chicago tower,” ProPublica said, he could face “a tax bill of more than $100 million.”

Alas, we know what happened next: Trump won back the White House and all hope of accountability for his life of scam, scheme, and flooding the zone with staggeringly corrupt shit was flushed down the Washington drains.

Since then, the hits have kept on coming. But even to hardened Trump watchers, it seemed Monday’s settlement of his spurious $10 billion lawsuit against the IRS – over Times reporting about his tax returns – announced a new level of utterly shameless corruption. The settlement produced a $1.776 billion “Anti-Weaponization Fund,” to be used to reward Trump supporters and allies, including those who sought to overturn the 2020 election, including violent criminals who invaded the Capitol on January 6.

The top lawyer at the Treasury resigned. On Tuesday, acting Attorney General Todd Blanche defended the graft in Congress. Senator Chris Van Hollen, the senior Democrat in the room, duly told him: “Every American can see through this illegal, corrupt, self-dealing scheme.”

Turned out, there was another quite possibly illegal, quite certainly mind-bendingly corrupt scheme just around the corner.

A DOJ document quietly posted online said Trump, his family, and his businesses were immune from all ongoing inquiries into their taxes, the federal government “FOREVER BARRED and PRECLUDED from prosecuting or pursuing” previous probes. Such as, say, the Chicago tower audit.

Daniel Werfel, an IRS commissioner under Joe Biden, told the Associated Press that Trump and his family had once again been placed above every other American citizen.

“Whether you are the president or Joe the Plumber,” Werfel said, “people expect the same tax rules and enforcement framework to apply to everybody.”

Such words seemed almost touchingly naïve. From the Supreme Court down, Republicans never tire of finding ways to place their Dear Leader above the law. Expect more astounding corruption, swiftly.

  • Rabb-le Rouser:Democratic socialist Chris Rabb pulled off adecisive victoryin Pennsylvania’s 3rd Congressional District, beating a crowded field that included Sharif Street, who was backed by Philadelphia Mayor Cherelle and New Jersey Senator Cory Booker. He is now on course to join The Squad in DC.
  • NATO no:The Trump administration will tell NATO allies it will reduce U.S. military capabilities available to assist European nations in a major crisis, Reutersscooped.
  • Tin ear:Yet again, Trump had nothing for voters struggling with the cost of living,sayingof rising gas prices caused by his illegal war, “This is peanuts. I appreciate everybody putting up with it for a little while… But I don’t even think about that. What I think about is you can’t let Iran have a nuclear weapon.”
  • Droning on:Speakingat his White House ballroom site, Trump boasted about how secure the building will be. Aides did not explain what he meant when he said the ballroom will have “massive drone capacity” and act as a “drone port.”
  • Go fund me:Trump alsosaidthe ballroom was a “gift… paid for by myself.” In Congress, GOP senators worked on how to contribute $1 billion in taxpayer funds.
  • Texas risk:Trumpendorsedscandal-ridden Texas AG Ken Paxton for Senate, over incumbent John Cornyn. A blow for the GOP establishment, it was a boost for Democrats hoping James Talarico might finally turn the state purple.
  • Shooting news:The FBIsaidtwo teens who killed three people at the Islamic Center of San Diego before killing themselves met online and shared white supremacist views. Imam Taha Hassane said guard Amin Abdullah died stopping the shooters from reaching classrooms. The other victims included Mansour Kaziha and Nader Awad.

In 2020, Texas Attorney General Ken Paxton was reported to the FBI over accusations of bribery and abusing his office. How many close Paxton aides blew the whistle on their boss?

Keep your eyes out for the answer below!

  • 🇵🇸

    Don’t forget Gaza:The U.S.saidit would impose sanctions against four people it claimed were linked to the Gaza aid flotilla, which made a third attempt to reach the besieged enclave before being violently intercepted by Israel. Flotilla organizers said 428 people from more than 40 countries were abducted and taken to an Israeli port. ReadZeteo’sstatementon the kidnapping of contributor Alex Colston, who was reporting from the flotilla.
  • 🇵🇸 Clear confession:Ultranationalist Israeli Finance Minister and settler Bezalel Smotrichsaidheorderedthe evacuation of a West Bank hamlet after learning that the International Criminal Court wanted to arrest him. The ICC did not comment.
  • 🇸🇾 Bad smell:Syrian President and former al-Qaeda leader Ahmed Al-Sharaaposteda picture of two boxes of Trump-branded “Victory” cologne and a note – a gift from the U.S. president. How is this…real life?

  • 🇨🇩

    Ebola update:WHO chief Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesussaidhe was “deeply concerned about the scale and speed of the epidemic,” as the authorities in Congo reported 134 suspected Ebola deaths and more than 500 suspected cases.


JD Vance, aka what passes for an intellectual in the Trump administration, aka the Veep who makes Selena Meyer look like LBJ, has some thoughts about No Kings protesters. Tom Paine, it turns out, he is not…

Zeteo’s Martin Pengelly contributed to this newsletter.

Still want to get Zeteo content, but not this newsletter? You can unsubscribe directly from ‘First Draft’ by going to ‘

If you enjoy Zeteo, share it with your friends and earn rewards when they subscribe.
