item.title: The 7 Most Islamophobic Things That Republicans Have Said Just This Year Alone
item.description: On Monday, two teenage suspects opened fire in San Diego’s biggest mosque, killing three people . The attack is only the latest incident in a rising tide of Islamophobia that’s swept the U.S. – from the stabbing to death of a 6-year-old boy after Oct. 7 to an assault on Representative Ilhan Omar in…
item.author: Zeteo
item.content:encoded:
On Monday, two teenage suspects opened fire in San Diego’s biggest mosque, killing three people. The attack is only the latest incident in a rising tide of Islamophobia that’s swept the U.S. – from the stabbing to death of a 6-year-old boy after Oct. 7 to an assault on Representative Ilhan Omar in January.

Republican leaders have been fueling the fire with unfailingly bigoted, inflammatory language against Muslims and their faith. GOP Twitter has raged against “Jihadi Mamdani” and ridiculously claimed the country is backsliding into a so-called “United Caliphate of America.” In a moment when houses of worship are no longer sanctuaries, some of the highest elected officials in the U.S. are working tirelessly to make sure Muslims feel unsafe wherever they turn.

Here are seven of the most outrageously Islamophobic and dangerous tweets from Republican lawmakers this year alone:

On March 10, Ogles tweeted: “On Paperwork doesn’t magically make you American. Muslims are unable to assimilate; they all have to go back.”

In March, Roy tweeted: “No more Muslims.”

In February, Gill tweeted: “Texans shouldn’t go to the mall and feel like they’re in Pakistan. Mass Islamic migration is killing the America we know and love.”

Last month, Fine wrote on social media: “Mainstream Islam is an evil death cult that glorifies murder, stoning women, and seeks to destroy Western values.”

Earlier this month, Self tweeted, “Sharia offers three choices to non-Muslims: convert, submit, or die. This totalitarian political ideology is intent on destroying our country from within and absolutely cannot be allowed to operate in America. Either assimilate to our culture or go home!”

Thanking Oklahoma lawmakers who introduced an Islamophobic (and likely unconstitutional) measure in the state legislature, Brecheen wrote on Feb. 26: “We must not be lullabied to sleep by political correctness as the European nations have been, and are now paying the price! Full adherence to the Quran is about conquering (from within) a nation and killing the “infidel,” not assimilation and tolerance.”

In March, Tuberville wrote: “Radical Islam and Sharia Law are a CANCER. We’ve seen it infiltrate and tear down Europe. We CANNOT let this poisonous DEATH CULT tear down America.”

Zeteo’s Aaliya Haque contributed to this piece.

Check out more from Zeteo:

If you enjoy Zeteo, share it with your friends and earn rewards when they subscribe.

item.title: Donald Trump’s War Crimes Game Show
item.description: Donald Trump is a game show host. Ever since 2015, he’s been telling the American voter – twice successfully – that his time hosting ‘The Apprentice’ and ‘Celebrity Apprentice’ should be seen as a qualification for the United States presidency. Once in the White House, Trump imported many aspects…
item.author: "Zeteo | U.S. Politics"
item.content:encoded:
Donald Trump is a game show host. Ever since 2015, he’s been telling the American voter – twice successfully – that his time hosting ‘The Apprentice’ and ‘Celebrity Apprentice’ should be seen as a qualification for the United States presidency.

Once in the White House, Trump imported many aspects of how he ruled over that NBC reality TV series to how he governed the U.S. This includes his casual, incredibly stupid racism, his cruelty towards those who are different and people with disabilities, and his propensity for covering for those credibly accused of sexual misconduct. After all, “handle it like you handled it on ‘The Apprentice’” is actually something he said to one of his senior White House aides during his first term.

But now, Trump has grafted his game-show-host sensibilities onto how he’s running an illegal, horrifying, grossly unpopular, and economy-shredding war on the Iranian people. We are almost a year and a half into the Trump-Vance administration, and this president and his cronies have been so consistently, luridly lawless and uniquely corrupt that it seems almost quaint to note that President Trump just committed yet another impeachable offense.

Still, it cannot possibly be stressed (or yelled about) enough: If the way Trump is waging his Middle East war – with the president openly, routinely declaring his plots to unleash carnage not on the Iranian government but on Iranian civilians, with all the Tune-In-Next-Week-style teasing and banal melodrama of episodic reality television – is not an impeachable offense, then nothing is.

And these are not idle threats. “Donald The Dove” is executing and plotting all of these crimes and killings with the backing of nearly every single senior appointee, administration attorney, and Cabinet member whose actual job it is to tell the president: Sir, that would be completely illegal. Therefore, instead of acknowledging any standard guardrails, the president of the United States is planning sprees of murderous war crimes by his so-called “Department of War” like it’s a fucking game show, with

“I have been asked by the Emir of Qatar, Tamim bin Hamad Al Thani, the Crown Prince of Saudi Arabia, Mohammed bin Salman Al Saud, and the President of the United Arab Emirates, Mohamed bin Zayed Al Nahyan, to hold off on our planned Military attack of the Islamic Republic of Iran, which was scheduled for tomorrow, in that serious negotiations are now taking place,” Trump posted to social media Monday afternoon. “Based on my respect for the above mentioned Leaders, I have instructed Secretary of War, Pete Hegseth, The Chairman of The Joint Chiefs of Staff, General Daniel Caine, and The United States Military, that we will NOT be doing the scheduled attack of Iran tomorrow, but have further instructed them to be prepared to go forward with a full, large scale assault of Iran, on a moment’s notice, in the event that an acceptable Deal is not reached.”

Zeteo has been reporting since last week on Trump’s recent secret orders to the Pentagon to accelerate planning for a large-scale re-escalation of the Iran war, coinciding with his public admissions that the supposed ceasefire is on “life support” at best. At the time, our reporting showed that senior administration officials were planning to ramp the war back up within days.

In his post Monday, the president appeared to confirm that was indeed the case. He claimed, however, he was (once again) pumping the brakes on his planned orgy of death and destruction, while also leaving the door ajar for launching a new “assault of Iran” soon and “on a moment’s notice.”

When Zeteo has spoken with officials in the White House, Pentagon, and elsewhere in the vast federal apparatus about the manner in which President Trump is impulsively and shambolically conducting his war, these officials themselves at times compare it to the kind of messy, exploitative reality TV that Trump used to host and executive-produce.

“The president keeps throwing in cliffhangers… and [plot] twists,” a U.S. official says. “It is reality TV, but with many, many lives hanging in the balance.”

It would be terrible enough if the American commander in chief were behaving this way while orchestrating a war of aggression purely against military targets. But that is explicitly not what he’s doing: Trump won’t stop threatening to commit genocidal war crimes by slaughtering scores of Iranian civilians. He previously declared that “a whole civilization will die tonight, never to be brought back again.”

The Trump administration has already shown a willingness to kill, not just talk about killing, civilians in the war on Iran, and has also hit some civilian infrastructure. But Trump’s expressed threats to carry out the mass murder of civilians are, on their own, a massive scandal for which he has yet to answer. One reason for that is because it’s not just Trump mouthing off – he has his whole government willing to coddle him and back him up, even if they think the war is crushing the Republican Party’s hopes and dreams ahead of this year’s midterm elections.

Two sources with knowledge of the matter described to Zeteo the menu of options that Trump’s lieutenants and military planners had prepared for him, in the run-up to his apparently canceled planned “large scale assault.”

The presented options for a new bombing campaign, according to the sources, were heavy on civilian targets, including but certainly not limited to critical energy infrastructure in Iran. Recently, the president has personally pre-approved striking several of those major civilian targets, and some Department of Defense lawyers have signed off on those potential strikes as supposedly “legal,” the people add – even though numerous legal scholars and former U.S. government attorneys say that doing so constitutes a blatant war crime.

One of the two sources simply tells Zeteo that “there’s just no way this would be legal.”

This administration has an answer to that, no matter how dubious or malevolent.

“The Trump administration seems to be taking expansive interpretations developed by the Department of Defense – over the course of decades and over a number of administrations and conflicts – and then stacking them on top of one another,” Adil Haque, a Rutgers University professor specializing in the laws of armed conflict, tells Zeteo.

“When you combine all of those interpretations that have stretched the law, and then also ask people to give you the benefit of the doubt when you’re making factual assertions that outside observers find implausible, then you get a very permissive framework for targeting civilian infrastructure,” he continues. “Suddenly, any bridge in Iran, any oil facility, any electrical facility can be classified a lawful target, because if you mix these interpretations together, anything that plays a role in the adversary sustaining its war effort becomes a legitimate target.”

Haque concludes: “This might allow DoD lawyers to sleep at night, but it’s not going to persuade anyone outside the U.S. government, or any normal practitioner of international law.”

The president of the United States, for his part, is sleeping perfectly well at night, and continues to shrug off the idea of international law whenever he’s confronted about why he’s been so busy planning a game show full of extremely bloody war crimes.

“I’m not worried about it,” Trump said last month. “You know what’s a war crime? Having a nuclear weapon, allowing a sick country with demented leadership [to] have a nuclear weapon. That’s a war crime.”

item.title: Islamophobes Can't Shut Up Even as Muslims Are Murdered at a California Mosque
item.description: On this day in 2024, Iranian President Ebrahim Raisi was killed in a helicopter crash caused by bad weather. The hardliner would be replaced by the more reform-minded Masoud Pezeshkian, who on Monday said talks to end the U.S.-Israel war on Iran did “not mean surrender.” Good morning, friends. Prem…
item.author: First Draft | Zeteo
item.content:encoded:
On this day in 2024, Iranian President Ebrahim Raisi was killed in a helicopter crash caused by bad weather. The hardliner would be replaced by the more reform-minded Masoud Pezeshkian, who on Monday said talks to end the U.S.-Israel war on Iran did “not mean surrender.”

Good morning, friends. Prem here. Wishing you a wonderful Tuesday, wherever you are. Unfortunately, I write in the wake of horrific violence in San Diego, California. I share my spirit with all who may be directly affected, and to all who deserve to be able to go without fear to their houses of worship.

In today’s ‘First Draft,’ we discuss all that led to yet another terrible anti-Muslim attack. Donald Trump, meanwhile, got a near-$2 billion slush fund to dole out to his allies, while Israel illegally intercepted another aid flotilla, kidnapping hundreds, including the Irish president’s sister and Zeteo contributor Alex Colston.

Let’s dig in.

Just days after yet another red-faced, fear-mongering Republican congressional hearing on “Sharia law,” a mosque was attacked in the United States. Two teenage suspects allegedly carried out a shooting at the Islamic Center of San Diego on Monday, killing three people.

While many of the details are still being uncovered, here’s what we know about the attack so far and the apparent motivations: the suspects were found dead, of apparently self-inflicted wounds. One reportedly left a suicide note that contained writings about racial pride, CNN reported, while hate speech was reportedly written on one of three weapons taken from the home of the parents of one suspect. A gas can with an “SS” sticker (a possible reference to Hitler’s Nazi paramilitary force) was also recovered.

Unfortunately, we can become desensitized towards such violence. Violence done with firearms, in a nation with more guns than people. Violence towards Arab Americans, especially since 9/11. Violence towards Muslims, given the posture of US foreign policy for decades on end.

But this attack hits hard, not only because it is at the nexus of so many social ills, but because the fomenting of anti-Muslim hatred in recent months has been so blatant – and so normalized.

For months, Republican members of Congress have unashamedly and publicly spewed venom towards Muslims and Islam, with little press or Democratic Party pushback. Comments that would tank political careers if they were made about any other minority are barely noticed if they are made about Muslims.

“I don’t know how you make peace with [Mainstream Muslims], I think you destroy them first,” Rep. Randy Fine, of Florida, said in December.

“Muslims don’t belong in American society,” Rep. Andy Ogles, of Tennessee, said in March, adding: “Pluralism is a lie.”

A few weeks later, Rep. Chip Roy tweeted: “No more Muslims. No more criminals. No more marxists. No more corporatists. #SaveTexas.”

That’s just a tiny sampling of the many hateful and dehumanizing comments from some of the most powerful people in the nation, directed at millions of Muslim Americans. And the political press largely treats such invective as mere evidence of another polarizing day on Capitol Hill. It barely makes the headline and attracts very little outrage.

And it is not just tweets or comments. When it comes to action, Congress has played a major role in inciting suspicion and hostility toward our Muslim neighbors. Just three days before the shooting in San Diego, the House of Representatives hosted a hearing entitled: “Sharia-Free America: Why Political Islam & Sharia Law are Incompatible with the U.S. Constitution: Part II.”

Yes. Part II.

Slated for the Senate this Wednesday? “Hidden in Plain Sight: Confronting the Muslim Brotherhood Network in America.”

Those are just two of numerous hearings staged and bills offered by members of Congress over the past few years, meant to sow hate and distrust towards Muslims and Arab Americans.

Such mania has even metastasized into an entire congressional grouping: the Sharia-Free America Caucus, which has more than 60 Republican House members.

“For months, I’ve been warning about the growing threat of Islam in the United States,” Alabama Senator Tommy Tuberville said, marking the launch of the caucus. “Islam isn’t a religion, it’s a death cult.”

Monday’s shooting targeting Muslims in San Diego was just one of a long list of Islamophobic, anti-Arab incidents in the United States.

In March, the Council on American-Islamic Relations, or CAIR, issued its annual report. It counted 8,683 complaints of anti-Muslim discrimination in the U.S. in 2025, slightly up from 2024 and the highest number of complaints since the report was introduced in 1996.

The GOP-led Congress has responded by inciting yet more hatred towards Muslims, while remaining seemingly determined to focus only on antisemitism as a problem to be confronted. Congress has openly embraced the Anti-Defamation League, unhesitatingly citing its questionable data on antisemitism. In contrast, many elected officials have targeted CAIR. Some states have even declared it a terrorist group.

The GOP-led Congress has held numerous hearings focused on antisemitism, but not one focused solely on anti-Arab, anti-Muslim hate. In 2024, there was a Senate hearing concerning hate crimes of all kinds, including those against Muslims, Palestinians, and other Arabs. But even that hearing was host to a shocking episode of Islamophobia, as Louisiana Republican John Kennedy barked at the only Muslim and Arab American witness, saying she supported Hamas, Hezbollah, and Iran.

“You should hide your head in a bag,” Kennedy told Maya Berry, executive director of the Arab American Institute and co-chair of the Hate Crimes Task Force for the Leadership Conference on Civil and Human Rights.

It was a fruitless hearing, in which Republicans expressed very little concern for victims of anti-Muslim and anti-Arab hate crimes, including Wadee Alfayoumi, a 6-year-old Palestinian-American boy stabbed 26 times at his home. Now, nearly two years later, the U.S. has suffered yet another horrific attack targeting Muslims.

And the reaction from right-wing Islamophobes? Barely-disguised and shameless glee. The deranged and bigoted Laura Loomer, an adviser – lest we forget! – to the president of the United States, responded to the terror attack in California by calling the mosque “evil” and Muslims a

The victims in San Diego were not immediately named by law enforcement. But one was reportedly Amin Abdullah, the mosque’s security guard for years, who was “pivotal,” say the police, in preventing the two shooters from killing more people inside the center. “At this point, I think it’s fair to say his actions were heroic,” San Diego Police Chief Scott Wahl said. “Undoubtedly, he saved lives today.”

According to reports, in his last Facebook post, two weeks ago, Abdullah wrote: “What is success? To many people success is financial stability, good reputation, beauty, etc. As for ME! Wallahi, thumma Wallahi. It is returning back to Allah OUR creator with the same pure soul he loaned me at birth.”

Regardless of your relationship to faith, one thing is certain. Whatever pure soul we may be born with, some people will do everything they can to corrupt it. That is what hate crimes do. So it’s on each of us to stay strong. To see one another as the universes we are, to recognize how beautiful and sacred that is, and how profane it is for anyone to so confidently, ruthlessly seek to destroy it, as happened in San Diego.

After the shooting, on the other side of the country, New York Mayor Zohran Mamdani issued a statement.

“Islamophobia endangers Muslim communities across this country,” he said. “We must confront it directly and stand together against the politics of fear and division. My thoughts are with the victims, their loved ones, and the entire community grieving this devastating attack.”

  • Trump threat:The presidentsaidhe called off a strike planned for Tuesday at the Gulf states’ request, amid “serious negotiations” to end the war. But he said the U.S. was ready for “a full, large scale assault of Iran, on a moment’s notice.”
  • Tehran responds:IransaidTrump had carried out a “retreat” based on “fear.”
  • Markets react:Trump’s message caused oil prices to fall, though the price of a barrel thenrose againto settle above $108 as the Strait of Hormuz stayed closed.
  • Public opposition:A survey byThe Institute for Global Affairsfound 58% those surveyed disapproved of Trump’s handling of the war, while 79% said the war had affected their cost of living, and 62% said the war had made it harder to pay their bills.
  • Desert sweep:Iraqi forces searched western areas, Al Jazeerareported, after it was revealed that Israel established two secret bases used to attack Iran.
  • Lebanon latest:As Israeli strikes continued regardless of the ceasefire, the Lebanese Health Ministrysaidthe death toll had passed 3,000, including 211 children. More than a million people have been displaced.

Donald Trump got his $1.776 billion slush fund on Monday, as the Department of Justice announced the settlement of the president’s $10 billion lawsuit against the Internal Revenue Service, over leaked records, and the establishment of the president’s new political piggy bank.

According to the DOJ, the Anti-Weaponization Fund will be administered by five people selected by Acting Attorney General and former Trump criminal lawyer Todd Blanche, but fireable by Trump.

Those people will be volunteers, but in what only the hardest cynic might say sounds like an invitation to self-dealing, they will be able to use the fund for “per diems, administrative services, funds, facilities, staff, travel, and other support services as may be necessary to carry out the mission of the Anti-Weaponization Fund.”

That mission, according to Blanche, is to “make right the wrongs that were previously done… setting up a lawful process for victims of lawfare and weaponization to be heard and seek redress.”

Those victims, defined by the Trump administration, will undoubtedly include January 6 rioters convicted after storming Congress in support of Trump’s lie that the 2020 election was stolen. Nine deaths are linked to that riot.

The symbolic amount of money – 1776, geddit? – for this arm of Trump’s corruption-palooza will come from the Judgment Fund, created by Congress to settle lawsuits against the government. The Anti-Weaponization Fund will exist until mid-December 2028 – oddly enough, just after the next presidential election.

Speaking to reporters, Trump claimed to “know very little about” the fund, and not to have been “involved in the whole creation of it.” He also said with characteristic clumsiness that the fund was for “people that were weaponized,” which might be said to be true, what with Trump himself having weaponized his followers on January 6.

Among observers, Donald K. Sherman, president of Citizens for Responsibility and Ethics in Washington, gave his thoughts to the New York Times – the paper that enraged Trump by reporting on his leaked tax returns under the pithy headline, “Long-Concealed Records Show Trump’s Chronic Losses and Years of Tax Avoidance.”

Sherman was succinct.

“This is one of the single most corrupt acts in American history,” he said.

  • Polls plunge:ATimes-Siena surveycontainedbad news for Trump: new lows for general approval (37%) and the economy (33%), and Democrats up 11 points in the generic ballot, a six-point surge since January.
  • Corrupt AF:Journalist Judd Legumshowedhow Trump bought stock in Thermo Fisher before and on the day he toured and praised the Ohio medical supply company. The same day, he visited an Apple facility, praised it – and bought stock.
  • Ballroom balls-up:Trump isreportedlypressuring GOP leader John Thune to fire Elizabeth MacDonough, the Senate parliamentarian who ruled that $1 billion in funding for Trump’s White House ballroom cannot be passed on a party-line bill.
  • Surveillance abuse:Congress failed to release a secret court rulingshowingFBI violations of safeguards against surveillance of U.S. citizens. “The only reason … is to cover up the government’s wrongdoing and ensure that it can continue,”saidHajar Hammado of Demand Progress.
  • Bench press:The judgeoverseeingTrump’s $10 billion suit against the BBCwrotefor The Free Press, slamming aTimespieceon Israeli sex crimes against Palestinians. The right-wing site said Roy K. Altman was writing “in his individual, not judicial, capacity.”
  • Stark truth:Promising action to help struggling New Yorkers, Mayor Zohran MamdanireplacedRonald Reagan’s famous “nine terrifying words, ‘I’m from the government and I’m here to help,’” with “I worked all day and can’t feed my family.”
  • Evidence ruling:A New York judgesaida gun and notebook prosecutors say link Luigi Mangione to the murder of United Healthcare CEO Brian Thompson can be used as evidence at trial. The defense said they were improperly seized.

In 1986, Ronald Reagan famously said, “The nine most terrifying words in the English language are, ‘I’m from the government and I’m here to help.’” But he didn’t invent the saying. Which sometime Democratic presidential contender said it 10 years earlier?

Keep your eyes out for the answer below!

  • 🇵🇸 Don’t forget Gaza:Israel illegally intercepted the Gaza aid flotilla, this time 250 nautical miles off the coast of the besieged enclave, boarding boats in daylight and abducting hundreds of activists, humanitarians, and journalists on board. Alex Colston, aZeteocontributor with the flotilla,livestreamedthe arrival of Israeli vessels. The whereabouts of the abducted participants remain unknown. At least 10 boats continue to sail on.

  • 🇨🇺

    Cuba rejoinder:President Miguel Díaz-CanelsaidU.S. military action would lead to a “bloodbath,” adding: “Cuba does not represent a threat.” He spoke after an Axios report based on “shared” intelligencesaidCuba could use drones to attack U.S. ships, Guantánamo Bay, and Florida. The U.S. imposed newsanctions.

  • 🇬🇱

    No sale:Greenland said talks with Trump envoy Jeff Landry went well but stressed the territory will not be sold to or surrendered to the U.S. “We will own Greenland for all time,” Foreign Minister Mute Egede said.
  • 🇰🇪 Kenya chaos:Four peoplediedamid protests over record fuel prices.

  • 🇨🇩

    Ebola update:The Democratic Republic of the Congoopenedmore centers to treat Ebola victims as the death toll passed 130. The U.S.saidan American doctor contracted the disease.


Trump’s personal stock trading is so blatantly corrupt that it can reduce even notoriously pugnacious CNBC host Jim Cramer to a series of inarticulate noises. Literally.

Zeteo’s Martin Pengelly contributed to this newsletter.

Still want to get Zeteo content, but not this newsletter? You can unsubscribe directly from ‘First Draft’ by going to ‘

If you enjoy Zeteo, share it with your friends and earn rewards when they subscribe.

  • Polls plunge:ATimes-Siena surveycontainedbad news for Trump: new lows for general approval (37%) and the economy (33%), and Democrats up 11 points in the generic ballot, a six-point surge since January.
  • Corrupt AF:Journalist Judd Legumshowedhow Trump bought stock in Thermo Fisher before and on the day he toured and praised the Ohio medical supply company. The same day, he visited an Apple facility, praised it – and bought stock.
  • Ballroom balls-up:Trump isreportedlypressuring GOP leader John Thune to fire Elizabeth MacDonough, the Senate parliamentarian who ruled that $1 billion in funding for Trump’s White House ballroom cannot be passed on a party-line bill.
  • Surveillance abuse:Congress failed to release a secret court rulingshowingFBI violations of safeguards against surveillance of U.S. citizens. “The only reason … is to cover up the government’s wrongdoing and ensure that it can continue,”saidHajar Hammado of Demand Progress.
  • Bench press:The judgeoverseeingTrump’s $10 billion suit against the BBCwrotefor The Free Press, slamming aTimespieceon Israeli sex crimes against Palestinians. The right-wing site said Roy K. Altman was writing “in his individual, not judicial, capacity.”
  • Stark truth:Promising action to help struggling New Yorkers, Mayor Zohran MamdanireplacedRonald Reagan’s famous “nine terrifying words, ‘I’m from the government and I’m here to help,’” with “I worked all day and can’t feed my family.”
  • Evidence ruling:A New York judgesaida gun and notebook prosecutors say link Luigi Mangione to the murder of United Healthcare CEO Brian Thompson can be used as evidence at trial. The defense said they were improperly seized.

In 1986, Ronald Reagan famously said, “The nine most terrifying words in the English language are, ‘I’m from the government and I’m here to help.’” But he didn’t invent the saying. Which sometime Democratic presidential contender said it 10 years earlier?

Keep your eyes out for the answer below!

  • 🇵🇸 Don’t forget Gaza:Israel illegally intercepted the Gaza aid flotilla, this time 250 nautical miles off the coast of the besieged enclave, boarding boats in daylight and abducting hundreds of activists, humanitarians, and journalists on board. Alex Colston, aZeteocontributor with the flotilla,livestreamedthe arrival of Israeli vessels. The whereabouts of the abducted participants remain unknown. At least 10 boats continue to sail on.

  • 🇨🇺

    Cuba rejoinder:President Miguel Díaz-CanelsaidU.S. military action would lead to a “bloodbath,” adding: “Cuba does not represent a threat.” He spoke after an Axios report based on “shared” intelligencesaidCuba could use drones to attack U.S. ships, Guantánamo Bay, and Florida. The U.S. imposed newsanctions.

  • 🇬🇱

    No sale:Greenland said talks with Trump envoy Jeff Landry went well but stressed the territory will not be sold to or surrendered to the U.S. “We will own Greenland for all time,” Foreign Minister Mute Egede said.
  • 🇰🇪 Kenya chaos:Four peoplediedamid protests over record fuel prices.

  • 🇨🇩

    Ebola update:The Democratic Republic of the Congoopenedmore centers to treat Ebola victims as the death toll passed 130. The U.S.saidan American doctor contracted the disease.


Trump’s personal stock trading is so blatantly corrupt that it can reduce even notoriously pugnacious CNBC host Jim Cramer to a series of inarticulate noises. Literally.

Zeteo’s Martin Pengelly contributed to this newsletter.

Still want to get Zeteo content, but not this newsletter? You can unsubscribe directly from ‘First Draft’ by going to ‘

item.title: Scott Jennings, the Most Annoying Man on Cable News
item.description: “I don’t answer to you!” Scott Jennings , CNN’s resident defender of Donald Trump , shouted at the panelist sitting a couple of feet to his left. Angrily pointing his finger in the face of the progressive commentator, Jennings reacted dismissively after the guest countered that the CNN pundit was…
item.author: Zeteo
item.content:encoded:
“I don’t answer to you!” Scott Jennings, CNN’s resident defender of Donald Trump, shouted at the panelist sitting a couple of feet to his left.

Angrily pointing his finger in the face of the progressive commentator, Jennings reacted dismissively after the guest countered that the CNN pundit was being “aggressive” with his actions when they were merely “having a conversation.”

“You gonna ask me for my number after the show? Brother, don’t start with me,” Jennings sneered.

No, this is not from Jennings’s recent dustup with liberal pundit Adam Mockler. Instead, this occurred nearly two years ago during a heated confrontation with academic Michael Eric Dyson.

So why then was Scott Jennings so bothered by Mockler gesturing with his hands while pressing him on Iran that he responded, ‘get your fucking hand out of my face” – when Jennings had in fact pointed his finger in the face of a Black man a year and a half earlier?

Perhaps the two incidents aren’t all that surprising. Jennings has been having contentious interactions with fellow panelists for years, racking up viral clip after viral clip, and laying bare what has become increasingly apparent both publicly and behind the scenes: the rules don’t apply to Jennings.

“A lot of CNN contributors are feeling like they’re on the Scott Jennings network, and their only job is to react to whatever Scott says,” one on-air CNN personality told Zeteo shortly after the MAGA firebrand’s tirade at Mockler.


Former CNN contributor Wajahat Ali told me that “Scott is just a symptom of a problem, which is the obscene double standard of corporate media and American institutions that continue to bend the knee to bad-faith right-wing grievances.”

Indeed, Jennings was back on the air the following day, leading both current CNN staffers and former network employees to point out that if any other paid CNN personality had acted similarly on the air, they would have been suspended or outright terminated.

“He violated what used to be a pretty clear line in TV news. You’re not supposed to cuss people out like it’s an episode of ‘The Jerry Springer Show,’” ex-CNN anchor Jim Acosta, who wrote a Substack article titled “Fire Scott Jennings,” told me.

A CNN spokesperson told Zeteo in a statement, “We select contributors - including Scott Jennings - to reflect the actual clash of political views and values that are being debated in America today.” The spokesperson added, “We choose not to limit either the views or the advocates who argue for them to suit any ideological side in the debate. We believe that giving our viewers a chance to witness a frank encounter of this kind is a service both to them and to democracy.”

Jennings declined to comment.

It’s not just on air, CNN insiders say. The once-affable Kentucky gentleman has become more coarse and callous behind the scenes over the years.

Jennings’s most recent profane outburst and his treatment of staff underscore his evolution from his “aw shucks” Bush-era conservatism to WWE-style pugilism – and of a man who once was referred to as a “Reasonable Republican” who condemned Trump over the Jan. 6 insurrection to the word bomb-hurling MAGA sycophant he is today.

It’s also worth asking how much of Jennings’s Trump idolatry and “own the libs” persona is just a natural evolution of the attention economy, considering how ubiquitous he’s become online these past couple of years. Beyond that, does CNN and its bosses find his MAGA act valuable so they can appease and pander to the president? All the while, amid ongoing layoffs and the specter of CNN becoming part of the Ellison empire, Jennings has only seen his salary grow at the network and his overall media and political profile rise, culminating in his scoring his own syndicated talk radio show.

Meanwhile, questions and concerns have also arisen over his vehemently pro-Israel stance. Journalists and commentators have publicly noted in recent days that Jennings’s show airs on Salem Media, whose chief strategy officer is a registered agent for the Israeli Ministry of Foreign Affairs.

Still, even if CNN were to somehow decide to push out Jennings, he will likely be just fine. As several current and former CNN employees told me, either a Bari Weiss/David Ellison-run version of the network would scoop him back up in a few months, or he’ll just go on to bigger and better things.

“So even if CNN gets rid of him, CNN has groomed him into this heel that will launch him either to more media notoriety and success or into the halls of Congress,” Ali said. “He doesn’t need CNN anymore.”

But for now, he remains at the network.

Strap in, readers, here’s how Scott Jennings became the most annoying and “hated” man in cable news today.

Despite being just in his 40s, Jennings has been a mainstay in GOP politics for more than a quarter-century. And throughout much of that time, he was the very definition of an establishment Republican.

Fresh out of the University of Louisville, a 22-year-old Jennings served as the political director of George W. Bush’s 2000 presidential campaign in Kentucky. Two years later, he served as political and communications director for Mitch McConnell’s 2002 re-election campaign – beginning a fruitful relationship with the one-time Senate majority leader.

From there, Jennings would go on to manage Bush’s 2004 re-election campaign in New Mexico, which saw the incumbent winning the swing state after narrowly losing it four years earlier. Following Bush’s victory over John Kerry, Jennings joined the White House as an associate director in the Office of Political Affairs, eventually moving on to become special assistant to the president.

During his time in the Bush administration, Jennings found himself involved in controversies, including a Hatch Act inquiry and his role in the administration’s dismissal of several U.S. attorneys in 2006, during which he was questioned about his use of an email account registered to the Republican National Committee.

After leaving the Bush White House, Jennings returned to his political strategy work in Kentucky, helping McConnell and other Republican state lawmakers win races. He also co-founded the public relations firm RunSwitch in 2012 – a company he claims provides “senior level political, communications, public affairs, media relations, and crisis communications counsel for national and international clients.”

Though he worked as an anchor and reporter for a local Louisville radio station while still in college, his media career began in earnest in 2013, when he began penning a bi-weekly column for the Louisville Courier-Journal, which was regularly reprinted in USA Today.

During the 2016 presidential election, Jennings made it very clear – through both his columns and his political activity – that he did not want a Trump presidency.

After initially working to get Jeb Bush elected in the GOP primary, Jennings tried to whip up support among Republicans for Marco Rubio following Bush’s flameout. At the same time, he wrote that Trump is an “authoritarian” who exhibited a “willful disregard for the constitutional limits placed on our Chief Executive,” urging Republicans to reject him.

Even after Trump secured the GOP nomination, Jennings still assailed the soon-to-be president, especially after the Access Hollywood tape dropped a month before the election. “He’s a vulgar, crude, braggadocious, desperate-to-impress playboy who tends to view most everything through a sexual lens,” he wrote at the time, adding that Trump “has disrespected American prisoners of war, body-shamed a beauty queen, feuded with a Gold Star family, and tweeted at three o’clock in the morning about sex tapes.”

The first Trump presidency ushered in Jennings’s career as a paid talking head. After becoming a frequent presence on Fox during the 2016 election, Jennings joined CNN as an on-air commentator in June 2017 – and has remained with the network since.

While Jennings warmed up to the president in his first term, and was even considered for a senior role in the administration (which he reportedly turned down), he regularly couched his defense of Trump by either blasting the legacy media’s coverage or taking aim at Democrats for their anti-Trump actions.

“There will be nothing to show for it but wasted time and a diversion of the nation’s political conversation away from issues that real people care about,” Jennings said about the first impeachment of Trump in 2019.

Jennings’s support of Trump, however, appeared to dissipate entirely following the Jan. 6 Capitol riots. Hours after a MAGA mob – whipped up by Trump – attempted to stop the certification of Joe Biden’s electoral victory, Jennings wrote an op-ed for CNN calling it an “insurrection” that Trump had “caused.”

“President Donald Trump caused this insurrection with his lies and conspiracy theories about the election process being rigged against him.” Jennings declared, describing the rioters as “domestic terrorists” and shaming “any Republican who fails to condemn this shameful behavior.”

Jennings would continue to dismiss Trump as a political outcast over the next couple of years, as the Washington Post reported in its October 2024 profile of Jennings and his then-nascent MAGA shift.

Having predicted that the GOP would lose the White House if it nominated Trump while urging his fellow conservatives to rally behind Ron DeSantis as the party’s new standard-bearer, Jennings came back around on Trump after Republican voters backed the MAGA leader in the 2024 primary.

Almost as if a switch had been flipped, Jennings was all aboard the Trump Train – and he hasn’t looked back since.

The shift from folksy Bush-era milquetoast conservative to full-throated MAGA lackey immediately paid professional dividends for Jennings.

Following Trump’s electoral victory, Los Angeles Times owner Patrick Soon-Shiong – who had nixed the paper’s endorsement of Kamala Harris as part of a rightward shift – named Jennings to the Times’s editorial board in November 2024. (Jennings had been a contributing columnist for the paper since 2019.)

Months later, it was announced that Jennings – who promised “liberal tears will flow” – would host a daily radio show on the conservative Salem Media network. That show was later expanded to two hours and took over the prime midday slot that was previously occupied by slain right-wing activist Charlie Kirk.

In March of last year, he also reportedly received a “lucrative new contract” with a “substantial pay increase” from CNN, all while the network and the rest of the broadcast and cable news industry were going through crippling cuts. In fact, two months before Jennings inked his new deal, CNN laid off roughly 200 staffers.

Still, Jennings has publicly rejected the notion that his evolution on Trump was borne of opportunism, noting that he’s “been on the roller coaster” with the president throughout the years and finally came to a realization that he’s the “last best hope for the West.”

“If Donald Trump gets me to where I want to be 98% of the time, that’s about 98% more than any Democrat is going to get me,” he said in an interview last fall. “It became very clear to me how the battle lines were being drawn as we were getting ready to do our 2024 coverage.”

He’s also seen his name floated for administration positions and potential runs for office. Shortly after Trump’s election win, Jennings was on the shortlist of candidates for White House press secretary, a job that ultimately went to Karoline Leavitt. Jennings also publicly pondered running to replace McConnell in the Senate, saying he would only take the leap if Trump asked him to (Trump has since endorsed Rep. Andy Barr).

Meanwhile, amid growing complaints from viewers and staff about Jennings’s on-air behavior – which included a never-ending stream of juvenile taunts, bad-faith debate tactics, and self-aggrandizing preening for the camera – he received a ringing endorsement from the top brass at CNN.

“Scott’s like D’Artagnan, he’s got his sword out and he’s got about four Democrats against him, but he spiked them all off. That’s much more like it, I think. He’s a worthy opponent, as it were, for the Democrats in the room,” CNN CEO Mark Thompson – who also reportedly “had Scott Jennings’s back” after the pundit’s false Iran war tweet in March – told Mediaite last October. “And it makes for not just good television, but also for in some ways, a slightly deeper testing of the ideas all the way around that table.”

“I guess it really is surprising to see someone like Mark Thompson, a truly smart and thoughtful journalist type, standing by Scott,” one CNN staffer told me. “But we live in strange times.”

And even before Paramount won its bidding war with Netflix to merge with Warner Bros. Discovery, the parent company of CNN, Jennings had reportedly met with CBS News editor-in-chief Bari Weiss. He followed this up by publicly sucking up to his potential future boss once Netflix bowed out.

Well before the Mockler incident on April 30, Jennings’s lib-owning, pro-Trump pugnaciousness had essentially become a daily occurrence on CNN’s airwaves. And because he can be regularly counted on to say something outlandish and cause a scene on any given panel, he has become a huge beneficiary of the attention economy.

Mediaite, whose content draws heavily from clips of cable news talking heads behaving badly, has published at least 180 articles about Jennings over the past 12 months, based on my latest count. That includes roughly 70 pieces on the CNN commentator since the start of this year. The site even published an op-ed that anointed Jennings as “the most important figure at CNN.”

It has become so obvious that Jennings is deliberately trying to create viral moments for his social media feed and online write-ups that CNN anchor Audie Cornish called him out for it back in December 2024. “He needs a clip for the internet,” she reacted after Jennings failed to bait her into a heated back-and-forth.

The whole idea that he is merely playing a role in order to not only curry favor with Trump and his MAGA base, but also to continue to draw media attention, is something he appears to have fully admitted.

In an interview with YouTuber Adam Friedland earlier this year, Jennings said that what he does “is a mixture of showbiz and WWE” while admitting that he is a “heel,” which is pro-wrestling parlance for villain. “I’m a lovable rascal,” he added.

“You know who’s a perfect metaphor for the GOP? Scott Jennings,” ex-CNN contributor Miles Taylor, the one-time Trump official turned outspoken critic of the president, tweeted in late March. “A pundit who mocks Trump with us during commercial breaks — but fawns over Trump when the camera is rolling. Brave enough to speak out… in the green room.”

“I’m not sure he genuinely believes in a lot of what he’s having to defend now. And I think that’s just true of most MAGA,” the on-air CNN personality said. “I think Scott is a guy who believes this is his job, and it feels really good to do it, and he gets paid a lot to do it, but I don’t think he actually believes a lot of the stuff he has to say.”

Ali – who expanded on these thoughts in his own Substack video – told me that “Scott has molded himself into an inauthentic heel” and is “playing a role,” pointing to the Friedland interview to note that Jennings “admitted as such.”

Meanwhile, Julie Roginsky, a Democratic strategist who has tangled with Jennings on-air, and claims CNN “shadow-banned” her for blasting the network for employing him, agreed that the conservative pundit is playing a role for the camera – just not very well.

“I worked at Fox for many years. The reason he couldn’t hack it at Fox is that they have the real deal. He’s basically Temu Greg Gutfeld,” she told Zeteo, referencing the abrasive Fox host and self-proclaimed “King of Late Night.”

A number of people I spoke to also placed some of the blame for Jennings’s pugilistic on-air behavior on the format of anchor Abby Phillip’s primetime CNN show, which has shifted in recent years from a standard news program to a no-holds-barred roundtable “food fight” – and seems to have a regular seat for Jennings to the left of Phillip.

“She’s setting a tone that allows for that, not just of Scott,” the on-air CNN personality stated, adding that “legitimate contributors started saying we’re not going on this show” because he’s just looking to make “moments for clicks and stuff.”

Now, if Jennings were just mugging for the cameras and engaging in kayfabe, that would be one thing. However, profanely berating another on-air guest in a threatening manner – as he did with Mockler – would still normally result in some form of punishment. And the fact that Jennings has appeared to get off scot-free has sparked outrage both publicly and behind the scenes at CNN.

Citing a “double standard,” a CNN staffer wondered, “why does he get to behave one way, but Ryan Girdusky acted similarly on air and rightly got banned,” referencing the October 2024 segment on Phillip’s program that featured Girdusky suggesting Mehdi was a terrorist while saying, “I hope your beeper doesn’t go off.”

Meanwhile, guests who stand up to Jennings, Roginsky told me, are banned. “That’s the part that’s absurd. Like [George] Conway and I were shadow-banned for doing nothing more than standing up to him,” Roginsky added. Shortly after the Mockler blow-up, Conway

“There are a lot of divas and sellouts in TV news. He appears to fit both of those categories. It appears to me he’s been given a lot of latitude to behave the way he does and lie the way he lies,” Acosta told me. “But under no circumstances should a paid pundit ever shout the f-word at another panelist in that fashion.”

Echoing what former CNN contributor Keith Boykin recently said about Jennings’s outburst at Mockler, Ali pointed out that the network once prided itself on holding its contributors to a higher standard.

“Your policy was also that on-air personalities were ‘brand ambassadors.’ … Are they no longer ‘brand ambassadors’? Are only non-MAGA ‘brand ambassadors’? Are the warnings all reserved for non-MAGA? Do MAGA get to say anything they want?” Ali rhetorically asked. “What is their new standard? Who gets warned, and how should a ‘brand ambassador’ comport themselves?”

The on-air CNN personality, meanwhile, explained how Jennings’s behavior – both on-air and off – has led many staffers and other paid contributors to believe that there is one set of rules for them and another for Jennings.

“The inside joke is that we all say, if that had been me, I would have been fired. And that’s particularly acutely felt among our Black contributors,” they stated. “I hear this on an almost daily basis from a number of Black contributors. If that had been me, I would have been fired. And so that’s creating some tension behind the scenes at work. It just doesn’t feel fair, or like there’s a separate set of rules.”

Jennings has engendered so much ill will among much of the staff that one longtime CNN analyst I spoke with made it clear that they were sick of talking about the right-wing pundit.

“I have nothing to say. I would rather speak about colonoscopies than about Scott,” the analyst huffed.

Jennings has also come under fire for his hawkish pro-Israel stance over the past couple of years, which has seen him liken pro-Palestinian protesters to Adolf Hitler and

In fact, it was his advocacy for the Iran war – which is being waged by both the United States and Israel – that led to the fracas with Mockler in the first place.

In recent weeks, both Drop Site News founder Ryan Grim and Roginsky have called out Jennings over what they feel is a failure to disclose the fact that Salem Media has ties to the Israeli government, and that this could be influencing Jennings commentary on Israel.

“You can be as critical as you want of the United States. You just can’t be critical of Israel,” Grim told Jennings during an immigration debate at George Washington University. This was after Grim shared that Brad Parscale, the network’s chief strategy officer and a former Trump campaign manager, is a registered foreign agent for the Israeli Foreign Ministry.

Grim also said it should be “criminal” for American media companies to take money from the Israeli government, adding that Israel should face sanctions from the United States.

“None of this makes Jennings a foreign agent but it does make the ecosystem worth scrutinizing. A CNN commentator now has a major platform at Salem, whose C-Suite executive is paid to help shape messaging on behalf of Israel across American media,” Roginsky wrote in a recent Substack post.

“It is worth asking whether [Mark] Thompson or anyone else in the CNN C-Suite is aware of this and if they find it troubling to share a pundit with a network that is openly integrating foreign viewpoints into its programming,” she added. “Weiss’ role is also critical here. She has made support for Israel a central part of her public identity and editorial worldview, calling herself a ‘Zionist fanatic’ … Irrespective of where one stands on Israel, this is a dangerous way to run a news organization.”

All of this mixed together – the permission structure, the perceived double standards, the network programming (namely Phillip’s show) catering to and incentivizing this type of behavior – seemingly points to a bad ending all the way around.

“I think Scott is like the tip of the spear here, but it’s really a symptom of a bigger problem inside the network that started long before the [Paramount] merger,” the on-air CNN personality sighed.

Justin Baragona is the author of Zeteo’s weekly ‘ Ragebait’ newsletter, which focuses on the right-wing outrage-industrial complex and the intersection of the political and media landscapes. Send Justin any tips on Signal to justinbaragona.85.

Check out more from Zeteo:

If you enjoy Zeteo, share it with your friends and earn rewards when they subscribe.

item.title: Antisemitism? Really? The 33 Most Absurd Examples Listed in the ADL's New Database
item.description: The Anti-Defamation League’s CEO, Jonathan Greenblatt, assured CNN that the organization used a “very rigorous methodology” as it compiled its database of antisemitic incidents that occurred last year – and that it draws a distinction between anti-Israel or pro-Palestine activities, and antisemitic…
item.author: Zeteo
item.content:encoded:
The Anti-Defamation League’s CEO, Jonathan Greenblatt, assured CNN that the organization used a “very rigorous methodology” as it compiled its database of antisemitic incidents that occurred last year – and that it draws a distinction between anti-Israel or pro-Palestine activities, and antisemitic behavior. A closer look at the database shows that is simply not true.

While the rise of antisemitism is of great concern – particularly in light of violence like the Bondi shooting in Australia and the Boulder, Colorado, firebombing attack – the ADL’s database is littered with examples showing how liberal it is in defining antisemitism. This matters in part because US politicians have long used ADL data to justify governmental attacks on free speech, and their unconditional support for Israel’s genocidal and apartheid regime.

The pro-Israel ADL writes that it “is careful to not conflate general criticism of Israel or anti-Israel activism with antisemitism. Legitimate political protest, support for Palestinian rights or expressions of opposition to Israeli policies are not included in the audit.”

Its approach to Israel-related expressions of speech, the ADL adds, “comports with the International Holocaust Remembrance Alliance’s… working definition of antisemitism,” which deems it antisemitic to compare Israel’s behavior to Nazis, for example. (There is much controversy surrounding the IHRA definition in the human rights world.)

Our Zeteo team dug into the ADL’s database of 6,274 documented incidents of antisemitism in 2025 and found scores of incidents that appear to contradict much of its own guidance when it comes to conflating antisemitism with anti-Israel or pro-Palestine expression.

The database counts people chanting “From the River to the Sea, Palestine will be Free” (a chant calling for Palestinian freedom from Israeli occupation) as antisemitism (never mind that “Greater Israel” supporters use a similar phrase to suggest Palestine belongs to Israel). Anti-Zionist messages are marked as antisemitic – despite the ADL’s assurance that such expressions would not be labeled as such. A flier that read “Love Jews Hate Zionism” was, astonishingly, counted as an antisemitic incident.

Meanwhile, many incidents are marked as “confidential,” or lack necessary context to independently assess their veracity. Some appear to be altercations or interactions between friends or family members.

“The ADL gives lip service to the idea that they distinguish between criticism of Israeli state policy and antisemitism, but even a cursory glance at their practices shows they do not mean it,” Dr. Jonah Rubin, manager of campus organizing for Jewish Voice for Peace, tells Zeteo. “They even label anti-Zionist Jewish groups, organizing as Jews, motivated by our Jewish traditions of justice to oppose genocide as somehow antisemitic.”

The ADL did not respond to a request for comment.

Here are just 33 pretty egregious examples of what the ADL is characterizing as definitively antisemitic (note: the incidents below are written exactly as they appear in the ADL’s database, and are listed in chronological order):

  • 2/3/2025; San Diego, California –

    Graffiti that read: “End Zionism” was spray-painted on several buildings around the San Diego State University campus.

  • 2/5/2025; Washington, DC –

    At an anti-Israel rally, protesters displayed a banner that read: “Ask what your country can do for Israel?” with imagery of the Statue of Liberty wrapped in a bloodied Israeli flag.

  • 2/6/2025; Eugene, Oregon –

    Stickers were placed on a statue at the University of Oregon that read: “From the river to the sea, Palestine will be free,” a slogan commonly used to call for an end to the Jewish state.

  • 2/21/2025; Boston, Massachusetts –

    A sticker was placed at Emerson College that read: “Fuck Zionism.”

  • 3/1/2025; Santa Clara, California –

    During a lesson about the Holocaust, a teacher played students a video that directly compared Israel’s treatment of Palestinians in Gaza to the Holocaust.

  • 3/2/2025; Rochester, New York –

    A moderator of a local Facebook group posted a list of “Zionist” businesses to boycott.

  • 3/24/2025; Albuquerque, New Mexico –

    At the University of New Mexico, individuals associated with Students for Justice in Palestine distributed stickers that read: “Zionism is Racism” and “Zionism is Genocide.”

  • 4/10/2025; Glenview, Illinois –

    A student wore a sweatshirt at school that read: “From the river to the sea,” a slogan commonly used as a call to end the Jewish state.

  • 4/15/2025; Los Angeles, California –

    A flier was posted at a Jewish institution that read: “A GENOCIDAL ISRAEL IS BAD FOR ALL JEWS” and that called for divestment.

  • 4/29/2025; Buffalo, New York –

    A University at Buffalo student posted a message in a student association’s group chat calling to boycott a theater for showing a pro-Israel film, writing that the theater was showing “a racist film celebrating the settler pedophile state.”

  • 5/12/2025; Seattle, Washington –

    A flier was placed on a lamp post that read: “Love Jews Hate Zionism.”

  • 6/2/2025; San Francisco, California –

    Messages were left in chalk on a public sidewalk that read: “Zionism Racism.”

  • 6/18/2025; Los Angeles, California –

    A speaker at a University of California, Los Angeles commencement ceremony said, “From the river to the sea” in her speech, a slogan commonly used to call for an end to the Jewish state.

  • 6/21/2025; Baltimore, Maryland –

    A flier for a neighborhood block party displayed at a coffee shop contained a disclaimer that read: “Racism, homophobia, transphobia, and zionism [sic] will not be tolerated!”

  • 7/20/2025; Alameda, California –

    A car was spotted on a public street with a license plate that read: “INT5ADA,” referencing “Intifada,” a slogan commonly used as a call for violence against Israel and the Jewish community.

  • 7/25/2025; Tustin, California –

    A car was spotted on a public street with a license plate that read: “NTFADA,” referencing “Intifada,” a slogan commonly used as a call for violence against Israel and the Jewish community.

  • 8/6/2025; San Francisco, California –

    A sticker was placed on an ATM that read: “Dykes Hate Zionists.”

  • 8/8/2025; Somerville, Massachusetts –

    Graffiti was found near train tracks that read: “Gaza will live and Israel will fall.”

  • 8/12/2025; Washington DC –

    An Israeli food cookbook delivered to a business had the word “Israeli” in title scratched out.

  • 8/14/2025; Princeton, New Jersey –

    A stop sign was vandalized and read: “Stop Zionism.”

  • 9/12/2025; Hatfield, Pennsylvania –

    A professional association released a webinar to members that included the claim: “Zionism is a form of racism.”

  • 9/26/2025; New Haven, Connecticut –

    “Fuck Zionism” and “Fuck Israel” were written in chalk on the campus of Yale University.

  • 10/7/2025; Manhattan, New York –

    At an anti-Israel rally organized by Behind Enemy Lines, a speaker said, “It is right to rebel against every form of oppression, Zionism, racism.”

  • 10/7/2025; Tallahassee, Florida –

    At an anti-Israel rally organized by Students for a Democratic Society, protesters displayed signs that read: “Say no to Zionism.”

  • 10/21/2025; Amherst, Massachusetts –

    The steps of a library at Hampshire College were vandalized with: “Judaism saves, Zionism kills.”

  • 10/24/2025; San Antonio, Texas –

    At an anti-Israel rally organized by San Antonio for Justice in Palestine and the Democratic Socialists of America, protesters chanted, “From the river to the sea, Palestine will be free,” a slogan commonly used to call for an end to the Jewish state. A speaker also said, “Israel is a laboratory for racism, disenfranchisement, disposition, extrajudicial violence, surveillance, [unintelligible] and torture.”

  • 10/25/2025; Princeton, New Jersey –

    A traffic sign was vandalized to read: “Stop Zionism.”

  • 11/9/2025; Oakland California –

    A sticker was placed in a public area that read: “Zionism is Racism Zionism is Genocide.”

  • 11/12/2025; Jersey City, New Jersey –

    A sticker depicting an Israeli flag ripped to partially reveal a Nazi flag underneath was placed on the railing of a pier.

  • 11/29/2025: Charleston, South Carolina –

    At an anti-Israel rally organized by Free Palestine CHS, protesters displayed messages that included: “From the river to the sea, Palestine will be free,” a slogan commonly used to call for an end to the Jewish state.

  • 12/4/2025; Ann Arbor, Michigan –

    Fliers were distributed at the University of Michigan that read: “Zionism out of SPH [School of Public Health].”

  • 12/13/2025; Alexandria, Virginia –

    At an anti-Israel rally organized by groups including Jewish Voice for Peace and the Palestinian Youth Movement, protesters chanted, “From the river to the sea, Palestine will be free,” a slogan commonly used to call for an end to the Jewish state.

  • 12/31/2025: Boston, Massachusetts –

    At an anti-Israel rally organized by Northshore for Palestine, Jamaica Plane for Palestine, Allston-Brighton for Palestine and Concord for Palestine, protesters displayed signs with messages that included: “Zohranism yes, Zionism no.”

Zeteo’s Alexa Cohen, Kennedy Garmong, Melanie Riehl, and Stephen Wentzell contributed to this reporting.

Have any tips or information for us? You can text Prem on Signal at premthakker.35.

item.title: Is Trump About to Attack Cuba?
item.description: 🚨 Breaking: Israel is illegally intercepting the Gaza aid flotilla in international waters near Cyprus. The last report we got from Zeteo contributor Alex Colston, who is on the flotilla, was that an Israeli speedboat was approaching the boat he was sailing on. He reported earlier that the crew on…
item.author: First Draft | Zeteo
item.content:encoded:
🚨 Breaking: Israel is illegally intercepting the Gaza aid flotilla in international waters near Cyprus. The last report we got from Zeteo contributor Alex Colston, who is on the flotilla, was that an Israeli speedboat was approaching the boat he was sailing on. He reported earlier that the crew on the boats that have already been intercepted are being taken to an Israeli ship, similar to the one on which the abducted participants were held when the fleet was last intercepted.

Good morning! Martin here, with your first ‘First Draft’ of the week, having somehow resisted the temptation on Sunday to catch the D6X down 16th Street and take in the Trump administration’s massive Christian Nationalist prayer rally on the National Mall, and yet ended up watching it anyway, all the while picking up a growing whirring sound as the Founders rolled in their graves…

In today’s ‘First Draft,’ a leading news outlet carries water for the administration as it plans yet another war, Donald Trump apparently does “do God,” a drone strike causes a fire near a UAE nuclear power plant, and the president’s polling somehow finds new depths to plumb…

On Sunday, Axios published what seemed a startling exclusive.

Under the headline “U.S. eyes attack drone threat from Cuba”, the piece read: “Cuba has acquired more than 300 military drones and recently began discussing plans to use them to attack the U.S. base at Guantánamo Bay, U.S. military vessels and possibly Key West, Florida, 90 miles north of Havana, according to classified intelligence shared with Axios.”

Noting that said shared intelligence “could become a pretext for U.S. military action,” the piece said the Trump administration sees Cuba as a threat due to factors including “the presence of Iranian military advisers in Havana.”

An unnamed “senior U.S. official” was quoted as saying: “When we think about those types of technologies being that close, and a range of bad actors from terror groups to drug cartels to Iranians to the Russians, it’s concerning. It’s a growing threat.”

It was enough to prompt Sheriff Rick Ramsey of Monroe County, covering the Florida Keys, to put out a statement, saying he was monitoring the situation.

But Sheriff Ramsey also said he had not heard from the administration, and given President Donald Trump’s frequent threats to invade Cuba, his oil embargo that has wrecked the island’s economy, the reportedly imminent U.S. indictment of 94-year-old former Cuban President Raúl Castro, and CIA Director John Ratcliffe’s visit to Havana just last week, there to deliver more threats, all widely reported, even the least curious reader of the Axios exclusive might’ve wondered just how serious this supposed Cuban drone threat might be, and just what motivation might lie behind its release via the press.

Dan Pfeiffer, a former adviser to President Barack Obama, raised the twin specters of George W. Bush and “WMD” when he said: “Real Iraq War vibes here. Classified intelligence shared with reporters to sell a war to a skeptical public.”

Senator Ruben Gallego said, “No country in the current economic and military situation that Cuba is in would ever preemptively strike a super power like the U.S. This is just propaganda to build a case for an invasion of Cuba.”

The Arizona Democrat, an Iraq veteran, added: “We are supposed to worry about 300 drones threatening our base at Guatanomo [sic] Bay, but not the 1,700 artillery pieces and hundreds of MLRS systems that have been aimed at the base for decades? Or could it be that they are just scaring us into another useless war?”

To be fair to Axios, it did note at the top of its piece that the drones claim “could become a pretext for military action.” But in a piece of considerable length for a publication that preaches (and sells) the gospel of “smart brevity,” another telling passage was distinctly buried.

Only in the… wait for it… 26th paragraph of the piece did Axios reveal the following: “U.S. officials don’t believe Cuba is an imminent threat, or actively planning to attack American interests.”

… a line which might be seen rather to undermine the five-alarm headline and lede.

Indeed, the same might be said for the sentence that followed: “U.S intelligence indicates the island’s military officials have been discussing drone warfare plans in case hostilities erupt as relations with the U.S. continue to deteriorate.”

… ie, intelligence indicates Cuba might use drones if the U.S. attacks.

… ie, Cuba might use drones in self-defense.

Axios said the Cuban government did not respond to a request for comment. It did post a statement on social media.

It said: “Like any country, Cuba has the right to defend itself against external aggression. It is called self-defense, and it is protected by International Law and the UN Charter.

“Those from the U.S. who seek the submission and, in fact, the destruction of the Cuban nation through military aggression and war, do not waste a single moment fabricating pretexts, creating and spreading falsehoods, and distorting as extraordinary the logical preparation required to face a potential aggression.”

Axios’s X post attracted a community note, linking both to the story and a Reuters report headlined “Cuba accuses U.S. of fabrication after Axios report alleging drone threat.”

The note said: “U.S. officials do not believe Cuba is an imminent threat or actively planning attacks, with drone discussions reported only in case of deteriorating relations. The claims rely on classified intelligence with no independent verification.”

It might’ve added context on something actually expected to happen this week: the Raúl Castro indictment. It concerns an incident in February 1996, when Castro was defense minister under his brother Fidel, in which two unarmed planes operated by right-wing Cuban exiles were shot down over international waters. All four men on the planes were killed. President Bill Clinton ordered sanctions. Four Cubans were indicted, but not extradited.

If Castro is now indicted in the U.S., the charges will have no international validity, of course, and will not legally justify an unprovoked act of aggression against Cuba by the Trump administration. But they will, it seems, be used as further pretext for further outrages by this lawless regime.

  • Familiar threat:As talks remained stalled, Donald Trumpposted: “For Iran, the Clock is Ticking, and they better get moving, FAST, or there won’t be anything left of them. TIME IS OF THE ESSENCE!”
  • UAE hit:A drone strikecauseda fire near the UAE’s sole nuclear power plant. No injuries or radiological release were reported. The International Atomic Energy Agency called for “maximum military restraint” near any nuclear power plant.
  • Saudi blocks:Saudi Arabiasaidit foiled three drones sent from Iraqi airspace.
  • Secret bases:Israel used notonebut two secret Iraq bases for attacks on Iran, theNew York Timesreported, describing how an Iraqi shepherd who stumbled across a base was killed by a helicopter, his truck “flaming and riddled with bullets.”
  • Lebanon news:At least eight werekilledin Israeli strikes on southern Lebanon, as the Netanyahu government continued to ignore a supposed ceasefire. Israel haskilledat least 2,988 people in Lebanon, including more than 200 children, since the latest escalation began.

On Jan. 1, 1802, in his letter to the Baptists of Danbury, Connecticut, President Thomas Jefferson described “a wall of separation between Church & State,” erected by the founders.

Lord knows that wall has been battered and undermined, but on Sunday the Trump administration sought to blow it to bits, holding a federally funded event, “Rededicate 250: A National Jubilee of Prayer, Praise & Thanksgiving,” on the National Mall.

Speakers included Secretary of Defense and Pulp Theologian Pete Hegseth, who exhorted attendees to “pray for our nation on bended knee and let us ask our Lord and savior Jesus Christ, as Washington did on that momentous day, so help us God.”

He was referring to an account of George Washington praying for guidance at a low point in the Revolutionary War, a story immensely popular on the Christian right regardless of the inconvenient truth that it didn’t happen.

Observers noted other ironies, including the presence of a single rabbi among the Christian speakers and a video address from Donald Trump, perhaps not the, uh, most religiously observant president ever elected.

Then there was Eric Metaxas. A day after the Senate parliamentarian put the kibosh on Republican hopes of using a party-line vote to pass $1 billion of taxpayer funding for Trump’s White House ballroom, the radio host and author told the assembled flock God himself wanted Trump to have his cherished “emiratic carbuncle.”

“It’s hard to believe that it would take two centuries for the Lord to raise up a great man to bring that ballroom finally to stand where it needs to stand,” Metaxas said. “It’s extraordinary. We only had to wait 200 years.”

His words were certainly extraordinary, if not in a good way. So was the event he spoke at.

  • Polls plummet:CBS found Trump suffering a44-pointapproval loss with white non-college voters, and77%of all voters saying they can’t cope with inflation.
  • Double down:Asked about saying “I don’t think about Americans’ financial situation,” TrumptoldFox it was a “perfect statement. I’d make it again.”
  • Bill beaten:After losing his primary to a Trump-backed challenger, Louisiana GOP Senator Bill Cassidy, who voted to remove Trump over January 6, threwshade: “When you participate in democracy,. sometimes it doesn’t turn out the way you want it to. But you don’t pout. You don’t whine. You don’t claim the election was stolen.”
  • Next up:Trump turned to Thomas Massie, the Kentucky congressman and Epstein Files campaigner who faces voters this week,tellingMAGA, “Vote the bum out.”
  • Corruption watch:Trump’s DOJ slush fund to pay out to allies who claim mistreatment will becalled The President Donald J. Trump Truth and Justice Commission and will total $1.776 billion, ABCreported. Trump was late disclosing tens of millions of dollars of dubiously timed share trades, so he was fined…$200.
  • Polis pressure:Under fire from fellow Democrats, includingcallsfor his impeachment, Colorado Governor Jared Polis said Trump did not persuade him to commute county clerk Tina Peters’s nine-year sentence, arising from Trump’s attempt to overturn the 2020 election. “It’s not a crime in our country to believe the earth is flat,” PolistoldtheNew York Times.

In Eric Metaxas’s “Donald the Caveman” children’s books, a club-wielding Donald Trump, clad in a Stars and Stripes onesie, vanquishes which top Democrats, portrayed as “an angry little girl“ and “an angry, crazy old man”?

Keep your eyes out for the answer below!

  • 🇵🇸 Don’t forget Gaza:Israeli strikes in Gaza killed at least five people on Sunday, Palestinian authoritiessaid, as Israel continued to ignore the supposed ceasefire. Three people were killed at a community kitchen near Al-Aqsa Hospital in Deir Al-Balah.
  • 🇵🇸 UN insult:Israelwillbuild a military facility on the site of the UN Palestinian refugee agency (UNRWA) compound in East Jerusalem, which Israeldemolishedlast year.
  • 🇨🇳​​🇹🇼 China crisis:Trump advisers fear China will invade Taiwan in the next five years, Axiosreported, after the U.S. president’s Beijing talks with Xi Jinping.

  • 🇵🇰

    Khan cable:Drop Sitepublishedimages of the leaked 2022 diplomatic cable in which a Biden State Department official told the Pakistani ambassador that Prime Minister Imran Khan should be removed.

  • 🇺🇬🇨🇩

    Ebola emergency:The World Health Organizationdeclaredan emergency over the Ebola outbreak in Uganda and the Democratic Republic of the Congo. Nearly 90 people have died.


Senator John Fetterman, the DINO from Pennsylvania, thinks Trump should get his ballroom and blue reflecting pool on the taxpayers’ dime. And he is only 200 years off on how old America is.

  • Trump lackey and war ghoul

    Lindsey Grahamcalled for strikes on Iranian infrastructure,telling NBC’s ‘Meet the Press’: “There’s still more targets to be had. And there’s things we can do to hurt. The energy infrastructure is their soft underbelly. If you go back to the fight, I’d put energy on top of the list.” That would bea war crime.

  • Congressman

    Thomas Massiewas defiant ahead of the GOP Kentucky primary in which Trump and MAGA hope to oust him. Massietold ABC’s ‘This Week’: “I have the endorsement of the right-to-life organizations, the gun organizations. I had four members of Congress come here yesterday and campaign with me. Plus, I’ve had millions of dollars come in from the grassroots… and that’s how we’re going to beat them.”
  • James Comeyhad sharp words forActing Attorney General Todd Blancheabout his promotion of the DOJ indictment of the former FBI director for supposedly threatening Trump with a message made of seashells. “I don’t talk about the case because the federal court rules require you not,” Comeytold NBC. “I would urge the acting attorney general to bone up on the rules.”
  • Tuesday, May 19:Georgia,Pennsylvania,Alabama,Idaho,Kentucky, andOregonwill hold congressional primaries.
  • Wednesday, May 20:In DC, protesters are expected to march from ICE HQ to the U.S. Capitol, to call on Congress to rejectbillionsin funding for the agency.
  • Thursday, May 21:NATOforeign ministers are set to meet in Sweden.
  • Friday, May 22:EU ministers are expected to meet in Brussels, Belgium, for discussions on the trade and economic security impact of the Iran war.

Zeteo’s Kennedy Garmong contributed to this newsletter.

Still want to get Zeteo content, but not this newsletter? You can unsubscribe directly from ‘First Draft’ by going to ‘

If you enjoy Zeteo, share it with your friends and earn rewards when they subscribe.
