item.title: How Charlie Sykes Went From Right-Wing Commentator To a Never-Trumper Purged From Twitter
item.description: Right-wing media personalities – like Tucker Carlson and Candace Owens – are finally turning against U.S. President Donald Trump, amid his reckless war on Iran and dipping poll numbers. But in this conversation, Zeteo’s John Harwood speaks to one of the first conservative media personalities to…
item.author: Zeteo
item.content:encoded:
Right-wing media personalities – like Tucker Carlson and Candace Owens – are finally turning against U.S. President Donald Trump, amid his reckless war on Iran and dipping poll numbers.

But in this conversation, Zeteo’s John Harwood speaks to one of the first conservative media personalities to speak out against Trump – Charlie Sykes, whose Twitter posts were recently purged, after publishing an article criticizing Elon Musk.

In this conversation, Sykes – one of The Bulwark’s co-founders and author of the Substack, “To the Contrary” – warns that until MAGA is stopped from deforming America’s identity, “this is who we are.”

Free subscribers can only watch a 3-minute preview. Consider upgrading to a paid subscription and never hit a paywall again!

This was a live conversation on Zeteo.com and the Substack app, where subscribers could ask questions. Be sure to subscribe to catch the next Substack Live.

Share


Check out Zeteo’s other recent stories:

item.title: Israeli Forces Kill More Children on Nakba Day, While Settlers Force Family to Dig Up Dead Father
item.description: The horror Palestinians endure every week is so relentless that it’s hard to document it all. As Palestinians marked Nakba Day yesterday, this week was no exception. The list below, for example, doesn’t include that Israel’s ultranationalist, noose-loving national security minister stormed the…
item.author: This Week in Palestine | Zeteo
item.content:encoded:
The horror Palestinians endure every week is so relentless that it’s hard to document it all. As Palestinians marked Nakba Day yesterday, this week was no exception.

The list below, for example, doesn’t include that Israel’s ultranationalist, noose-loving national security minister stormed the Al-Aqsa Mosque compound and unfurled an Israeli flag on Thursday. Nor does it include new data that shows Israel has sharply increased the use of solitary confinement for detained Palestinians. Nor the story of a Palestinian child Israeli forces shot dead just yesterday. (His name was Fahd Zidan Owais. He was 16-years-old.)

What it does include is just as horrific: settlers forcing a Palestinian family to dig up their father’s body; the killing of another child, 16-year-old Yusef Ali Kaabnah, by Israeli forces; Israel’s predictable, yet disgusting response to a report about rape and other forms of sexual violence Palestinians endure at the hands of Israeli security forces; and more:

Al Jazeera and other outlets reported that a day earlier, Israeli settlers – under the protection of the Israeli military – forced a Palestinian family to dig up their 80-year-old father’s body, who had been buried earlier in the day after dying from natural causes in the Asasa village near Jenin.

The settlers claimed that Hussein Asasa had been buried in land used by a nearby settlement. Not only are Israeli settlements illegal under international law, but the Asasa family said they had gotten prior approval from the Israeli military to bury his body in the cemetery.

Israeli strikes killed at least three Palestinians and injured several others, including children, in a string of attacks on Gaza. Those killed included the director of Khan Younis police investigations, officials said.

Israel has killed more than 850 Palestinians since the so-called “ceasefire” supposedly went into effect last October.

The Israeli Knesset passed a law to set up a special tribunal with the power to impose the death penalty on those convicted of carrying out the Oct. 7 attacks in Israel.

Israeli and human rights groups warned that the measure eliminates certain procedures in place that are supposed to ensure fair trials. “The bill explicitly permits mass trials that deviate from standard rules of evidence, including broad judicial discretion to admit evidence obtained under coercive conditions that may amount to torture or ill-treatment,” Muna Haddad, a lawyer with the Israeli human rights group Adalah, told Al Jazeera.

US Democratic Senators Chris Van Hollen and Jeff Merkley sent a letter to Secretary of State Marco Rubio and FBI Director Kash Patel, demanding justice and accountability over Israel’s killing of Palestinian-American journalist Shireen Abu Akleh four years ago.

The senators called on the U.S. officials to release the 2023 report conducted by the US security coordinator into her death and to provide an update on the FBI’s investigation into her killing.

Watch Zeteo’s explosive documentary, which identified, for the first time, the Israeli soldier who shot Abu Akleh:

Israeli forces killed 16-year-old Yusef Ali Kaabnah after Israeli settlers raided the village of Sinjil and stole Palestinian families’ sheep.

The Israeli Foreign Ministry announced it was suing the New York Times for defamation over Nicholas Kristof’s report detailing the rape and other sexual violence Palestinians have endured at the hands of Israeli security forces and settlers.

Kristof’s in-depth and meticulously reported piece, which included the accounts of 14 Palestinian men and women who said they had been sexually abused by Israeli soldiers, prison guards, or settlers, only confirmed what has long been reported by other media outlets, the UN, human rights groups, and, most importantly, Palestinians themselves.

Israel killed at least seven Palestinians, including three women and a child, in targeted strikes on a residential building and a vehicle near Gaza City.

The strikes also killed Hamas’s top military commander in Gaza, Izz al-Din al-Haddad.

The attack took place on Nakba day – the day Palestinians use to mark the ethnic cleansing and forced displacement of hundreds of thousands of Palestinians during the creation of the state of Israel in 1948.

The Nakba continues today.

Miss last week’s edition? Catch up here. Plus, check out more from Zeteo:

If you enjoy Zeteo, share it with your friends and earn rewards when they subscribe.

item.title: ‘Mega-Bloodbath’: Trump Officials Hate His Plan to Seize Iran’s Uranium
item.description: As Donald Trump weighs a potentially imminent escalation of his war on Iran , five sources in and close to his administration tell Zeteo that the president continues to tell senior U.S. officials and other confidants how interested he is in plans for sending American troops into Iran to capture and…
item.author: "Zeteo | U.S. Politics"
item.content:encoded:
As Donald Trump weighs a potentially imminent escalation of his war on Iran, five sources in and close to his administration tell Zeteo that the president continues to tell senior U.S. officials and other confidants how interested he is in plans for sending American troops into Iran to capture and remove stockpiles of highly enriched uranium.

Seemingly everybody in the federal government thinks such a mission would be a bloodbath.

The president seems to have cooled somewhat on plans his administration drew up for invading other parts of Iran, such as Kharg Island, but knowledgeable sources maintain that he is still intrigued by the idea of sending U.S. troops and special forces to invade Iran for the purpose of seizing the nuclear material.

President Trump has publicly acknowledged as much, stressing, “I’d just feel better if I got it.” It does not appear that Trump has made a final decision on this yet, even as his administration lays the groundwork for ramping the war on Iran back up.

Sources who’ve discussed this idea with the president say that earlier this year, he used to insist that a uranium-extraction operation would be “easy” for elite U.S. special forces units – or, at least easier than critics and naysayers are making it out to be.

When Trump has spoken about it behind closed doors, it’s often sounded like “he’s talking about a scene in a movie,” one Trump administration official says.

If that sounds familiar, when Trump invaded Venezuela and kidnapped its leader early this year, he later marveled: “I watched it literally like I was watching a television show.”

The president has repeatedly told advisers that “many” smart people he knows, like Fox host and informal Trump counselor Mark Levin, support the idea.

In an interview with ‘60 Minutes’ that aired on CBS last week, Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu said the U.S.-Israeli war on Iran is not over “because there’s still nuclear material – enriched uranium – that has to be taken out of Iran,” adding: “You go in and you take it out.” Netanyahu also said that Trump had personally told him that he indeed wants “to go in there” to get it.

There’s one problem, though: A lot of officials working at the senior and Cabinet levels of Trump’s government think it’s a resoundingly terrible idea. (In fact, it’s hard to find any Trump officials of actual influence who, in their quieter and private moments, will say that it’s a decent idea.) Some of these officials have been trying to subtly wean the president off of this proposal, citing the likely possibility of high numbers of U.S. troops being killed, captured, or wounded, as well as other logistical and political nightmares that could come with ordering such a dangerous mission.

Still, another Trump administration official tells Zeteo, “We might actually be stupid enough to try it.”

Trump White House spokesperson Anna Kelly tells us, “President Trump is right – Iran cannot be allowed to possess enriched uranium that could quickly be upgraded to produce a nuclear weapon. Zeteo’s anonymous sources ‘close to the administration’ have no idea what they are talking about. The President’s entire team is behind him as he protects our homeland from the short- and long-term threat posed by a nuclear Iran.”

The sources note that over the years, the U.S. government – across multiple Republican and Democratic administrations, not just the Trump-Vance team – has extensively wargamed and run various exercises on precisely this scenario: a possible U.S. troop deployment to Iran to extract highly enriched uranium.

The current Trump administration has the classified assessments. It knows what officials and staff at the Pentagon, Energy Department, and elsewhere in the federal apparatus know. And it’s why the senior Trump administration officials who have been briefed on this information are largely frightened by the thought of invading to secure the nuclear material.

First of all, a high-risk operation of this nature would likely require thousands of troops, many of whom would not be expected to make it back alive.

“Taking Kharg Island would be a bloodbath,” one U.S official familiar with the uranium-related exercises says. “This would be a mega-bloodbath … There’s also reason to think that some senior Iranians want us to put boots on the ground because it makes it easier to kill Americans.”

Such a mission almost certainly wouldn’t be as easy as President Trump believes; it would not look like the comparatively short operation that Trump ordered that resulted in the successful kidnapping of Venezuelan leader, Nicolás Maduro.

U.S. forces could be stuck in Iran, including at a secret underground Iranian facility, for days, in severely hostile enemy territory. Furthermore, highly enriched uranium weighs a lot and can be – as you might expect – extremely hazardous.

“There’s a lot of it and it’s fucking heavy,” the U.S. official bluntly notes. This sort of undertaking would also require hazmat suits and a large amount of extraction and transport equipment – which is why “it’s not the Seal Team 6 movie that Donald Trump has in his head,” this official adds.

Internal U.S. government assessments – including ones developed by the new Trump administration – on scenarios for this kind of mission have all led numerous senior American officials to believe that it would be an unmitigated disaster.

People in and close to this administration tell Zeteo that they’ve spent weeks periodically reminding the president about, among other things, the high U.S. casualty estimates, in hopes of scaring him off of this as a viable option.

So far, it hasn’t worked.

“It’s no surprise that this [idea] isn’t being driven by the administration. This whole war was driven by people on the outside,” says Matt Duss, a former top foreign policy adviser to Senator Bernie Sanders. “All of these hawkish idiots – like Mark Levin, Netanyahu, Lindsey Graham, all the goofballs at the Foundation for Defense of Democracies – are the ones who got in Trump’s ear to launch what has clearly become yet another strategic catastrophe for the United States. But to put thousands of U.S. troops on the ground in Iran would be even more extraordinarily stupid than this war already is. The Pentagon had multiple scenario exercises for years that showed the war like the one that Trump launched would result in exactly this catastrophe.”

Though Trump has vacillated somewhat on the uranium-extraction plot – as he has with practically every key issue of his illegal, grossly unpopular war on Iran – it keeps cropping up as one of his flashiest demands and pet fixations.

One Trump adviser flagged the interview that the president gave this week to his pal, Fox host Sean Hannity, to argue that when it comes to the gentle pressure Trump has gotten about not invading Iran to seize uranium stockpiles, “some of it is sinking in.” In his sitdown with the primetime host, the president did seem to acknowledge the operation would invariably be a days-long, difficult undertaking.

But even in the Hannity interview, the president couldn’t help but voice his intense desire to just seize it.

“We were thinking about doing it … You gotta move these massive amounts of granite … I don’t think it’s necessary except from a public relations standpoint,” Trump said, undercutting some of his previous arguments for why extracting stockpiles was so important.

“I think it’s important for the fake news that we get [the uranium],'“ he continued. “I’m the one that said we are going to get it, and we’re gonna get it … I’d rather get it, to be honest with you … I’d just feel better if I got it, actually. But I think it’s more for public relations than it is for anything else.”

item.title: Saudi Arabia and the UAE Bombed Iran for Totally Different Reasons
item.description: The Gulf royals learned the hard way that Donald Trump’s affections can’t be bought, only rented. Mohmmed bin Salman, the crown prince and de facto ruler of Saudi Arabia, known as MBS, and Mohammed bin Zayed, the president of the UAE, known as MBZ, pledged trillions of dollars in U.S. investments ,…
item.author: Zeteo
item.content:encoded:
The Gulf royals learned the hard way that Donald Trump’s affections can’t be bought, only rented. Mohmmed bin Salman, the crown prince and de facto ruler of Saudi Arabia, known as MBS, and Mohammed bin Zayed, the president of the UAE, known as MBZ, pledged trillions of dollars in U.S. investments, including billions for projects directly connected to Trump and his family, only to find their countries turned into cannon fodder overnight in a war the U.S. president started without even bothering to warn them.

The violent shocks of the Iran war have accelerated a phenomenon already underway in Arab Gulf capitals: Planning for a future where they can no longer depend on American protection.

But MBS and his erstwhile ally and mentor MBZ imagine very different futures.

In 2015, MBS and MBZ teamed up for a catastrophic invasion of Yemen. The bloody quagmire is among the misadventures that humbled the ambitions of the Saudi crown prince, who eventually sued for peace with Yemen’s Iran-backed Houthis and restored diplomatic relations with Iran’s government. But it only seems to have whetted the appetite of his Emirati counterpart, MBZ, for supporting separatists if he thinks they’ll nab him a valuable port or help crush political Islam, a lifetime fixation of the Emirati ruler.

Today, MBS and MBZ see many regional conflicts as zero-sum contests for influence, including in Yemen, where they now support opposing factions.

But stability-focused Riyadh and revisionist Abu Dhabi have, until recently, accepted the same pitch made by successive U.S. administrations hoping to finally escape the Middle East: the Gulf states start working with – or more likely under – the Israeli government against shared adversary Iran. Trump implemented this in his first term with the Abraham Accords, which established open diplomatic relations – and arms deals – between the governments of Israel and the UAE, Bahrain, and Morocco. Joe Biden further institutionalized Israel-Gulf security cooperation with the Middle East Air Defense initiative, which had Saudi Arabia and neighbors protecting Israel from Iranian missiles, and never stopped trying to bring Riyadh into the Abraham Accords, even after the Israeli government’s genocide in Gaza made it politically impossible for MBS.

But with the benefit of hindsight, after seeing the beating Gulf Arab states are taking for a war they didn’t start, it might have been a mistake for them to bind their security to a U.S. president who is famously incapable of making commitments and an Israeli prime minister who leads a movement united by the belief that Arabs are subhuman. The Israeli government’s nihilistic regional escalation following October 7, including unprecedented airstrikes on Qatar last summer to target Hamas leaders, raised fears that Tehran was not the only threat in the region.

MBS’s response to this reality has been to stick to his policy of de-escalation with Tehran and diversification of security partnerships, but MBZ, who before the war also oversaw pragmatic trade and diplomatic relations with Tehran, appears to have doubled down on partnering with Israel and provoking Iran.

The diverging strategies explain why MBZ was unable to convince MBS to join him in attacking Iran at the start of the war, and why, when MBS eventually did strike Iran, he did it on his own terms.

Compared to Saudi Arabia, the UAE is more vulnerable by every metric: Its small population of just over a million citizens – the other 90% of UAE residents are foreign workers – lives entirely within range of Iran’s short-range missiles. While Saudi Arabia can still export some oil via the Red Sea, Emirati shipping is entirely at Tehran’s mercy, with the Iranian military expanding its blockade zone to include Fujairah, the one major Emirati port not trapped behind the Strait of Hormuz. The UAE’s carefully cultivated image as a safe haven for foreign capital, tourists, and expats is shattered, perhaps irreparably, with each Iranian drone strike.

So, how to explain MBZ risking prolonging the war by bombing Iran again immediately after Trump announced the April 7 ceasefire? Or days earlier, Abu Dhabi surprising the Pakistani government by demanding immediate, painful repayment of $3 billion in debt as apparent punishment for Pakistan’s proactive role mediating U.S.-Iran negotiations? At the same time, Abu Dhabi’s cooperation with Tel Aviv has been unprecedented – and ostentatious – including bringing Israeli air defense systems manned by Israeli troops into the UAE, and not-so-secretly hosting Netanyahu for a visit. While the UAE’s small, well-surveilled population means MBZ isn’t constrained by public opinion vis-à-vis Israel, flaunting the relationship still puts a target on the UAE’s back for Tehran; when Trump launched limited strikes on Iran last week, the UAE was the only Gulf state the Iranian military targeted in retaliation.

Did MBZ, like too many policymakers in DC and Tel Aviv, buy the hype and believe this war was his one shot to pacify Iran’s government and remake the Middle East? “Maybe they were under the mistaken impression this could happen in a way that was not deeply destabilizing,” Annelle Sheline, an expert on Gulf geopolitics at the Quincy Institute, tells me, “but they must know Iran better than that.” Sheline says that despite the risks, including that the war would only anger, not defeat, neighboring Iran, MBZ wanted to demonstrate the UAE’s worth as an ally to Trump and, more importantly, Netanyahu. Abu Dhabi is, she says, “putting all its eggs in the Israel basket.”

Sheline points to comments from prominent Emirati academic Abdulkhaleq Abdulla, understood to represent Abu Dhabi’s position, that “the UAE no longer needs America to defend it,” with the implication that Tel Aviv can fill any void left by DC. The claim also exposes the risk – and ridiculousness – of MBZ’s approach, given that both the Israeli and Emirati militaries depend on U.S. arms, and Israel itself relies on extensive US military protection.

At the same time, the Emirati government’s adamant denial that the Netanyahu visit ever happened does suggest that whatever MBZ hoped might happen in the first month of the war, today he still hopes to avoid further provoking Tehran.

The relatively more cautious MBS also bombed Iran, as Reuters revealed just this week. But the Saudi airstrikes, sometime in March, are a rare successful application of “escalate to de-escalate.” It looks like Riyadh basically bombed Iran to get its attention, then immediately followed up with diplomatic engagement professing its desire to avoid further escalation with Tehran. It worked, with the Iranian government toning down strikes on Saudi Arabia. As Sheline points out, this engagement was only possible because Riyadh worked to restore diplomatic relations with Tehran in 2023.

While Tehran may not have especially feared the Saudi military threat – it was already under intense bombardment from the US and Israeli militaries – it did want a new de facto ally in pressuring Trump to end the war. The bomb-then-forgive strategy paid off for Tehran, too. Last week, the Saudi and Kuwaiti governments essentially canceled Trump’s blockade-busting “Project Freedom” by denying US access to bases and airspace. Riyadh is now considering a formal non-aggression pact with Tehran.

With his Iran war, Trump is closer than his predecessors ever were to reshaping the region – though the new order he’s ushering in won’t be dominated by DC or Tel Aviv, but instead organized around withstanding their destabilizing interventions. The Saudi and Pakistani governments signed a defense pact last year, not in response to Iranian aggression but to the Israeli strike on Qatar; the Turkish and Qatari governments may also join their pact; Saudi Arabia, Turkey, Egypt, and Pakistan are increasingly coordinating responses to the Iran war and other security issues. All of these governments maintain strong relationships with DC, but none treat Iran as an existential threat, and the latter three have stayed neutral during this war. MBZ, on the other hand, seems firmly committed to taking sides. The question is how many conflicts he’ll be dragged into, and how isolated he finds himself in the region, before he realizes Trump/Netanyahu is a losing ticket.

Harrison Mann is a former US Army major and executive officer of the Defense Intelligence Agency’s Middle East/Africa Regional Center who resigned in protest of his office’s support for Israel’s war in Gaza under the Biden administration. He is currently with the group Win Without War and recently became a Zeteo contributor.

item.title: Trump Wants to Give $1.7 Billion of YOUR Money to HIS Pals
item.description: On this day in 1942, gasoline rationing went into effect in 17 eastern states, in an attempt to help the US war effort. By the end of the year, all 48 states were rationing gas. There’s no way such a drastic step will ever be needed again. Right? Good morning! Mehdi here. Later today, I’ll be…
item.author: First Draft | Zeteo
item.content:encoded:
On this day in 1942, gasoline rationing went into effect in 17 eastern states, in an attempt to help the US war effort. By the end of the year, all 48 states were rationing gas. There’s no way such a drastic step will ever be needed again. Right?

Good morning! Mehdi here. Later today, I’ll be unpacking the ongoing AOC-vs-MTG controversy in a video monologue and making the case for the Democratic congresswoman and against the former Republican congresswoman. That’ll be in your inboxes in a few hours.

But for now, in today’s ‘First Draft’ newsletter, Trump’s corrupt behavior reaches corrupt new heights; Manchester Mayor Andy Burnham is trying to get back into Parliament to topple flailing UK Prime Minister Keir Starmer; and Israel continues to bomb Lebanon in violation of the supposed “ceasefire” as yet another War Powers Resolution vote fails in Congress.

“You can take all the corruption from every single US administration in history, add it all together, and it will not equal the amount of corruption committed by the Trump Regime in less than two years,” tweeted Ron Filipkowski, the conservative-turned-liberal commentator and ardent Trump critic. He was referring to a New York Times

But let’s be honest: Filipkowski could have been responding to any number of corruption scandals in this second Trump term. How about the president’s acceptance of a $400 million plane from Qatar in violation of the Emoluments Clause of the Constitution? Or Trump and his family’s acceptance of $187 million from the Abu Dhabi royals via their investment in the Trump family cryptocurrency venture, another clear violation of the Emoluments Clause? Or companies associated with Trump’s sons winning at least two lucrative government contracts?

The list goes on and on and on and on.

Nevertheless, I’d argue that this was the week we learned of the biggest Trump corruption scandal of all.

Here’s the background:

In 2019, an IRS contractor leaked Donald Trump’s confidential tax returns to ProPublica and the New York Times, which reported that Trump paid only $750 in taxes in both 2016 and 2017. The leaker was later found, prosecuted by the Biden administration, and sentenced to five years in prison.

But that wasn’t enough for Trump. In January of this year, he and his two adult sons, Don Jr. and Eric, sued the IRS and the Treasury Department for a whopping $10 billion, claiming the tax leak had caused them “reputational and financial harm” and “public embarrassment.”

Got that? The head of the executive branch of the United States decided to sue the executive branch of the United States. And it wasn’t his first such lawsuit. Last year, Trump sued the Department of Justice for $230 million for searching his home in Mar-a-Lago (for the classified documents he stole!) and for daring to investigate his well-documented ties to Russia. In a rare Trump understatement, he conceded that his lawsuit “sort of looks bad.”

“It’s interesting because I’m the one who makes a decision, right, and, you know, that decision would have to go across my desk,” the president said in the Oval Office in October. “It’s awfully strange to make a decision where I’m paying myself.”

Yes, yes, it is! But it gets worse. Last month, a federal judge threatened to throw out Trump’s lawsuit because, she noted, Trump and the defendants – the Treasury Department and IRS – might not be “sufficiently adverse” to justify the case proceeding. Judge Kathleen Williams gave both sides a deadline of May 20 to show how they are in actual conflict with one another, given that both government entities answer to… the president.

So what did the White House and the DOJ do? They began, according to the New York Times,

And guess what that potential settlement looks like? ABC News had the scoop yesterday:

“President Donald Trump is expected to drop his $10 billion lawsuit against the Internal Revenue Service in exchange for the creation of a $1.7 billion fund to compensate allies who claim they were wrongfully targeted by the Biden administration, sources familiar with the matter told ABC News.

The commission overseeing the compensation fund would have the total authority to hand out approximately $1.7 billion in taxpayer funds to settle claims brought by anyone who alleges they were harmed by the Biden administration’s “weaponization” of the legal system, including the nearly 1,600 individuals charged in connection with the Jan. 6 Capitol attack as well as potentially entities associated with President Trump himself.”

Donald Trump is planning on using our tax dollars to (re)pay his friends – and his thugs. It’s a corrupt slush fund for MAGA. And I know some of you must be thinking, ‘But surely there must be some oversight of this money, some safeguards and protections for the American taxpayer?’

Nope. Nada. Zilch. From ABC News, again:

“The arrangement would be an unprecedented use of taxpayer dollars with little oversight. Under the terms of the potential settlement agreement, President Trump would have the authority to remove members of the commission running the fund without cause, and the commission would be under no obligation to disclose its procedures or decision-making process for awarding more than a billion dollars, the sources said… the process for awarding money and the identities of the recipient could be kept private, according to sources.”

How is this not the biggest scandal in the United States right now? How is a sitting president getting away with suing the government he is in charge of and then settling the case, effectively with himself, to gain access to more than a billion dollars of taxpayer cash, which could end up in the personal bank accounts of his cronies? Has there ever been a presidential scam quite like this?

“This is not just the most corrupt presidency in American history,” wrote conservative, ‘Never Trump’ commentator Mona Charen of The Bulwark last week, “it is among the most corrupt regimes on earth.”

  • War Powers:Anattemptto pass a House resolution to end the war failed after a tie. Three Republicans defied Donald Trump to vote yes, but one Democrat voted no, sinking the effort. It was Jared Golden of Maine – a resolutionco-sponsor.
  • Hormuz news:One ship was captured and taken towards Iran while another sank after being attacked, British and Indian observerssaid, amid rising tension over the Strait of Hormuz, the key waterway Iran has closed to most shipping.
  • Fright House:Trump needs “a visible consumer relief move now,” a White House sourcetoldReuters, describing increasingly panicked attempts to find a way to offset gas price rises caused by the war, as polling shows huge public anger.
  • Civilian deaths:The chief of Central Commandtoldsenators a U.S. bomb may have killed 175, mostly children, at an Iranian school but dismissed other reports of civilian deaths.
  • Lebanon latest:Continuing to ignore the supposed ceasefire, Israel hit southern Lebanon with air strikes and artillery,killingseveral. Lebanese authoritiessayat least 2,896 people have been killed by Israel, and more than 8,800 injured.
  • US-China talks:In Beijing, Xi JinpingwarnedDonald Trump of “clashes and even conflicts” over Taiwan, “putting the entire relationship in great jeopardy.”
  • Ethics, schmethics:Acting Attorney General Todd Blanche was told last year to recuse himself from cases involving Trump, who Blanche represented in criminal cases, CNNreported. Blanche, who now leads the legal onslaught against Trump’s enemies, signed a DOJ ethics pledge. No, really.
  • Kash splash:Visiting Hawaii, FBI Director Kash Patel enjoyed a “VIP snorkel” around the USS Arizona, APreported. The battleship sank at Pearl Harbor and is the gravesite of more than 900 sailors and marines, raising questions of propriety as well as scandal over Patel’s use of official resources for personal enjoyment.
  • Chief out:Border Patrol chief Mike Banks, whoreportedlysaid he had sex with prostitutes on personal overseas trips, abruptly resigned, telling Fox it was “time to enjoy the family and life.”
  • Abortion pill:The Supreme Court kept Mifepristone available by mail –for now.
  • New gerrymander:GOP South Carolina Governor Henry McMastercalleda special session to pursue partisan redistricting before the midterms.
  • Renewed resolve:Rashida Tlaibreintroducedher House resolution “Recognizing the Ongoing Nakba and Palestinian Refugees’ Rights.” With four new sponsors, the measure aims to promote understanding of Palestinian suffering under Israeli rule, and calls for renewed support for UN assistance for refugees.

On a 2018 visit to one of a number of Columbia properties rented to students by South Carolina Governor Henry McMaster, what did reporters find “caked inside a panel of the kitchen stove”?

Keep your eyes out for the answer below!

  • 🇵🇸 Don’t forget Gaza:Ships from a global aid flotillaset offfrom Turkey. Dozens of boats on a previous leg of the journey were illegally intercepted by Israel, which abducted more than 100 activists, humanitarians, and journalists, and took them to Crete. It also kidnapped two flotilla leaders and took them to an Israeli prison. They were later released.
  • 🇮🇱 🇵🇸 Genocidal chants:Far-right Israelis marking Israel’s 1967 capture and illegal occupation of East JerusalemattackedPalestinians and Jewish peace activists in the city, chanting genocidal slogans including “May your village burn.” Ultranationalist National Security Minister Itamar Ben-Gvir drew condemnation after storming the Al-Aqsa Mosque Compound andunfurlingan Israeli flag.

  • 🇬🇧

    Labouring on:UK Health Secretary Wes Streeting quit but did not trigger a party leadership race amid attempts to oust Prime Minister Keir Starmer. Manchester Mayor Andy Burnham, a favorite to replace Starmer,willtry to return to Parliament.
  • 🇨🇺 Out of oil:Cubasaidit had run out of oil thanks to the U.S. blockade, and also said CIA Director John Ratcliffe visited Havana on Thursday. CBS Newsreportedthat the U.S. is moving to indict former leader Raúl Castro, 94, over the downing of planes operated by a Cuban exile group 30 years ago.

“Fox host Jesse Watters says something mind-numbingly stupid and/or borderline racist” is less “dog bites man” than “water wet,” but still… the man has stamina and commitment to his utter awfulness, it has to be said. On Thursday, the father of four (by two women) gave his “pronatalist” take on Black birth rates and the Voting Rights Act. We apologize in advance for what you’re about to see and hear, above.

Here’s What I Told the DNC Autopsy

At The Bulwark, Rob Flaherty, deputy campaign manager for Kamala Harris in 2024, offers a long consideration of what went wrong, given that the official “autopsy” seems likely not to be released. Flaherty knows where the bodies are buried. Exhumed, they show evidence of total collapse. One conclusion: campaigns as we know them are dead, killed by clips and AI.

The Men Who Want Women to Be Quiet

At The Atlantic, Helen Lewis tours the far-right swamps where Pete Hegseth’s pastor and other angry men want women to lose the vote and to be confined to home and babies. “You can make good money telling men they’re the truly oppressed sex,” Lewis writes, before pegging one book, in fact written by a woman, as “eerily reminiscent of a friend recounting half a dozen Wikipedia pages that they read while drunk.”

How the American Oligarchy Went Hyperscale

Tim Murphy of Mother Jones offers a chilling study of the rush to build vast data centers called things like “Prometheus” or “Colossus,” to create artificial general intelligence (AGI). He does, however, predict setbacks. “When did you suspect it was a bubble?” Murphy asks. “Maybe it was when former Energy Secretary Rick Perry became the face of a $15 billion project in Amarillo named for Enrico Fermi. Maybe it was when [OpenAI’s Sam] Altman literally said it was.”

Zeteo’s Martin Pengelly contributed to this newsletter.

Still want to get Zeteo content, but not this newsletter? You can unsubscribe directly from ‘First Draft’ by going to ‘

If you enjoy Zeteo, share it with your friends and earn rewards when they subscribe.

item.content:encoded:
On this day in 1942, gasoline rationing went into effect in 17 eastern states, in an attempt to help the US war effort. By the end of the year, all 48 states were rationing gas. There’s no way such a drastic step will ever be needed again. Right?

Good morning! Mehdi here. Later today, I’ll be unpacking the ongoing AOC-vs-MTG controversy in a video monologue and making the case for the Democratic congresswoman and against the former Republican congresswoman. That’ll be in your inboxes in a few hours.

But for now, in today’s ‘First Draft’ newsletter, Trump’s corrupt behavior reaches corrupt new heights; Manchester Mayor Andy Burnham is trying to get back into Parliament to topple flailing UK Prime Minister Keir Starmer; and Israel continues to bomb Lebanon in violation of the supposed “ceasefire” as yet another War Powers Resolution vote fails in Congress.

“You can take all the corruption from every single US administration in history, add it all together, and it will not equal the amount of corruption committed by the Trump Regime in less than two years,” tweeted Ron Filipkowski, the conservative-turned-liberal commentator and ardent Trump critic. He was referring to a New York Times

But let’s be honest: Filipkowski could have been responding to any number of corruption scandals in this second Trump term. How about the president’s acceptance of a $400 million plane from Qatar in violation of the Emoluments Clause of the Constitution? Or Trump and his family’s acceptance of $187 million from the Abu Dhabi royals via their investment in the Trump family cryptocurrency venture, another clear violation of the Emoluments Clause? Or companies associated with Trump’s sons winning at least two lucrative government contracts?

The list goes on and on and on and on.

Nevertheless, I’d argue that this was the week we learned of the biggest Trump corruption scandal of all.

Here’s the background:

In 2019, an IRS contractor leaked Donald Trump’s confidential tax returns to ProPublica and the New York Times, which reported that Trump paid only $750 in taxes in both 2016 and 2017. The leaker was later found, prosecuted by the Biden administration, and sentenced to five years in prison.

But that wasn’t enough for Trump. In January of this year, he and his two adult sons, Don Jr. and Eric, sued the IRS and the Treasury Department for a whopping $10 billion, claiming the tax leak had caused them “reputational and financial harm” and “public embarrassment.”

Got that? The head of the executive branch of the United States decided to sue the executive branch of the United States. And it wasn’t his first such lawsuit. Last year, Trump sued the Department of Justice for $230 million for searching his home in Mar-a-Lago (for the classified documents he stole!) and for daring to investigate his well-documented ties to Russia. In a rare Trump understatement, he conceded that his lawsuit “sort of looks bad.”

“It’s interesting because I’m the one who makes a decision, right, and, you know, that decision would have to go across my desk,” the president said in the Oval Office in October. “It’s awfully strange to make a decision where I’m paying myself.”

Yes, yes, it is! But it gets worse. Last month, a federal judge threatened to throw out Trump’s lawsuit because, she noted, Trump and the defendants – the Treasury Department and IRS – might not be “sufficiently adverse” to justify the case proceeding. Judge Kathleen Williams gave both sides a deadline of May 20 to show how they are in actual conflict with one another, given that both government entities answer to… the president.

So what did the White House and the DOJ do? They began, according to the New York Times,

And guess what that potential settlement looks like? ABC News had the scoop yesterday:

“President Donald Trump is expected to drop his $10 billion lawsuit against the Internal Revenue Service in exchange for the creation of a $1.7 billion fund to compensate allies who claim they were wrongfully targeted by the Biden administration, sources familiar with the matter told ABC News.

The commission overseeing the compensation fund would have the total authority to hand out approximately $1.7 billion in taxpayer funds to settle claims brought by anyone who alleges they were harmed by the Biden administration’s “weaponization” of the legal system, including the nearly 1,600 individuals charged in connection with the Jan. 6 Capitol attack as well as potentially entities associated with President Trump himself.”

Donald Trump is planning on using our tax dollars to (re)pay his friends – and his thugs. It’s a corrupt slush fund for MAGA. And I know some of you must be thinking, ‘But surely there must be some oversight of this money, some safeguards and protections for the American taxpayer?’

Nope. Nada. Zilch. From ABC News, again:

“The arrangement would be an unprecedented use of taxpayer dollars with little oversight. Under the terms of the potential settlement agreement, President Trump would have the authority to remove members of the commission running the fund without cause, and the commission would be under no obligation to disclose its procedures or decision-making process for awarding more than a billion dollars, the sources said… the process for awarding money and the identities of the recipient could be kept private, according to sources.”

How is this not the biggest scandal in the United States right now? How is a sitting president getting away with suing the government he is in charge of and then settling the case, effectively with himself, to gain access to more than a billion dollars of taxpayer cash, which could end up in the personal bank accounts of his cronies? Has there ever been a presidential scam quite like this?

“This is not just the most corrupt presidency in American history,” wrote conservative, ‘Never Trump’ commentator Mona Charen of The Bulwark last week, “it is among the most corrupt regimes on earth.”

  • War Powers:Anattemptto pass a House resolution to end the war failed after a tie. Three Republicans defied Donald Trump to vote yes, but one Democrat voted no, sinking the effort. It was Jared Golden of Maine – a resolutionco-sponsor.
  • Hormuz news:One ship was captured and taken towards Iran while another sank after being attacked, British and Indian observerssaid, amid rising tension over the Strait of Hormuz, the key waterway Iran has closed to most shipping.
  • Fright House:Trump needs “a visible consumer relief move now,” a White House sourcetoldReuters, describing increasingly panicked attempts to find a way to offset gas price rises caused by the war, as polling shows huge public anger.
  • Civilian deaths:The chief of Central Commandtoldsenators a U.S. bomb may have killed 175, mostly children, at an Iranian school but dismissed other reports of civilian deaths.
  • Lebanon latest:Continuing to ignore the supposed ceasefire, Israel hit southern Lebanon with air strikes and artillery,killingseveral. Lebanese authoritiessayat least 2,896 people have been killed by Israel, and more than 8,800 injured.
  • US-China talks:In Beijing, Xi JinpingwarnedDonald Trump of “clashes and even conflicts” over Taiwan, “putting the entire relationship in great jeopardy.”
  • Ethics, schmethics:Acting Attorney General Todd Blanche was told last year to recuse himself from cases involving Trump, who Blanche represented in criminal cases, CNNreported. Blanche, who now leads the legal onslaught against Trump’s enemies, signed a DOJ ethics pledge. No, really.
  • Kash splash:Visiting Hawaii, FBI Director Kash Patel enjoyed a “VIP snorkel” around the USS Arizona, APreported. The battleship sank at Pearl Harbor and is the gravesite of more than 900 sailors and marines, raising questions of propriety as well as scandal over Patel’s use of official resources for personal enjoyment.
  • Chief out:Border Patrol chief Mike Banks, whoreportedlysaid he had sex with prostitutes on personal overseas trips, abruptly resigned, telling Fox it was “time to enjoy the family and life.”
  • Abortion pill:The Supreme Court kept Mifepristone available by mail –for now.
  • New gerrymander:GOP South Carolina Governor Henry McMastercalleda special session to pursue partisan redistricting before the midterms.
  • Renewed resolve:Rashida Tlaibreintroducedher House resolution “Recognizing the Ongoing Nakba and Palestinian Refugees’ Rights.” With four new sponsors, the measure aims to promote understanding of Palestinian suffering under Israeli rule, and calls for renewed support for UN assistance for refugees.

On a 2018 visit to one of a number of Columbia properties rented to students by South Carolina Governor Henry McMaster, what did reporters find “caked inside a panel of the kitchen stove”?

Keep your eyes out for the answer below!

  • 🇵🇸 Don’t forget Gaza:Ships from a global aid flotillaset offfrom Turkey. Dozens of boats on a previous leg of the journey were illegally intercepted by Israel, which abducted more than 100 activists, humanitarians, and journalists, and took them to Crete. It also kidnapped two flotilla leaders and took them to an Israeli prison. They were later released.
  • 🇮🇱 🇵🇸 Genocidal chants:Far-right Israelis marking Israel’s 1967 capture and illegal occupation of East JerusalemattackedPalestinians and Jewish peace activists in the city, chanting genocidal slogans including “May your village burn.” Ultranationalist National Security Minister Itamar Ben-Gvir drew condemnation after storming the Al-Aqsa Mosque Compound andunfurlingan Israeli flag.

  • 🇬🇧

    Labouring on:UK Health Secretary Wes Streeting quit but did not trigger a party leadership race amid attempts to oust Prime Minister Keir Starmer. Manchester Mayor Andy Burnham, a favorite to replace Starmer,willtry to return to Parliament.
  • 🇨🇺 Out of oil:Cubasaidit had run out of oil thanks to the U.S. blockade, and also said CIA Director John Ratcliffe visited Havana on Thursday. CBS Newsreportedthat the U.S. is moving to indict former leader Raúl Castro, 94, over the downing of planes operated by a Cuban exile group 30 years ago.

“Fox host Jesse Watters says something mind-numbingly stupid and/or borderline racist” is less “dog bites man” than “water wet,” but still… the man has stamina and commitment to his utter awfulness, it has to be said. On Thursday, the father of four (by two women) gave his “pronatalist” take on Black birth rates and the Voting Rights Act. We apologize in advance for what you’re about to see and hear, above.

Here’s What I Told the DNC Autopsy

At The Bulwark, Rob Flaherty, deputy campaign manager for Kamala Harris in 2024, offers a long consideration of what went wrong, given that the official “autopsy” seems likely not to be released. Flaherty knows where the bodies are buried. Exhumed, they show evidence of total collapse. One conclusion: campaigns as we know them are dead, killed by clips and AI.

The Men Who Want Women to Be Quiet

At The Atlantic, Helen Lewis tours the far-right swamps where Pete Hegseth’s pastor and other angry men want women to lose the vote and to be confined to home and babies. “You can make good money telling men they’re the truly oppressed sex,” Lewis writes, before pegging one book, in fact written by a woman, as “eerily reminiscent of a friend recounting half a dozen Wikipedia pages that they read while drunk.”

How the American Oligarchy Went Hyperscale

Tim Murphy of Mother Jones offers a chilling study of the rush to build vast data centers called things like “Prometheus” or “Colossus,” to create artificial general intelligence (AGI). He does, however, predict setbacks. “When did you suspect it was a bubble?” Murphy asks. “Maybe it was when former Energy Secretary Rick Perry became the face of a $15 billion project in Amarillo named for Enrico Fermi. Maybe it was when [OpenAI’s Sam] Altman literally said it was.”

Zeteo’s Martin Pengelly contributed to this newsletter.

item.title: Pro-Palestine Artist Misan Harriman Is Not Antisemitic. It’s Dangerous to Imply He Is
item.description: The British Jewish community currently feels under siege (make no mistake, the feeling is widespread), which should make it critical for the causes to be correctly diagnosed and antidotes correctly prescribed. Getting those things wrong, let alone spectacularly wrong, can be highly consequential –…
item.author: Zeteo
item.content:encoded:
The British Jewish community currently feels under siege (make no mistake, the feeling is widespread), which should make it critical for the causes to be correctly diagnosed and antidotes correctly prescribed. Getting those things wrong, let alone spectacularly wrong, can be highly consequential – the problem will be exacerbated.

At times of heightened tensions, leaders and public figures tend to reach for oversimplifications, running to extremes and eschewing the kind of serious and deep engagement most likely to generate policies that make things better rather than worse. More egregious still is for an issue like antisemitism to be instrumentalized in ways that serve agendas unconnected or even antithetical to Jewish communal well-being and social inclusion. All of those things are currently on display in the debate in the UK.

And on this occasion, the instrumentalization is so glaring that it has become a teachable moment.

The campaign to vilify Misan Harriman, a photographer, Oscar-nominated filmmaker, and chair of London’s Southbank Centre is the case in point.

Harriman is unapologetic in his assertion that all art is political, and his reach extends to a mass social media following.

He is currently the target of an attempted hit job, centering around accusations of cheapening the Holocaust and of espousing antisemitic conspiracy theories – except he has done nothing of the kind. The art correspondent of the British Telegraph newspaper has penned two attacks on Harriman in the two weeks, with the story amplified across a predictable domestic media ecosystem, including GB News, the Daily Mail, the

In a thoughtful video reflecting on last week’s local council election results, Harriman quoted Jewish American writer Susan Sontag and her study of how the conditions were created that ultimately led to the Holocaust, by exploring the social, political, and media climate that can give rise to extremism. Harriman explicitly dismissed the idea that Reform voters were devils, suggesting instead that they were being exposed to certain ideas espoused by charlatans that were being insufficiently challenged.

A decontextualized and shortened clip of his comments was posted by a pro-Israel activist (wrongly claiming Harriman had compared the surge in support for Reform UK to the Holocaust), setting off a snowball effect. UK Reform Party Shadow Chancellor Robert Jenrick insisted on social media that Harriman “should be nowhere near a taxpayer-funded organisation,” while Labour MP David Taylor suggested the Southbank Centre should “consider removing” Harriman from its board. Disturbingly, the chief executive of the Holocaust Educational Trust, Karen Pollock, joined the pile-on in reposting the edited clip and describing it as “shocking.” If Pollock did that carelessly and in haste but in good faith, then an apology should be issued without further delay.

Harriman was accused by the Telegraph of other crimes, notably the spreading of a “conspiracy theory” regarding the April 29 stabbings in Golders Green by pointing out that police and media ignored or underplayed that the first victim was a Muslim, and his sharing of posts claiming Israel was trying to seize land as part of a Greater Israel project. The problem? Both happen to be true. From personal experience, having conducted two interviews unaware of that first victim, it is incontrovertibly the case that the stabbing of Ishmail Hussein in Southwark earlier on that day did not fit the narrative and was conspicuously neglected.

That matters in the broader scheme of things because it trivializes the notion of “conspiracy theories” (which can be harmful) and it helps downplay the neglect of mental healthcare policy in the UK (the attacker was known to mental health services).

Facts about Israel are also apparently irrelevant – including that the pursuit of Greater Israel is part of a formal coalition agreement of its current government, is proudly boasted of by government ministers, and is acknowledged as a reality by just about everyone. Israel’s extremism and relentless violations of international law are real, and attempts to deflect from this reality in the guise of antisemitism are embarrassing, counter-productive, and ultimately futile.

Sprinkling such blatant falsehoods and unseriousness into the debate on antisemitism carries consequences, including that people begin to throw their hands up and declare that they are sick of this nonsense. The real challenge of confronting antisemitism becomes cheapened in the eyes of the public when it is wrapped up in transparently disingenuous and ad hominem campaigns, often serving a wholly different agenda. This enhances the danger that antisemitism will be taken less seriously and increasingly dismissed by the public – with the net effect of making Jewish communities less safe.

In the attacks on Misan Harriman, it’s not even subtle; there is barely an attempt to disguise the culture war underpinning. In the same articles, the Telegraph was going after public funding of an arts institution and even managed to weave in Harriman’s “well-documented friendship with the Duke and Duchess of Essex.” Perhaps that’s his real crime?

An indication of how counterproductive such things can be is that the Independent Press Standards Organisation (IPSO), Britain’s press watchdog, has received over 85,000 complaints (so far) regarding the articles impugning Harriman – the most complaints received about press coverage in UK history. In parallel, hundreds of leading cultural figures, including Gary Lineker and Greta Thunberg, have signed a letter supporting Harriman and decrying the attacks as a “dishonest smear campaign.”

The choice of target and the underlying assumptions behind this campaign should be frustrating to anyone genuinely motivated by creating conditions for less antisemitism and racism in general.

Harriman, as one would expect, was unequivocal after the Golders Green stabbings in condemning antisemitism and sharing solidarity with the Jewish community.

Post-October 7, 2023, Harriman shared an image of 9-month-old Israeli Kfir Bibas, who was kidnapped along with his family, with the #ReleaseTheHostagesNow. Bibas, along with his mother and brother, was found to have been killed while held captive in Gaza.

It is the granularity and depth of Harriman’s engagement on the issue that make him such an interesting messenger and perhaps such a threat to those for whom the goal is a Manichean weaponization seeking to equate pro-Palestine speech with antisemitism. Ending occupation and apartheid should be a unifying struggle; it’s a goal that can be challenged by those who disagree, but it surely is not something to be silenced and criminalized.

Harriman uses his social media influence to extensively document Jewish solidarity with the Palestinian predicament, including at the London rallies. It is something that led Mark Godfrey, former curator at Tate Modern, to comment on Harriman’s Hope 93 Gallery exhibition as showing “his profound respect for Jews.” Harriman leans into examples of Israeli-Palestinian cooperation to deliver peace, including by publicizing his attendance at a rally of the Standing Together movement.

Perhaps there is a silver lining to this episode. Perhaps the absurdity of the targeting of Misan Harriman is an opportunity to insert some much-needed sanity, nuance, and clarity into debates currently raging around antisemitism in the UK and elsewhere.

Serious work needs to be done in reclaiming the anti-antisemitism agenda from the hands of extremists.

That Israeli statecraft sees benefit in positioning the state as the Jew on the international stage, thereby conflating Jewishness with Israel’s actions, does not make it true. Nor does the enthusiasm with which some diaspora Jewish organizations and individuals embrace this conflation. In reality, these are reckless, irresponsible positions, fueling and encouraging the very antisemitism they claim to oppose.

A similar critique should be applied to the de facto normalizing and mainstreaming of what is, in reality, a far-right approach to the entire issue. If combating antisemitism is embedded in a Judeo-Christian civilizational struggle against the threat of Muslims and their immigration to the UK (conveniently ignoring that centuries of antisemitism are premised on the Christian “othering” of Jews), then that invites one particular set of solutions. Likewise, the attempts to situate the problem as predominantly residing on the left of the political spectrum, as well as in universities and cultural institutions.

As Barnaby Raine notes in a piercing interview with Equator: “Proclamations of concern for Jews have become the primary vehicle for mobilizing a racist panic about Muslims…allowing the far-right to reposition themselves as the only true defenders of Jews, because they are the ones most willing to target Muslims.” That’s how one ends up with the far-right Reform deputy leader, Richard Tice, being a celebrated speaker at a Jewish “Standing Strong” rally against antisemitism in London on May 10, where he called for an end to student loans, grants, and research funding for universities. It also leads to calls for a Palestinian Nakba commemoration rally to be banned, while a march on the same day led by anti-immigrant Islamophobe Tommy Robinson goes unquestioned.

Antisemitism will not be overcome by encouraging a less tolerant, less inclusive, and more racist society in which the right to speech and protest is further squeezed, any more than it will be overcome by a preposterous ad hominem attack, spuriously attempting to cast an anti-racist British cultural icon as an enemy of the Jews.

Daniel Levy is a Zeteo contributor, political commentator, and the president of the U.S./Middle East Project. He has served as an Israeli negotiator in peace talks and is a former adviser in the Israeli Prime Minister‘s Office.

Check out more from Zeteo:

For any issues with your subscription, please email us at info@zeteonews.com.

item.title: ‘Trump’s War Fever Is Back’ – and His Iran War Could Escalate Again Very Soon
item.description: The Iran war ceasefire, which was never actually a ceasefire , appears to be rapidly coming to an end. Two senior Trump administration officials and two other sources briefed on the situation tell Zeteo that Donald Trump has grown increasingly frustrated by the state of peace talks with Iran, and…
item.author: "Zeteo | U.S. Politics"
item.content:encoded:
The Iran war ceasefire, which was never actually a ceasefire, appears to be rapidly coming to an end.

Two senior Trump administration officials and two other sources briefed on the situation tell Zeteo that Donald Trump has grown increasingly frustrated by the state of peace talks with Iran, and the president has ordered an acceleration of the Pentagon’s preparation for a new phase of his war in the Middle East.

U.S. officials who spoke to Zeteo say the Trump administration is ready to ramp up the war again within the coming days, after the U.S. president returns from his state visit in China, should Trump give the order to strike.

“Trump’s war fever is back,” one of the senior administration officials said this week.

Sources add that military options Trump has voiced a preference for include a potential massive new bombing campaign against the Iranians. The president is still keenly interested in sending in ground troops and special forces, including to find and remove highly enriched uranium stockpiles, but some senior Trump appointees expect him to hold off on that kind of high-risk operation, at least for the moment.

As with all things related to the decision-making of the notably mercurial and impetuous President Trump, things could conceivably change in negotiations, and his decision to re-escalate the war in Iran is final when he says it is. However, knowledgeable sources who have previously and correctly warned Zeteo of Trump’s policies and actions to come were in near-uniform agreement: Get ready for more war – and soon.

We must say: We hope we’re wrong. Though we can confirm these moves have been made at the highest levels of the Trump-Vance administration, we truly wish for the sake of our nation and others that Trump does not escalate his war again, as senior Trump administration officials expect him to in the next several days.

Spokespeople for the White House and Pentagon did not immediately respond to a request for comment.

The official who told us that the president’s war fever has returned, like other sources, was emphatic that they do not want this conflict to continue, given how much of a political disaster it’s become for Trump, and how badly it has jacked up prices for American voters during a midterm election year.

Trump, for his part, has made it very clear he does not care that Americans are being crushed by exorbitant gas prices as a result of his war – and that our growing collective suffering won’t lead him to make a deal with Iran.

“I don’t think about Americans’ financial situation,” he said earlier this week, after noting many times that he always expected oil prices to spike due to the war – and that he expected them to be even higher than they are now.

Despite a temporary ceasefire, Trump’s war fever never actually went away. Ever since the U.S.-Israel-Iran pause was announced early last month, the fighting has continued. Trump has responded to Iran’s successful effort to close the Strait of Hormuz, which has bottled up the flow of oil and sent energy prices soaring, by imposing his own naval blockade.

Somehow, the Trump administration – in true Orwellian fashion – has kept publicly insisting that the ceasefire still remains in effect, even as the U.S. president has continued threatening Iran with the prospect of apocalyptic war crimes, if the regime didn’t give him a deal could try to sell as a victory to the American people.

Furthermore, it is not just senior U.S. officials who are preparing for an imminent resumption of Trump’s major bombing operations; top Iranian officials are doing the same. Drop Site News reported earlier this month that government officials in Tehran are already prepping for a “new round of U.S.-Israeli bombings and assassinations.”

As one senior Iranian official told the news site: “Based on current assessments, another military attack seems likely. [Trump’s] goals from the naval blockade haven’t been achieved. He can’t keep the blockade going for much longer. We think the U.S. will focus on Hormuz, so military attacks and operations will likely expand along Iran’s coastline.”

On Tuesday, NBC News reported that the Department of Defense has actively considered renaming Trump’s war, changing the official title from “Operation Epic Fury” to “Operation Sledgehammer.” As NBC notes, the high-level discussion of a rebrand is significant because it highlights “how seriously the administration is considering resuming the war started on Feb. 28, and could allow Trump to argue that it restarts the 60-day clock that requires congressional authorization for war.”

Early this week, before he left for Beijing, President Trump hardly sounded bullish on peace talks, telling reporters that the ceasefire was on “life support,” following his rejection of Tehran’s latest counter-proposal for bringing this war to a close.

By Wednesday, Vice President JD Vance was claiming to reporters at the White House: “I think that we are making progress.” Vance said that “the fundamental question ​is do we ​make enough progress that we satisfy ‌the president’s red line?” Trump’s veep added that “the red line ​is ​very ⁠simple: He needs to feel confident that ​we put a ​number ⁠of protections in place such that Iran will never ⁠have ​a nuclear ​weapon.”

Trump’s objectives in this war have shifted repeatedly since the president and Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu launched it, with this administration plunging into a regional bloodbath with no clear mission in the first place.

“We will be leaving very soon,” Trump told journalists in the White House’s Oval Office. That was in late March.

As of mid-May, it appears the momentum within Trump’s government has shifted toward staying.
